posts.name: Stan Stevenson
posts.cooked:
The world of fine wine, as a matter of fact, is full of myths or mistaken notions, often passing for gospel. Take the idea of letting a wine “breathe” – that is to say, opening a bottle or decanting it into another container long before drinking it, ostensibly to allow it to “improve” with the exposure to oxygen. There is indeed many a wine expert (especially wine boors) who would never dream of enjoying a good bottle of red wine unless it has been given a chance to “breathe” for at least an hour.

In a December 1997 issue of Decanter – a highly respected wine periodical published in the U.K., which also happens to bill itself as “The World’s Best Wine Magazine” (and many wine experts would not disagree) – there was a report on a blind tasting involving six of the world’s most discriminating wine judges. These judges included Hugh Johnson (the author of more best selling wine books than anyone), Steven Spurrier (the original organizer of the infamous “Paris Tasting” of 1976), Serena Sutcliffe (a Master of Wine), and Patrick Léon (a famous winemaker for several Bordeaux châteaux, such as Mouton-Rothschild).

In this tasting, the esteemed judges were asked to assess the quality of several red wines from France’s classic Bordeaux region (including 1961 Mouton-Rothschild, a 1982 Clerc-Milon, a 1980 d’Armailhac, and a 1990 Mouton-Cadet) that were

Uncorked a few minutes ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked a few hours ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few minutes ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few hours ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked, and then immediately poured into glasses and tasted (that is, no “breathing” at all).

For which wines, across the board, did these wine judges ultimately show the most preference? You guessed it: the wines that were uncorked, immediately poured and tasted. It turns out that “breathing” – whether for a few minutes, or a few hours – doesn’t really “improve” wine at all. At least not according to this stellar panel.

Lodi Winegrape Commission

Does price (and letting wines “breathe”) matter?

    Does price affect the “quality” of wine?  Yes, indeed it do. At least according to results of a study that was released back in 2008, conducted by Antonio Rangel, Associate Professor of Economics at the California Institute of...

posts.name: Berry Crawford
posts.cooked: Young wines need to breath. Acidic ones in particular. People can believe it or not, but its so self evident I wouldn’t bother to debate anyone on it. With older wines it really depends on the wine and usually a full decant will detract from the wine. A slow ox method is much better.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Decanter did another trial of this kind within the past year or so. It was pretty interesting, but not particularly conclusive. I think the general conclusion was that decanting doesn’t hurt, but may not help.

Having said that, I tend to subscribe to the thoughts on this written by Prof. Waterhouse of UC Davis, which can be found in this article:

http://www.curiouscook.com/site/2009/01/for-a-tastier-wine-the-next-trick-involves-.html

Mr. Waterhouse thought the elimination of sulfur aromas is all that these accessories — or, for that matter, aeration — had to offer.

“A number of sulfur compounds are present in wine in traces and have an impact on flavor because they’re very potent,” he said. “Some are unpleasant and some contribute to a wine’s complexity. You can certainly dispose of these in five minutes with a little oxygen and a small area of metal catalyst to speed the reactions up, and change your impression of the wine.”

But Mr. Waterhouse maintained that no brief treatment could convert the tannins to less astringent, softer forms, not even an hour in a decanter.

“You can saturate a wine with oxygen by sloshing it into a decanter, but then the oxygen just sits there,” he said. “It reacts very slowly. To change the tannins perceptibly in an hour, you would have to hit the wine with pure oxygen, high pressure and temperature, and powdered iron with a huge catalytic surface area.”

So why do people think decanting softens a wine’s astringency?

“I think that this impression of softening comes from the loss of the unpleasant sulfur compounds, which reduces our overall perception of harshness,” Mr. Waterhouse said.

For me, there is zero question that aerating a wine (particularly a young one) is beneficial.

link_counts.title: Opening wine to let it "breathe"

posts.name: Marco Maiocco
posts.cooked: That’s interesting. I’ve recently read in the “Gout du vin” of E. Peynaud a similar position: he said that making wine breath is useless. On the other hand this seems contrary to my everyday experience, especially of nebbiolo-based wine drinkers. What I suspect is that it depends from wine to wine, and probably a burgundy or a bordeaux (of which I have very little experience) needs much less oxygen than a barolo…

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Well, there is ‘science’, and there is ‘perception’, and there is ‘experience’ - and oftentimes, the three are at odds.

It’s impossible to say whether breathing simply ‘blows off’ the unpleasant sulfur compounds or if there is more involved. All I know is that when I let a wine breath in a glass, it ‘changes’ - both aromatically and my perception of its flavors and its structure, especially with younger wines.

But heck, what do I know?!?!?

Cheers

posts.name: mike pobega
posts.cooked: Well, in the grand scheme of things, they are probably correct to a point as most of the world wines are made accessible and in a forward style so they are probably not speaking of many of the wines we collectively enjoy.

posts.name: Ed Kurtzman
posts.cooked:
Breathing definitely helps premoxed whites. An hour or so in a decanter can “freshen up” a premoxed white, maybe not to where it should be, but can make an undrinkable wine at least somewhat enjoyable.

Now that the breathing myth is busted, I wonder if anyone has ever done a study on the myth of the wet cork. That’s the one where people look at me like I’m from outer space when I bring it up, but I don’t believe bottles need to be on their sides or upside down while aging. In the tens of thousands of bottles I’ve opened, I’ve yet to see a dry cork that was so dry it came out of the bottle easily. Just the opposite. You could push a completely soaked cork into the bottle with your pinkie, but a dry cork keeps wine in the bottle and air out better than a wet cork.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: We used to blow bubbles in our wine glasses with straws to open up/oxygenate the wine! It works beautifully, but you kind of lose points on sophistication!

I love Decanter magazine, but I would say this:

It is not a “myth”, it just isn’t true for every bottle, of every variety, of every age, of every situation. It also isn’t untrue for all of these…

Consider old nebbiolo, for example

As for bottles standing up, I put wines in my locker according to how I can get the boxes to fit most efficiently. Some end up on their sides, some standing. If I could only choose one or the other, I’d probably choose standing for long term storage. I’m with you, I’ve had too many soaked, deteriorating corks, but none from standing bottles that had a problem.

There was a Somm who worked at Niebaum Coppola Winery who developed a ‘wine straw’ to be used by wine professionals to assess wines. I still have a few of his first batch - it never caught on.

What I can say is that it actually does a terrific job of isolating aromas - and is a great way to discern faults in wines. I’ll have to remember to bring one or two to the next wine gathering I’m part of.

posts.name: Jay T.
posts.cooked:
I don’t really know whether there is a point in arguing with the results – which have been discussed here in the past on at least a few occasions. But it’s hard to square with my own experience and that of just about everyone I know. I almost always prefer young wines after decanting. I drink a lot of older Nebbiolo, and it is almost universally the case that I prefer the wines several hours after decanting. In fact, they are often completely uninteresting immediately after opening, thin, and dominated by acid. While Bordeaux acts a bit differently – it’s often much more accessible than Nebbiolo upon opening – I’ve enjoyed enough bottles over several hours where the last glass was the best to make we wonder what the hell happened in the Decanter tasting.

Also, someone’s qualifications as an “esteemed judge” don’t seem at all relevant on this question. That said, maybe the wines in the 1997 Decanter tasting really did taste better immediately after opening. Interesting, if true. But it’s hard to see what the useful takeaway is, other than that wine is highly variable, and unfortunately it’s impossible to create universal rules that maximize enjoyment.

posts.name: Nolan E
posts.cooked:
I only decant for sediment anymore.

If you decant a wine and ignore it for 2 hours you miss a lot of great information.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: I’m curious about the claim of the inability of oxygen to meaningfully interact with tannins in short periods of time/typical circumstances. One of my most common experiences with young wine is that I find them powerful, fruity, and drinkable right after opening, but after (an hour, two, high degree of variation) the tannins become much more noticeable and the wine much less enjoyable. What causes this?

posts.name: Nick Ryan
posts.cooked: Article is the purest bullcrap for young wines. Every time I go to a new-release Burgundy/Bordeaux tasting I force myself to let a glass evolve for 20 minutes because the first impression is frequently of a closed and unforgiving wine, and the last impression much different. Cali wines are more forgiving but also usually need decanting.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
I think you get more from most wines…by letting them aerate for a long time. However, if you don’t have time…for a longer aeration, pop and pour (after getting rid of sediment) is preferable. A two hour decant, for most red wines is likely to cause the wine to close up; often it takes less time to close up. So…if I don’t have time for a long one, I don’t risk the short one…but not for the “information” I “miss”…just because if I drink it then, it is likely to show its worst.

For some whites, particularly, imo, chablis and condrieu (and to a much lesser degree with Alsace riesling; I know no other riesling well enough), and Cote de Beaune whites…a long aeration is necessary to bring out the most charming, informative stuff…often the next day when it shows its best.

For me, in sum, it’s pop and pour or long aeration…a two hour one or less is likey to make the wine show less. I’d rather just nurse it for those two hours…and glean that limited information…rather than tsate tannins and acidity until the wine really opens up.

I opened a 1995 Daniel Rion Clos Vougeot Saturday. I aerated it for several hours because it was tight and closed at first. It was OK Saturday night. Last night, two days later, it was the best RB I’d had in 2016…it all came together so beautifully, sitting in decanter for two days. To me, the “information” is that the wine is a beauty and will last forever (as will most RB from “good” vintages). Had I deduced the information in the first two hours after cleaning the wine and decanting it…I’d have thought it was maybe ok, but nothing special…and did think that.

I doubt most people ever taste wine aerated as long as I did last night…but…the evidence can be compelling when it turns a good wine into a spectactular wine. And, it doesn’t work all the time, of course…but…more times than not the long aeration reveals a wine that never reveals itself without that long aeration, though it is almost always pleasant within 15mins-1/2 hour of opening.

Mythology 102

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
i think the main difference is that with a younger wine, you don’t have to worry or monitor. With an older wine you have to think about it more and monitor it…and develop your own comfort level with the aeration time.

What Tom is describing with the '55 happens to me all the time (just with older wines, not ancient wines)…and I have a comfort level letting them aerate. But, I do monitor them and look at really extended aeration as more of a science experiment that never ceases to surprise me.

Never thought much about the wet cork issue, though have pushed many wet corks through…and had many dry one stick to the sides and create dry cork rafts in the wine from trying to get them out.

posts.name: Chris. Mc Cart
posts.cooked:
What a strange article.
I disagree with the panel results. Like many here, indiv wines require indiv responses at different stages.

I’ve had lots of bottles that showed best straight after being popped & poured, and many that required 48-72 hours to show their best (aged Sauternes, Savennieres). Most are somewhere in the 20 minutes to 4 hour range. I decant to “breathe” more now than I ever have.
I generally don’t decant wines that will go into large bowl glasses (like red burgs).
I do like the slow-ox method for older (35yrs+) bottles, but not all the time.

So, to summarize - Decanting = yes or no, but only some of the time.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:
Jay Miller:

The sad thing is that there are people who will believe this.

I’ve been drinking high end wines for 40 years, been to tastings around the world and have hosted tastings as well. I believe it. 5 minutes in the glass(if that) is all the ‘breathing’ any wine needs. The rest is the brain playing tricks/psychological agendas. Read the studies.

Why should reading the studies, no matter what they purport to “prove,” change the way you approach drinking wine? It’s not about getting it right, in some sort of objective sense on which you are being graded. It’s about following an approach that gives you the most pleasure and enhances your experience of the wines. If wines taste better to me after a long/short/no decant, then why should I drink them a different way just because a study shows my impressions are all in my head?

If you have never experimented with decant times for different wines, then by all means read the studies and the blogs and the posts here. It’s one of the fun things about this hobby - the experimenting and learning. Try for yourself and see what works for you, as there is clearly no consensus here. Given that there is no down or upside to any approach - no health hazards, no added costs, no risk unless you’re not paying attention to the wine - the one and only thing that should determine how you treat your wine is how you and those you are drinking with like it best. Who cares if it’s in the brain or not? It is most certainly in the mouth, when all is said and done.

posts.name: George Chadwick
posts.cooked: In my experience opening a bottle and letting it breathe and then slowly drinking it until it’s empty and then going to the cellar to get another bottle always improves the flavor of the second bottle.

Often the first few glasses are poured within minutes. Whites are different, or at least the sweeter whites are - they seem to need a couple hours to a couple days to reach their potential.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
To me, just another example of the fact that science cannot explain everything about wine. And personally, I love this!

In almost every case, younger wine that I have opened certainly do change over time, but not necessarily in a predictable manner. Can we truly explain what is happening scientifically?

To me, it’s impossible. Each and every bottle is unique wine and will act independently of every other single bottle. Everyone that is opened will experience factors unique to that single bottle.

We know, for instance, that most aromatic compounds are combinations of individual compounds that are bound together in amounts that are in parts per billion. We know that when wine experiences oxygen, either via passive oxidation or via swirling, some of these bonds are broken and new ones are created. But it would be very difficult to duplicate this swirling again and again.

On top of that, we know that everyone has a unique set of tolerances for picking things up both aromatically and flavor wise in wines. Therefore, the matrix of things that make wine wine, make each bottle truly unique.

Cheers.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

The world of fine wine, as a matter of fact, is full of myths or mistaken notions, often passing for gospel. Take the idea of letting a wine “breathe” – that is to say, opening a bottle or decanting it into another container long before drinking it, ostensibly to allow it to “improve” with the exposure to oxygen. There is indeed many a wine expert (especially wine boors) who would never dream of enjoying a good bottle of red wine unless it has been given a chance to “breathe” for at least an hour.

In a December 1997 issue of Decanter – a highly respected wine periodical published in the U.K., which also happens to bill itself as “The World’s Best Wine Magazine” (and many wine experts would not disagree) – there was a report on a blind tasting involving six of the world’s most discriminating wine judges. These judges included Hugh Johnson (the author of more best selling wine books than anyone), Steven Spurrier (the original organizer of the infamous “Paris Tasting” of 1976), Serena Sutcliffe (a Master of Wine), and Patrick Léon (a famous winemaker for several Bordeaux châteaux, such as Mouton-Rothschild).

the wines that were uncorked, immediately poured and tasted. It turns out that “breathing” – whether for a few minutes, or a few hours – doesn’t really “improve” wine at all. At least not according to this stellar panel.

I can tell you I do not agree 100% with their conclusion. While the majority of wines produced will not change with air, that is not relevant as the majority of wines are meant to be drunk within the first 6 months of bottling.

Based on personal experience opening and tasting thousands of bottles, as well as having performed decanting experiments in blind tastings, decanting helps many fine wines show better. An easier way to look at this is, if a wine can improve in the glass, decanting will also allow a wine to show better.

In this tasting, the esteemed judges were asked to assess the quality of several red wines from France’s classic Bordeaux region (including 1961 Mouton-Rothschild, a 1982 Clerc-Milon, a 1980 d’Armailhac, and a 1990 Mouton-Cadet) that were

Uncorked a few minutes ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked a few hours ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few minutes ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few hours ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked, and then immediately poured into glasses and tasted (that is, no “breathing” at all).

For which wines, across the board, did these wine judges ultimately show the most preference? You guessed it:

Except for the 61 Mouton Rothschild, the other wines are absolute crap. They are not relevant. Who cares?

FWIW, if decanting does not allow wines to show better, (Because you used Bordeaux as an example) when time permits, why does just about every chateau in Bordeaux decant their wines before serving at the property?

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
Paul Flynn:

Neil deGrasse Tyson did a bit on this in one of his Star Talk Radio shows. He said it’s not about letting oxygen in, it’s about letting other gases out. He suggested that using a blender was the most efficient method.

My sister-in-law did that! I brought over a Carlisle Old Vine Zin RRV 2013, and she poured about 2 glasses worth into a blender. She ran the blender for about five seconds and funnelled the wine back into the bottle.

It did appear to help “open up” the wine.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:
Wes Barton:

You’ve never had a wine that tasted better the next day?

What’s leftover wine?

That’s a fundamental question to this discussion. If you aren’t willing to answer, you have no credibility.

Wes Barton:

You’ve never had a wine that tasted better the next day?

Well, Wes…yes…I’ve absolutely have had wines that tasted better the next day after standing open on the sideboard. I think we all have.
I’ve also had old wines that are absolutely shot the next day…ones that have turned an ugly/murky brown and have become
very astringent & dried out. I’ve also had old wines that are much better the next day and the tannins have mellowed out. With old wines,
it’s pretty much of a crap shoot.
I’ve also had young wines that have lost a lot of their fruit and have turned very hard/angular & tannic overnight. I’ve also had young
wines that had so much youthful fruit that I can find no change in them overnight…or even after 2-4 days. And I’ve also had young wines that
have gotten noticeably better overnight. Usually, those are ones that have had a bit of a funky or reduced character. With young wines,
it’s pretty much of a crap shoot as well.
The real problem is predictability. It’s pretty much of a crap shoot if the wine will get better overnight or not and I don’t think I can PnP a
wine and accurately predict that it’ll be better the next morning…when I’m usually trying these wines w/ my bowl of FrostedFlakes.
So…let’s settle this once & for all for all those thousands of folks following the two of us on the InterNet. When our HookerCreek Zin '14
arrives this week…let’s you & I do the experiment. Leave the btl open out on the counter overnight and we’ll try it the next morning and see what happens.
What do you predict…will it get better or worse or have no discernible change??? I expect the last one…but I’m only guessing.
You with me on this one, Wes??? Not giving you a hard time…just a bit.
Tom

I’m… I don’t know what I am. I generally side on “science”, but as others have pointed out, there really hasn’t been a lot of rigorous research on this subject. Same for those who’ve indicated that certain whites that have sat in a fridge for 2-3 days have improved, sometimes massively.

I’ve noticed little difference when I have decanted. With one exception. A while back I popped a '98 Mayacamas. It had these really bizarre aromas of tart cherries and Parmesan cheese. I thought for a minute that maybe I’d mistakenly purchased a Pinot (knowing that Mayacamas doesn’t make a Pinot…). No tannins. It was just weird.

I set the bottle aside, and checked in on it periodically. Ultimately the Parmesan notes blew off, the fruit got darker, and some gentle tannins finally showed up. But ya know what? That took a helluva lot longer than 5 minutes…It took a couple of hours and was still improving when we finished it.

As Larry says, there’s so much about this thing of ours that science can’t explain.

posts.name: Don Maloney
posts.cooked:
FWIW, Americas Test Kitchen decanted wine into a pitcher, then back-and-forth into another pitcher, about a dozen times or so, they said it made a big difference and the wine bloomed quickly owing to this. I’m sure they did not do this with something rare and aged, but it sounds interesting.

I’ve been enjoying wine 40+ years, and many bottles simply improve with breathing time, depending on the wine, and you must keep on top of the situation. Champagne almost always benefits from breathing in my experience. And I have enjoyed many bottles of the bubbly.

Now, about restaurants… From what I have heard, some actually pour the wine into a blender, and blend for an amount of time. Now I do not believe they are doing that widely, or with rare and aged wines, but…

YMMV

Nathan Myrvold and others have suggested using a blender.

As others have said, it would seem to depend on the wine. I was drinking a southern Rhone wine one night and didn’t really love it but after about an hour it seemed to get a lot better. So I wrote down some TNs for myself and they completely contradicted what I had thought earlier. A few weeks later I was doing a tasting for some wine novices and thought of an experiment. They were all enthusiastic about wine, but none of them claimed any particular knowledge. Quite unlike the magazine in question, which declares itself to be one of the best, and unlike the “stellar” tasters.

Anyhow I served two wines blind to about 20 people and they all had very definite preferences. None of the tasters was indifferent. They were curious as to what the wines were and where they could buy the second of them. So I unwrapped the wines. They were the same except that I had opened and decanted that bottle for six hours before rebottling and pretending to open it in front of them.

It isn’t hard science, but these people had no idea and there were no egos involved. I have to assume that they genuinely found the wines different. As did I.

Regarding Jeff’s comments on the wines in the tasting - interesting points. If the wines are older, the effects are not necessarily going to be the same as they would be for young wine. And then of course, the quality of the wine in the first place would matter. If you have crap to start with, airing it out won’t change its fundamental nature.

posts.name: Tim York
posts.cooked:
My favourite wine magazine, La Revue du Vin de France, also does tests like Decanter’s from time to time but with rather different results, which can be summarised as “it depends on the wine”. This is not very useful unless one happens to own one of the wines on which they have experimented.

Because the family chef is so unpredictable and often changes recipes at the last moment, I practice “pop and pour” on most occasions and rarely feel that I am missing much, though I do sometimes make a mental note that next time I should decant Château Un Tel to open and loosen it up a bit; that is if the chef gives me the time.

I used to decant religiously older clarets, fearing sediment. Having once ruined a Ducru-Beaucaillou 1970 by early decanting, I now never decant older, i.e. 15+ year, wine. The only sediment which now bothers me is the fine suspended kind. I have even learnt to empty the old bottle and let the sediment settle in my glass.

Vintage port is an exception. I still decant that but it is probably as much from respect for tradition as from necessity.

I have read a lot of good about the Audouze method but don’t have time for that in my household.

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
I’m a firm believer in decanting for enhancement.
The cheap Bordeaux bottles I consume regularly benefit significantly from at least 2 hours of air time. The better bottles I’ve had are no different. 28 year old Bordeaux responds in the same way, to my palate.

I always sample the wine as I let it rest on the counter.
There are fairly well defined phases that I experience, where either the acid or the tannin are at the fore, before the wine comes into balance and presents its best features.

Even better is a less than full bottle, kept upright (with cork inserted) in the cooler for a day or two, then decanted for an hour or more.
Most of the (non-Bordeaux) reds I’ve drank benefitted from some exposure to air as well.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
In my view, aeration is much more vital for enjoyment of a “mature” wine (whatever that means) than to a young one. It is also a much more delicate, perhaps riskier, proposition.But, there is more reward, I think. The young wine will show fruit and structure with good aeration. The older wine will show nuance and what older wines have to offer. Both need aeration to do that.

I always filter out sediment in older red wines. Whether it is “necessary” or not, that sediment, mostly from precipitated out tannins from the aging of red wines, is never a plus. By that time it has done its positives. And, those particles floating around ("suspended:) can play tricks with tastebuds and simulate “over the hill” wines aromatically and to the uniformed about what’s going on. (I know of many people who’ve had older Burgs that they thought were “over the hill” when the wine needed cleaning; I know that, too…from my own such experiences early on.)

No forumulae of course. Just guessing and experimenting to come up with a plan for the situation.

And, I think merely opening the bottle and “lettting it breath” aka “slow ox” is “no ox.” The same as “pop and pour” and maybe even a cop out by people who don’t want to bother taking a risk or thinking that they are doing nothing. (And there are variations that likely do more like “double decanting”…cleaning the wine and putting it back in bottle to “slow ox”.)

I know Audouze believes in it for the ancient wines he reguarly kills off en masse. I can’t argue with him, as I don’t have the relevant experience. I do suspect that he’s experiencing “pop and pour” essentially, though.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Considering young wines, it happens very often that I pop and pour as the freshness of the wine appears more genuinely and I see the eclosion of the wines. And I like to follow this evolution.

Considering old wines, and at this moment I have in my files notes on 3720 wines drunk prior to 1966, a considerable number of wines which were wounded have come back to life and shined after a period of slow oxygenation.

A 1874 Yquem that I considered dead came back to life.
A 1956 Romanée Conti considered as dead came back to life.

Stan probably considers me as a dreamer unexperienced, but I am sure of what I have witnessed by myself following the wine from the moment it is just opened and the moment it is served.

I remember opening a 1945 Beaujolais in restaurant of hôtel Meurice, and I let the sommelier smell it. He makes a bad face and says : “This bottle is dead and should never be served”. I told him : “you will see”. And the 1945 was voted third in a dinner where great wines were served.

I feel relax enough to recognize a mistake if I commit one. But to persuade me that I am wrong, the one who wants to do it must have strong weapons !

I would be happy that the so experienced people who made the experiment make a new one with wines of 1928 and 1915 and we woud see !!!

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked:
I almost always give wines some air before drinking them for the same reason I cellar wine – in order to maximize my chance of drinking them when they’re showing really well, if not necessarily at peak (impossible to say when that might occur for any individual bottle.)

Even though it would often be much more convenient to pop and pour, I’ve had so many wines that are almost completely uninteresting when just opened but then an hour or two or three later become wonderful that it’s no longer even a question for me, air helps. I have no interest at all “following a wine in the glass” when a lot of that time the wine is sometimes so boring (or nasty) that I think I made a mistake buying it; how is that fun? Seems more like a waste of what might be a terrific wine.

Never done a day or more in an open decanter but I’m not going to deny the experience of others (most of whom I doubt are delusional.)

Edit: I should have added that if I really like a wine when I open it, I don’t give it much more air, often just decanting for sediment then right back into the rinsed and drained bottle which I’ll give a spritz of gas and put the cork back in.

posts.name: Jason L Jones
posts.cooked:
Interesting study. As someone who is relatively new to wine, I often find myself drinking young bottles. According to CT, which I use to track what we take out of the cellar at home (not at restaurants), about 55% of the wines I’ve consumed in the last 4.5 years (roughly 1000 bottles) have been from 2010 or newer. While far from scientific, I’ve found that extended aeration has benefited my perception of nearly all of these wines.

With respect to Émile Peynaud, I’m reminded of a thread here where winemakers discussed how their palates have changed after frequently tasting wine in barrel.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

And, I think merely opening the bottle and “lettting it breath” aka “slow ox” is “no ox.” The same as “pop and pour” and maybe even a cop out by people who don’t want to bother taking a risk or thinking that they are doing nothing. (And there are variations that likely do more like “double decanting”…cleaning the wine and putting it back in bottle to “slow ox”.)

I know Audouze believes in it for the ancient wines he reguarly kills off en masse. I can’t argue with him, as I don’t have the relevant experience. I do suspect that he’s experiencing “pop and pour” essentially, though.

That’s the point of that method, imo, that the minimal oxygen exposure acts as a catalyst or allows bad volatile aromatics to escape or something, without significant oxidation of the wine. It’s not a substitute for when a wine needs air.

And, that’s why I believe it has the same effect as “pop and pour”…the amount of air in or bad stuff escaping through the dime (nickel?) sized surface that is exposed, I believe, is less than minimal. I think that’s not controversial. I think most wines are fine with pop and pour…as long as they’re clean and as long as one is willing to recognize that they can “recoil” into something very closed…pretty quickly…and you might have sit sniffing the glass for many hours to get back to the initial exuberance. That’s why I do it when I have no time to “properly” aerate…it’s better than aerating and doing it for too little, IMO.

Again, I can’t argue with Audouze, though I suspect that if he popped and poured these wines, he wouldn’t really notice much difference…except maybe the sediment would be more involved with popping and pouring. But, he has variables that I’m not familiar with including lots more ullage issues. But , IMO, to extrapolate from his exploits to “mature” or young wines…is likely folly.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Enjoying reading everyone’s thoughts on this topic, so thanks for starting it Stan. I have less experience than some, more than others, will only say off the top of my head.

My particular thoughts always gravitate to Sauternes when I’m thinking about this topic. I don’t believe I’ve had a single one that has not benefitted (in my OWN realm of taste and aromatic preferences) from a long period—as in more than a day—of oxygenation. That does not mean that I don’t swirl and taste it on opening…I do, because it’s important to me to get that first impression and it’s enjoyable and informative to see how the wine will change. I had some fun with this not too long ago when I had a full of 2001 La Tour Blanche that I decanted for about 6 hours, poured back and then left to slow-ox for a further day and a bit. I brought it to a dinner with Al Stewart, who’s had the wine many times and enjoyed it. He had never given it this kind of treatment and told me it was a completely different, and even more enjoyable, experience.

Ultimately, it comes down to your taste preferences and, eventually, a knowledge base for how a type or vintage of wine may react to aeration. I will probably always be learning on this front.

There are certainly wines, some of them young, that I just pop and pour. I definitely don’t decant everything—far from it. But I do slow-ox/Audouze a lot of the wines I drink nowadays and, whether I’m fooling myself or not, I enjoy them more because I choose to do that. For sure I tend to try to at least keep a little of even a pop and pour to see how it does the next day—for myself, I kinda feel I’m doing the wine a bit of a disservice if I don’t.

Now Champagne, that’s another matter


Salud,

Mike

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Stan,
What Emile Peynaud says is completely in phase with what I say.
Slow ox helps, decanting does not help and tortures very old wines.

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
Different wines “need” to aerate for different reasons. We’ve all had the experience of pouring a freshly-opened bottle of young wine into a glass and either gettiing weird smells or no smells at all. You come back to the glass 15-30 minutes later, and the off-smells may be gone or the nose has blossomed in the interim. You can try to make generalizations, but frankly it’s a science experiment each and every time. And I’ve certainly had the experience of pop and pour on an older red wine and thinking it was terrible, only to recork and stick in the fridge and voila it’s a fine, mature red wine.

One thing to keep in mind in terms of the organoleptics of red wine. When you first drink the wine, the wine may seem harshly tannic as the tannins begin the process of coating/binding with the taste receptors on your tongue. After you’ve been sipping the same wine for 15-30 minutes, the wine may not taste as tannic because of the taste receptors on your tongue, even though the wine itself hasn’t actually changed that much.

As the saying goes, you never step in the same river twice.

Bruce

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked:
My practice is fairly simple.

Young wine - decant or drink the bottle over 2 to 3 days. It is fascinating to observe the wine changes over 3 days. Some get better and some get worse.

Old wine (more than 10 to 15 yrs) - slow ox, usually just one hour.

I would like to take a Coravin and pour a glass and let it sit for an hour. Pour another and let sit for and hour. Pour another to consume then. Taste through the three glasses you get to taste all three time frames Within five or ten minutes. you don’t have to wonder if the change, if any, is due to what you are eating, temperature variation, mood, etc.

posts.name: J O S H L O N G
posts.cooked: I had a private tasting at Joseph Phelps (Claude I believe) and he decanted all the wines. He then took each one before pouring and violently shook them up in the decanters for around 30 seconds. He said this was the best way to get air into the wine more quickly. I had never seen that done before.
posts.display_username: J O S H L O N G

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked: I remember the first time I saw that…in Burgundy in 1996 (at George Mugneret, done by Christine)…I was startled and skeptical. Of course, if there’s sediment in the wine from aging…it will only mess up the experience, but with a young wine…it does seem to work, though it looks crazy.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Stuart,
I think that today I have an experience with old wines. And contrarily to many people having access to very old wines, I have opened them by myself, because I paid to have them and I have no one to prepare them for me.

When Michael Broadbent, Serena Sutcliffe, Jancis Robinson, Robert Parker, and other prestigious persons drink or drank old wines, someone had opened them for them. As I love to open them, World is well made.

I have drunk 2,250 wines before 1950 (this is a data only for the last 15 years and I drink old wines since 1975) and I have probably opened 1,800 / 1900 of them.

I cannot see many people who are in the world of wine who would have opened by themselves as many old wines as I did. People who have drunk more old wines than me exist and are certainly many, but people who have opened more than me are certainly not many and I do not know any. I do not have any obsession to be first or not, but observing the world of people consuming great very old wines, I think that I have never met someone who has opened more very old wines. If I am wrong there is no problem, I do not compete, I drink old wines as I love them.

I am conscious of the fact that if I make a mistake every time, the fact to have great experience has in that case no value. But as I have experienced almost every way to open wine, because I wanted to check if my method is a good method, makes me more inclined to say that slow oxygenation works largely better than decanting for very old wines (let us say 1961 and older).

I have already seen that an old bottle opened at the last moment appeared better than if I had used slow oxygenation. It happens and I would never say that it does not happen.

But the immense majority of wines are largely better with slow oxygenation.
Someone talked about Sauternes. This is the most obvious example of the positive effect of slow oxygenation, and of the fact that decanting has not the same positive effect.

This said, I do not want to impose my opinion. I have such an experience on opening old wines that I could write for hours examples of the brilliant effect of slow ox on wines on which other persons hesitated.

I accept easily that some persons doubt about the results of my experience, I have convinced so many persons that it is enough to satisfy me.

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked:
Stuart, I think you have to consider that when Francois slow-ox’s a 40-100 year old bottle the chances that the fill level is still “in the neck” and “dime-sized” is nil. He once mentioned too, selecting bottles from his cellar because they “needed to be drunk”. I do this too, with older bottles, and the primary way I judge such bottles is based on them developing a lower than ideal fill level!

When I have a bottle I want to slow-o, and it has a fill level in the neck, I will pour out a small exploratory taste, to expose some surface area in the bottle (like silver dollar size) and also to see what the “starting point” is like!

Wines that are at least semi-mature will benefit greatly from slow-o (at least with some surface area exposed), but it depends on their quality level too. I.e. a simple wine tends to remain simple, whereas a wine with excellent pedigree will develop lovely nuance and complexity that one hopes for, but is usually not present in the just-opened bottle.

Younger wines tolerate (and may need) the extra burst of immediate oxygen that decanting delivers. But tannic ones may shut down too! Older Burgs can be hurt, so I always slow-o these now, though I always used to decant them! I’m also retired and at home all day so I can pull a cork hours before supper, when others are still at work! Certain varietals like Syrah and Nebbiolo need more time in general, and so does Bordeaux, in general, because iit has more body and tannin than Burgundy.

Don’t forget a cool (but not cold) serving temperature too!

Oh… also… there is a big difference between “tasting” a freshly opened bottle, and “leisurely drinking” a wine over time. That probably explains the “results” expressed in the first post. A first taste is likely based on fruit, freshness, and “impact”, whereas when you slowly consume a wine over time you are looking for subtlety, nuance, complexity, etc.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Thanks for your nice, reply, Francois.

I have no doubt that “slow ox” creates nice experiences with very old wines. I just am not convinced that it is really any different from “pop and pour”, ie. opened at the last moment. Certainly, I am convinced that “slow ox” does no harm from your experience and your statement. The question I continue to have is whether it does anything meaningful. (I don’t think it does for the wines I drink most often.)

The issue is whether it does anything meaningful one way or another. I’m not sure how you can really tell…or anyone can. People usually do things because they are convinced that they are a positive. That’s why I aerate generously with “mature” i.e., wines 20-30 years old, which is what I drink most often. But, I can’t prove much of anything…nor can I dispute anything.

To some people, especially the guy who titled this thread, it is all “myth”. Maybe he’s not totally wrong…just mostly.

stuart

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked:
Taking an initial “exploratory taste” not only enlarges the surface area, but also shows you what the wine tastes like “at first”…

P.S. did you notice my post immediately before your last one?

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

And, that’s why I believe it has the same effect as “pop and pour”…the amount of air in or bad stuff escaping through the dime (nickel?) sized surface that is exposed, I believe, is less than minimal. I think that’s not controversial. I think most wines are fine with pop and pour…as long as they’re clean and as long as one is willing to recognize that they can “recoil” into something very closed…pretty quickly…and you might have sit sniffing the glass for many hours to get back to the initial exuberance. That’s why I do it when I have no time to “properly” aerate…it’s better than aerating and doing it for too little, IMO.

Again, I can’t argue with Audouze, though I suspect that if he popped and poured these wines, he wouldn’t really notice much difference…except maybe the sediment would be more involved with popping and pouring. But, he has variables that I’m not familiar with including lots more ullage issues. But , IMO, to extrapolate from his exploits to “mature” or young wines…is likely folly.

You’re kind of mixing things here. Super old wines, super young wines, and everything in between, I think it’s best to treat each bottle as its own thing. Francois can answer for himself, but I think he’s previously explained quite well how wines that countless times he’s had ancient wines that initially seemed dead, but with many hours of that dime-sized exposure came around gloriously.

We do know what a lot of these processes are. One “class” of changes has nothing to do with oxygen. Volatile aromatic compounds seek an equilibrium and will leave the wine for headspace in Coravin’d bottles, kegs, tanks, etc. There’s a notable loss during fermentation, as a factor of heat and time, where the headspace can be infinite, which is a concern for winemakers. (Cool ferm for aromatics, hot ferm for extraction, to oversimplify.)

Fluids aren’t static. So, while a dime sized oxygen exposure won’t quickly oxidize a wine, there’s no doubt it will cause reactions on the surface, and that just the dynamic of reactions results in movement in the fluid, which brings “new” wine to the surface. Sulfur dioxide unbonding in reaction to air can unbleach, unmute, and then unmask as it dissipates from the wine. Who knows all of what’s going on, as the number of variables from one bottle to the next are well over 1000. But, it’s not far-fetched to at least vaguely grasp how slow ox works to bring an ancient bottle around.

Personally, I pop-n-pour by default. A small pour to see what’s going on with the wine. I can then decant if needed (though I try to avoid cradle robbing, and with young wines I open to examine I want to see accurately how they show and evolve). An old wine that shows funky or dead can be set aside to revisit. I’ve rescued a few (pour’t) wines from the sink, to have them come around well. Funny how quickly some people are to give up on a wine they cellared for decades. (Btw, that pour’t as in pour’t down the sink.)

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Lots of anecdotal “evidence” here…from everyone, including me.

And, lots of generalizing about things that maybe can’t be generalized about.

However, “treating each bottle as its own thing” isn’t too practical in coming up with theories from those anecdotes, though it sounds nice.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
The wording “slow oxygenation” does not pretend to be scientific as what happens in fact is not oxygen going down in the bottle but bad molecules evaporating.

It would be good that I tell again the way I discovered the Audouze method.

It is probably in 1978 + or minus one year I do not remember exactly. For the dinner of December 24th I had chosen in my cellar a Chambertin 1929 and I do not remember the domaine.
My wife does not drink, my kids were very young, and I was the only one to drink.
I drank half of the bottle which was extremely good. I left the remaining standing in the cellar for the Christmas lunch.

When I took the bottle in the cellar to bring it in the dining room I smelt the wine and it was a miracle. The perfume was largely better and the taste was largely better too.
It was as if a wine having 96 points would have been transformed in a wine having 98 or 99 points.
I was so moved than I invited friends to leave their family and come to share a sip of this wine. Three of my friends living in the vicinity left their family and toasted, sharing with me this miracle.

It was then natural to ask myself: what happened. This was not the slow ox method but the power of air to enlarge the wine.

What is my experience with this method is that so many people or winemakers say: “open at the last moment, and drink, if not, the wine will collapse in the 15 minutes”.
In my case, wines never collapse because the slow ox method reinforces them. It is spectacular that a wine has the same taste for 30 minutes when opened this way.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
just for the fun

the "eye in the middle is made of corks of DRC wines.
This represents a small percentage of what I have opened.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
François Audouze:

The wording “slow oxygenation” does not pretend to be scientific as what happens in fact is not oxygen going down in the bottle but bad molecules evaporating.

It would be good that I tell again the way I discovered the Audouze method.

It is probably in 1978 + or minus one year I do not remember exactly. For the dinner of December 24th I had chosen in my cellar a Chambertin 1929 and I do not remember the domaine.
My wife does not drink, my kids were very young, and I was the only one to drink.
I drank half of the bottle which was extremely good. I left the remaining standing in the cellar for the Christmas lunch.

When I took the bottle in the cellar to bring it in the dining room I smelt the wine and it was a miracle. The perfume was largely better and the taste was largely better too.
It was as if a wine having 96 points would have been transformed in a wine having 98 or 99 points.
I was so moved than I invited friends to leave their family and come to share a sip of this wine. Three of my friends living in the vicinity left their family and toasted, sharing with me this miracle.

On a more serious note, your story of the transformation from the Reveillon to the Christmas lunch is what I experience all the time with my “mature” red Burgundies. I usually put them back in the bottle for a day or two (as we always have about 40% left from a bottle) and leave them otherwise “unprotected”. Shockingly, the wines very often (more than half the times) are marvelous and way better the next day or the day after. (Often the wine was aerated in decanter before the first drinking; a small percentage was “pop and pour” as I did not prepare the wine or have time to do that). I call this “aeration”, though…not “slow ox” because there is so much surface exposed to both take in oxygen and exhale other chemicals to improve the wine. I am a believer in what you found that day in 1978 or 1979. But, I am agnostic on what I think just opening a wine and letting it sit without a closure does, beyond “pop and pour”…But, clearly there is a continuum.

One of the reasons I have had some less than thrilling experiences at Bern’s over the last 35 years is because there is no time to aerate the bottles, even when my friends and I have chosen them in advance and asked that they be warmed to room temperatur and stood up to allow the sediment to settle. I understand their limitations there…and their good intentions, but…I think it lessens the experience, as the wines are kept so cold they are preserved and unevolved and really need some serious aeration.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
OK…hot off the press issue. An old, close friend of 40+ years (from Tampa) just called. We bought some 2002 d’Angerville Volnay Champans at the estate on a trip there together in 2004. He is going to a dinner party and will eat dinner in less than two hours. He asked “what should I do”.? (When he told me he had some 2004, but couldn’t find it, I told him to go back home and look for it.)

FWIW, I said, when he go to the party, open it and run it through a brown coffee filter…then clean the bottle and pour it back in… and let it “slow ox”. No time for any real “aeration”. He said he’d report back tomorrow


What would y’all have said re: prep given his time constraints ?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

Fluids aren’t static. So, while a dime sized oxygen exposure won’t quickly oxidize a wine, there’s no doubt it will cause reactions on the surface, and that just the dynamic of reactions results in movement in the fluid, which brings “new” wine to the surface.

Wes, I think this is not a very realistic view of what’s happening in a static bottle (or even glass) of wine. Whatever minor reactions might be taking place on or near the surface, those aren’t driving circulatory flow of any kind. In the absence of coherent flow, diffusion is a very slow process. For an O2 molecule in water, for example, it takes almost 7 hours to move just 1 cm (here’s a nice reference and interactive calculator where you can plug in diffusion coefficients and times or distances and calculate the open variable Diffusion Time Calculator - PhysiologyWeb). This is why swirling, decanting, any method of getting the liquid moving is beneficial. But just leaving it there sitting is going to do almost nothing, even over hours.

link_counts.title: Diffusion Time Calculator - PhysiologyWeb

posts.cooked:

posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Wes Barton:

Fluids aren’t static. So, while a dime sized oxygen exposure won’t quickly oxidize a wine, there’s no doubt it will cause reactions on the surface, and that just the dynamic of reactions results in movement in the fluid, which brings “new” wine to the surface.

Wes, I think this is not a very realistic view of what’s happening in a static bottle (or even glass) of wine. Whatever minor reactions might be taking place on or near the surface, those aren’t driving circulatory flow of any kind. In the absence of coherent flow, diffusion is a very slow process. For an O2 molecule in water, for example, it takes almost 7 hours to move just 1 cm (here’s a nice reference and interactive calculator where you can plug in diffusion coefficients and times or distances and calculate the open variable Diffusion Time Calculator - PhysiologyWeb). This is why swirling, decanting, any method of getting the liquid moving is beneficial. But just leaving it there sitting is going to do almost nothing, even over hours.

The Audouze Method starts with pouring out a small amount to make sure it’s not vinegar, leaving a surface somewhere between a quarter and a half dollar in size. That modest action alone introduces more aeration than just pulling the cork.

Keep in mind that, while invariably the wine becomes “better,” it does not invariably become “great.” So while some of the old lesser-vintage wines that François opens are merely “agreeable” or “pleasant,” they are valued for the fact that they taste far different from their modern counterparts and broaden one’s knowledge of what long-aged Bordeaux or Burgundy can offer, especially with complementary food.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Wes Barton:

Fluids aren’t static. So, while a dime sized oxygen exposure won’t quickly oxidize a wine, there’s no doubt it will cause reactions on the surface, and that just the dynamic of reactions results in movement in the fluid, which brings “new” wine to the surface.

Wes, I think this is not a very realistic view of what’s happening in a static bottle (or even glass) of wine. Whatever minor reactions might be taking place on or near the surface, those aren’t driving circulatory flow of any kind. In the absence of coherent flow, diffusion is a very slow process. For an O2 molecule in water, for example, it takes almost 7 hours to move just 1 cm (here’s a nice reference and interactive calculator where you can plug in diffusion coefficients and times or distances and calculate the open variable Diffusion Time Calculator - PhysiologyWeb). This is why swirling, decanting, any method of getting the liquid moving is beneficial. But just leaving it there sitting is going to do almost nothing, even over hours.

Firstly, you’ve ignored my primary point of volatile aromatic compounds leaving solution. Secondly, I think you are vastly underestimating the complexity of wine. How do you explain popping a cork to find massive stink, letting the bottle sit for a number of minutes and having it all blow off? Too much time to only be in the headspace. Obviously not something that was just on the surface. Many times I’ve seen complex solutions actively roiling. Wine has alcohols, sugars, many types of weakly bonded compounds, many hundreds of different molecules. Introduce the catalyst of a little oxygen to that and you really don’t think anything will happen? Wine isn’t anything close to water (other than Pinot Gris). How do you explain the dramatic color change anecdote upthread? Sounds like the unbonding of anthocyanin-4-bisulfite in reaction to oxygen.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Sigh. You’re just throwing buzz words around now. My comments were directed at the case of opening a bottle and just letting it sit there. Oxygen is not a “catalyst”, it is a reactive compound (which, if you believe Waterhouse, actually doesn’t doe much to a bottle of wine, at least most young wines). And those wines you’ve seen actively roiling in the bottle? Not sure I want to drink those. Sounds more like a scene out of the exorcist…

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Alan,

Waterhouse is (I assume) ignoring Thiols (‘reductive’ compounds) becoming oxidized, which will generally change their perception…generally to be less or not perceptible. I don’t know much about the chemistry of a 40+ bottle of wine (does anyone), but I’d guess this (oxidizing of Thiols) is a significant part of what’s going on during a slow-ox.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
George Chadwick:

In my experience opening a bottle and letting it breathe and then slowly drinking it until it’s empty and then going to the cellar to get another bottle always improves the flavor of the second bottle.

And the memory of the first.

You’re kind of mixing things here. Super old wines, super young wines, and everything in between,

Really I think that’s the problem. A wine that is 15 - 20 years old is NOT an “old” wine. In fact, for a lot of wines, that’s when they start becoming interesting. A wine that is 50+ years old is a different thing entirely. You can’t treat a five year old wine the same as its great-great-great grandfather of a wine.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Eric Lundblad:

Waterhouse is (I assume) ignoring Thiols (‘reductive’ compounds) becoming oxidized, which will generally change their perception…generally to be less or not perceptible. I don’t know much about the chemistry of a 40+ bottle of wine (does anyone), but I’d guess this (oxidizing of Thiols) is a significant part of what’s going on during a slow-ox.

I suspect you’re right, that Waterhouse was referring more to dissipation of SO2 in younger wines than to oxidation of thiols. But when a wine has noticeable thiols, that’s typically something you perceive on the aroma early on, but does it affect the taste of the wine? The point I was making is applicable either way: just opening a bottle of wine, even if you pour out a small amount to expose more surface area, is going to be an extremely slow process of diffusing O2 into the entire bottle (I’m not even sure that exposing more surface area makes that much difference when there is still the literal bottleneck restricting gaseous diffusion to the liquid surface, but that is probably not the limiting factor). So I’m going to continue to claim (certainly for a younger wine) that decanting or just pouring into a glass is much more efficient at allowing O2 in then just opening the bottle, which will do almost nothing.

posts.cooked:

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

I suspect you’re right, that Waterhouse was referring more to dissipation of SO2 in younger wines than to oxidation of thiols. But when a wine has noticeable thiols, that’s typically something you perceive on the aroma early on, but does it affect the taste of the wine? The point I was making is applicable either way: just opening a bottle of wine, even if you pour out a small amount to expose more surface area, is going to be an extremely slow process of diffusing O2 into the entire bottle (I’m not even sure that exposing more surface area makes that much difference when there is still the literal bottleneck restricting gaseous diffusion to the liquid surface, but that is probably not the limiting factor). So I’m going to continue to claim (certainly for a younger wine) that decanting or just pouring into a glass is much more efficient at allowing O2 in then just opening the bottle, which will do almost nothing.

From my observation, thiols do affect the taste as well. For one thing, most of ‘taste’ is ‘inner mouth’ aromatics…and thiols definitely affect that.

I agree that ‘slow-o’ has more impact on older wines than younger. For one thing, younger wines still have sulfur, so a fair amount of oxygen would be caught up by the sulfur (depending on the free so2 levels), where older wines will have none. But I think you’re underestimating the effect of the initial pour, both in the amount of O2 that gets into the wine, and the mixing involved. Getting ‘some’ oxygen into an older wine via slow-o, mostly due to the initial tiny pour, doesn’t seem like a problem to me…and even with younger wines, depending on the free sulfur it starts at.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Francois,
I think we’re having two slightly different conversations. In most cases, people are talking about letting younger wines breathe, while you are talking about much older wines. I think most of us have the experience that older wines are more fragile, and can change relatively quickly once opened and poured into a glass. And I don’t know anything about the chemistry that might be going on in very old wines once opened and exposed to air. I can’t argue with your own observations over thousands of bottles - though I do wonder if you’ve often tried wines after being open for an hour or two, then compared later after a day of opening.

To answer your shark question, I have a very simple experiment you can try: pour a medium size glass of water at room temperature. Let it sit for a minute or two, then carefully lower a spoon-full of sugar to the bottom of the glass. You could use some sugar cubes and just drop them in as well. Then just let the glass sit, and every now and then take a look and see how much sugar is left. Lightly dip the spoon at the surface and taste the water to see if it tastes sweet. But don’t stir or disturb the glass. You may find it interesting how long it takes the sugar to dissolve, and how much longer still it takes to perceive any sweetness at the surface of the water.

posts.name: Jon Hesford
posts.cooked:
I was asked to write an article on this subject a couple of months ago. I did a fair bit of internet-style research and the general consensus among those writing about wine is that decanting generally improves wines, especially young, tannic wines but it can damage older wines, like the 1961 in the example.

I also found that uncorking does nothing and that methods of super-aeration were also pointless. The key things were that all the wine has access to the air and that the wine is then allowed to rest for at least an hour so that the absorbed oxygen can have time to alter the tannins.

I would argue that Decanter isn’t a particularly well-respected wine magazine among those who know wine. Their panel tastings are often way out compared to the general consensus. This “experiment” where all the wines, except the supermarket plonk of Mouton Cadet, were over 17 years old already, is hardly proof of anything.

Is there anyone else in the wine business or trade out there who does not think that wines improve over several hours when presenting red wines before their prime drinking time? Or even complex white wines?

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
I learned the slow oxigenation method from my grandmother . She always opened the bottles a few hours before " service " and claimed they needed this for breathing , just the way Francois describes . Most of these bottles were red Bordeaux or red Burgundy , aged at least 20 to 30 years old . My experience is also in favor of opening old bottles , let them breathe , but do NOT decant them . I still remember a fantastic Haut Brion 1947 , delicious at opening , that was oxidized after the sommelier had pored it in a decanter 3 to 4 hours before serving it .
So if you need to decant an old bottle , start drinking it within a reasonable time frame.
Young wines ( but only excellent ones ) benefit from decanting , no doubt about it .

I love the Francois story from nearly 30 years ago. It shows the man’s passion and exuberance one can have for what many view as a ‘silly little drink’. Calling friends away from their families on Christmas Eve to ‘come taste this joy’ embodies EXACTLY what I love about wine.
Thank you sir for sharing!

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow

Mike, I love the story, too, though the people were “called” at lunchtime Xmas day after the wine sat in the bottle overnight and earlier that day.

It has made me smile for several reasons since I read it, including the one you describe. It also reminds me of the first few times I had a red Burgundy with some age(not that much age as Francois’) do exactly that after sitting in a half empty bottle for some period. Early on with those results, I tried experiments as I did not believe what I was experiencing. And, I smile because the result in Francois’ case was from what I would consider full on aeration…or nearer to that than the method called “slow ox”. That is ironic that the epiphany was prompted by aeration of an ancient wine, rather than the method he is credited for. (And, I long ago gave up on that method actually doing anything for wines: I had read much and heard much that it was the case that it did nothing much, if anything.)

I have enjoyed this discussion, though…I am uconvinced that “slow ox” and “pop and pour” are much, if at all, different for any wine. (Of course, if you slow ox for week…maybe more likely different.) I thought I had read something here that convinced me to look for a wine to become a “lava lamp” with slow ox as the volcano simmers…but…after reading the responses to that claim, I’ve never seen that, so I remain where I was. I’d love an “answer” , but have been looking for one for over 30 years on this “slow ox” method…for any wine, ie, that it does anything appreciable to a wine of any age to pull the cork and let the bottle rest. (I realize age is a factor, but…am not persuaded that it makes a difference re: “slow ox”.

And, yes…there are many who do that method and whose grandmothers advocated it. It certainly doesn’t hurt. But, I’d like to be persuaded that it does much of anything. Stories and long-held practices are just that, including mine. They are all part of the possible “myth” that started this thread, though , IMO, even more so, as everyone seems to agree that aerating can impact a wine. (I think everyone agrees?). But, the question is whether slow-ox is “aerating” in any meaningful way to a wine of any age? Or is just a “why not” approach?

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Paul Savage:

Taking an initial “exploratory taste” not only enlarges the surface area, but also shows you what the wine tastes like “at first”…

P.S. did you notice my post immediately before your last one?

Paul,

Sorry. Yes I noticed that post but not your follow-up one.

What you describe are variable amounts of surface exposed to air (and allowed to degas, too) depending on the amount of surface exposed. IMO, this is a continuum that at some point is “slow ox” and at some point becomes the same as “decanting” or “aeration.” (Francois’ story about the Xmas wine and its epiphany effect is, for me , the product of aeration, as it is very far along that continuum and the wine was previously shaken in serving the first part, though not as vigorously as the shaking described by someone here to simulate aeration.) But, I think with “slow ox” we are generally talking about pulling the cork and letting the wine “breath” through the bottle neck. On that end of the continuum, I persist in being unconvinced that the effect is more than “pop and pour”, though…the time of “slow ox” is also a continuum…perhaps…though not a significant factor in the time context that we seem to be talking about.

So, continuing on the title of the topic…i guess I think “aeration” is not a “myth”. But, I am still unconvinced that “slow ox” is not a myth in that it has any tangible benefits. I’d love to be disabused of this, as I have been considering it LONG before I ever heard of Mr. Audouze or this board. I used to do it…why not…until credible people told me it was a “myth” (though not in those words). It was then, and particularly after having many experiences like Francois’ Xmas story…in both colors of Burgundy-- that I started to focus on “aeration” and its benefits…and dangers, both using decanters and, more often, just leaving half consumed bottles as Francois did, to try the next day. (Usually in the am, as that’s when I taste wines best , I think.

I know you are a believer, Paul. But just relating more and more anecdotes (and experience) is, for me, like a someone who is speaking a foreign language to someone who doesn’t understand it…louder and louder (a la Garrett Morris on Saturday Night Live) and not getting that that doesn’t do the job any better than whispering it. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g2Q0cyJSs04

I fully agree with François … and in addition I do think that to slow-ox also younger wines can definitely be for the benefit … I´ve experienced it many times since I learned this method from François - not matter if Decanter or whoever says the opposite …

Only one example: last Saturday I´ve lead a tasting of 25 wines “From the nineties” … see the thread The 90ies - Bordeaux, Burgundy, Rhone ... - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers with this title a few days ago …

I´ve opened all wines 6 to 6.5 hours in advance - then tasted a tiny glass directly from each bottle and put the rest back …
Then some 20 minutes before serving I decanted each wine carefully … to remove the sediment as well as to give further aeration (different from Francois) …

There was not ONE single wine (with the exception of the corked bottle) that didn´t show better on the evening than right after opening … and quite a number of wines did give very few to almost nothing after opening - and really shined on the evening …

That is enough proof for me … may anybody say whatever he wants … I´ve suffered enough with wines served to me pop-and-poured - and which were rather disapointing (when I knew them well enough to say that they could have performed much better …)

With very young wines slow-oxing can or cannot improve it … it depends - but it will definitely do no harm …
in opposite immediate decanting can easily make the wines more acidic and/or more tannic … so I usually decant only after several hours of opening (if time allows) …

Francois…

“stubborn” is not the right word. Convinced of something without any proof is more precise.

I believe your experience…and its breadth. I just became convinced many years before I heard your name that opening a bottle and letting it sit open for a period (I am presuming less than a day) does nothing much…nothing harmful…and nothing meaningful.

And…I certainly believe that the first sip can be different; after all the tiny portion of the wine at the top that is exposed to oxygen (and outflow) is undoubtedly affected, maybe even perceptibly. But…since that bottle was not popped and poured and a popped and poured bottle was not “slow oxed”…and, by definition can’t be both, I don’t know how one tells.

I am unwilling to abandon my long held beliefs until I have reason. I don’t think that is stubborn; the opposite is “foolish”.

You believe what you believe…and I have NO doubt that you have wonderful experiences after using your methods…and that others do, too. I just question whether the “slow ox” contributes to them…or…never disturbing them by pouring them out into a decanter…or just popping and pouring…would be an equally good experience. In other words, is the slow ox even a significant variable in what it does to the wine…or is is what it doesn’t do to the wine: disturb it or, perhaps, expose it to significant aeration? I am all ears and have been for decades.

More evidence of your experiences, though, is like the guy yelling louder and louder to a deaf person…as in the video I posted. It doesn’t add anything to the communication.

You seem to feel that I doubt your views or your experiences. I don’t…

The people in ancient Greece had lots of stories about Zeus and other things from Delphi. When I was on my honeymoon there in 1983 (later that trip to Alsace and Bourgogne), I had lots of trouble distinguishing between the mythological and the concrete when our guides spoke of Zeus and his friends as if they were “facts”. Since then, I have been particularly careful to understand the distinctions-- and ironically , I “fell” for Burgundy wines on that trip…and had my first lesson on “aeration” at the end of our long trip. In Paris my wife’s close friends told me that Bordeaux need decanting and Burgundies should be poured directly from the bottle into the glass, shortly after opening them. But, no one could explain why. So, I asked for more and more Burgundy…as they seemed to honor it less…and I liked it a lot more. When I got home, I was hooked. And, it seemed Burgundy required a lot less effort, too…no decanting.

And, then there is the movie (“The Idiot”) where Steve Martin’s character thinks he is being fooled by being sold “old” wines. the jerk - wine scene - YouTube

Some people on this board seem to believe that the benefits of allowing potentially great wines to age to be “myth” even. They slaughter large numbers of infant wines at one setting to disprove the “myth”. Mon Dieu!!

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Wes, diffusion doesn’t care if a wine is old or young. Just pulling the cork, even if you pour off a small amount right way, leaves the liquid quite static after the convection settles down in a minute or so. At that point there are no mechanisms other than diffusion to bring O2 in, or get volatile compounds out. And being volatile has no bearing on diffusion rates - try opening a can of coke and just let it sit there, you’ll find plenty of carbonation even the next day.

Try the sugar experiment I suggested. It’s not meant to be a model for wine, but it will give you a very obvious and visual picture of diffusion in a liquid.

In the discussion that I created concerning Pommard Epenots of Domaine Parent I mention at one moment a 1915 Nuits-Saint-Georges Les Cailles Morin Père & Fils. I will tell a story about this wine which will add something to this discussion.

End of nineties, I bought 14 bottles of this 1915 NSG. It is my cherished baby as this one has always performed. The last one will be drunk in a dinner to come.

The story of one of them is this one:
One day in the newspaper “Le Monde”, there is an article of one third of a page, which is big as the format of the newspaper is big, about changes of the menus in restaurant Lucas Carton whose chef was Alain Senderens, a famous three stars chef, who invented the “Nouvelle Cuisine” some decades ago. The article says that Alain has decided a very big change : the menu in his restaurant will not present the dish first but will present the wine first and the dish which will accompany it. The major point is not the dish that you choose, but the wine that you choose.

The article talks about the audacious move of Alain and the article finishes so : in fact Alain Senderens does what François Audouze does for years in his dinners.

As this article was written in 2002 I was totally unknown and Alain did not know me. I phoned him, saying that we were mentioned together in the article of Le Monde and Alain who did not know me said : “we will have a private lunch the two of us, come to my restaurant”. I said : “do you allow me to bring an old wine as it is my passion”. He says : “I do not like so much old wines, it is not necessary that you bring anything”. I insisted and I said that I would like that the wine is opened some hours before the lunch.

The day of our meeting I brought at 9h30 my wine, a 1915 Nuits-Saint-Georges Les Cailles Morin Père & Fils and I asked the sommelier to open it now as I had an appointment not allowing me to do it myself.

When I came back at 12:30, I asked the sommelier : “how was the smell ?”. He made a face indicating that the smell was not nice. I saw in his eyes that for him, the wine was bad. As I like to make unexpected answers I said to the sommelier : “this is a good thing for what I want to prove”. I smelled the wine and the perfume was great. It was not provocation from my side, but the will to be excited by what life brings to me.

We had the lunch together and I must say that Alain is a fantastic chef thinking of so many details which have an importance that I was under his charm. He proposed that we chew roses with the NSG and it was fantastic. I met him several times in his private ateliers for food and wine with people like Jean-Pierre Perrin of Beaucastel.

But the story does not finish with this meal. One month later a newspaper called “l’Amateur de Bordeaux” interviews Alain Senderens. And at one moment of the article the journalist asks Alain : “what is the greatest wine that you drank in the last month”.

Alain Senderens said that his best wine of the last month was the 1915 Nuits-Saint-Georges Les Cailles. I was a little sorry that my name was not mentioned, but I was happy that my wine was mentioned.

If I add this memory to our discussion it is because a sommelier of a three stars restaurant had found the smell of my wine ugly by opening and the wine was appreciated by a three stars chef who does not like old wines as the best wine of his last month.

I have the weakness to think that ugly smells which transform into nice perfumes belong to the method that I use.
