posts.name: chris savino
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

To answer your shark question, I have a very simple experiment you can try: pour a medium size glass of water at room temperature. Let it sit for a minute or two, then carefully lower a spoon-full of sugar to the bottom of the glass. You could use some sugar cubes and just drop them in as well. Then just let the glass sit, and every now and then take a look and see how much sugar is left. Lightly dip the spoon at the surface and taste the water to see if it tastes sweet. But don’t stir or disturb the glass. You may find it interesting how long it takes the sugar to dissolve, and how much longer still it takes to perceive any sweetness at the surface of the water.

Comparing dissolving sugar in water and a gas or volatile in the presence of a liquid are two different things. Gases will off gas based upon partial pressures and can also get into the liquid based upon the same method. A more realistic test, take a bottle of bleach and a bottle of water, open both and connect the tops with a hose and wait for 4-6 hours and see if you taste chlorinated water now in the bottled water.

Henry's law

In physical chemistry, Henry's law is a gas law that states that the amount of dissolved gas in a liquid is directly proportional to its partial pressure above the liquid. The proportionality factor is called Henry's law constant. It was formulated by the English chemist William Henry, who studied the topic in the early 19th century. In simple words, we can say that the partial pressure of a gas in vapour phase is directly proportional to the mole fraction of a gas in solution. An example where...

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
cjsavino:
Alan Rath:

To answer your shark question, I have a very simple experiment you can try: pour a medium size glass of water at room temperature. Let it sit for a minute or two, then carefully lower a spoon-full of sugar to the bottom of the glass. You could use some sugar cubes and just drop them in as well. Then just let the glass sit, and every now and then take a look and see how much sugar is left. Lightly dip the spoon at the surface and taste the water to see if it tastes sweet. But don’t stir or disturb the glass. You may find it interesting how long it takes the sugar to dissolve, and how much longer still it takes to perceive any sweetness at the surface of the water.

Comparing dissolving sugar in water and a gas or volatile in the presence of a liquid are two different things. Gases will off gas based upon partial pressures and can also get into the liquid based upon the same method. A more realistic test, take a bottle of bleach and a bottle of water, open both and connect the tops with a hose and wait for 4-6 hours and see if you taste chlorinated water now in the bottled water.

The purpose of the sugar experiment is to provide a visual demonstration of how slow diffusion in a static liquid really is. We all know that sugar easily dissolves in water, right? But put a tablespoon of sugar at the bottom of a glass, and most of it will just sit there for hours and hours. Why? Because water will dissolve the solid sugar, but rapidly build up a high concentration near the sugar/water interface. For the solid sugar to continue dissolving, the already dissolved sugar has to migrate (diffuse) away. That takes a long, long time. Yes, sugar is probably a bit larger molecule, with a lower diffusion constant than some smaller molecules, but it’s representative of the principle. Stir the water with a spoon for a minute, and all of the sugar will dissolve. That’s the difference between decanting and just opening a bottle.

You’re making the mistake of thinking that a molecule which would normally exist in the gas phase (say O2, or SO2) where there is high diffusion, will have that same rapid diffusion dissolved in water. That’s not true. Once dissolved, a normally “gaseous” compound will diffuse through water just as any other non-volatile compound does. Take a look at the short list of compounds shown in the reference I linked earlier. Glucose has a diffusion coefficient of about 5e-6, while O2 (a much smaller, and volatile molecule) is only 4 times faster at 2e-5.

It’s an easy experiment. Try it.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I have no doubt the wines change significantly with exposure to air, but a lot of times I think we can’t separate that from other effects like warming, or just our perception changing with time.

Say I taste a cellar temp white right when I open it, then chill it in an ice bucket. Then over time it warms up. At some point it is back to the original temperature. All the time I’ve been eating, drinking, or whatever. I would bet that even if there is no change at all in the wine itself, I will perceive it differently. And of course all the time the wine is changing temperature. I don’t see how anyone can separate these factors. You could control for temperature, but not for your brain.

“You have seen a wolf. But are you sure that you have seen it? Was it really a wolf that you have seen? Were you sane when you have seen it? Was it raining ? When it rains, your view can be different. Do you wear glasses? Because when you wear glasses and when it rains, what you think to have seen can be very different. So I conclude that it is impossible that you have seen a wolf. You have seen a cat and I do not understand why you talk about a non important event”.

At a certain moment, where is the interest of giving information which are systematically criticised?

But as the subject motivates me as I would like that people understand better the world of old wines, I will continue to defend the world of old wines.

In my book edited in 2003 I have said that 50% of old wines which were thrown away were great wines. My opinion is that the ignorance about old wines pushed people to declare dead wines which were not dead.

I will defend the world of old wines by showing amateurs how to open them and how to enjoy them. In the Academy for ancient wines, we have made 25 meetings and we have shared 2,200 wines that I have opened by myself. This is a huge work but it shows to the members that the number of lively wines exceeds largely the expectation of many amateurs.

It is why I accept this obscurancy as the negation of reality is extremely vivid for reasons that I have difficulty to understand.
Whenever we talk about slow oxygenation method, what comes first is criticism. And whatever is the depth of the experiences that I make, critics come first.

That is life and it proves that I am not clever enough to convince others.
I must try harder.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
Last year , we opened a magnum of Haut Brion 1929 . My friend and I took a little sip , it was very bad . My friend dismissed the wine completely . I asked him to wait : this wine has been " trapped " for more than 85 years , give it time to open up . And indeed , 2 hours later , it was magnificent . It seems that this experience describes the method of slow oxigenation , I am a true believer .

Of course ,this is just anecdotal evidence ( even when you do this a thousand times , like Francois ) . In order to prove this , you would have to compare sufficiently large groups of identical wines ( 100 bottles per group ? ) , tasted blind , and compare results using statistical methods , just the way we do it in the pharmaceutical industry . Seems close to impossible to do , with old wines .

So anecdotal evidence is no proof , but a believe based on experience . Francois , you will convince some but not everybody because you offer no proof . But why should this bother you ? I love your posts and I learn a lot from them and I think a lot of people here do too . I am very interested in your experience because I drink the same wines you do , and want to avoid mistakes . You have your personal style , which is very very French , and needs to be understood . And I always love it when you claim the wines you bring are the best . But your posts are the ones I look most forward to .

posts.name: Chris Murphy
posts.cooked:
I originally felt the same way about slow ox. No way that oxygen can make it to the bottom of a bottle in any reasonable time. But I recently had a discussion with a friend and he mentioned an experiment he had done in grad school where oxygen would quench what he was studying. His experimental data showed that oxygen quickly diffuses into the entire solution, within minutes. He had no doubt that the same would happen in wine

Also found a paper online which discusses diffusion in sea water. Much faster than simple diffusion calculations would predict

Experimental data convinced me.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
IMO, there is no reason to have to “prove” everything one claims. But, the other side of that coin is that one cannot claim to have proven it if one cannot. That seems to be the issue here. Like religion, sometimes faith is the only glue.

Slow-ox does no harm. So…as Herwig says…it is at least interesting.

And, if experiment were possible, I think a few bottles could tell the story…initially.

I am still very interested in two things from this thread:

  1. The scientific debate threaded through it. Very interesting to someone who is clueless like me.
  2. What we can draw from Francois’ epiphany with the wine Xmas eve into Xmas day lunch and its transformation with aeration at age 50+. It is ironic that this epiphany would be for someone who advocates “slow ox” only…and voila…more aggressive aeration caused his epiphany.

I think everyone understands here, as Herwig says, there is no disrespect here…just healthy skepticism, as there is with many subjects that are not “proven”.

As I said before the idea of popping a cork and letting a wine breathe…whether real or myth…has intrigued me for 32+ years. I had long assumed it did nothing much of anything, alone. But, of course, then, to taste the wine, you have to put it in the glass and then…it sits there for at least some time…both “aeration” , albeit maybe negligible-- for a young sturdy wine. So…it makes the “proof” even more futile, perhaps?

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Some people on this board seem to believe that the benefits of allowing potentially great wines to age to be “myth” even. They slaughter large numbers of infant wines at one setting to disprove the “myth”. Mon Dieu!!

I know that this is your own personal vendetta against people drinking Burgundies too young, but surely you must realize that they are not drinking the wines young to disprove the benefits of aging. The reasons for drinking great wines young that I see most often stated or implied here include: (1) evaluating for knowledge of how the wine will age; (2) the individual actually enjoys tasting the wine at various points in its evolution; and (3) the individual only has access to a recent vintage, so his or her only option is to consume young.

I seldom see people here claiming that red burgundy is inherently better young or won’t improve. I believe, based upon your participation in many threads, that this is only your personal characterization of the motive of others.

White Burgundy is, of course, a different story, in that the failure rate of wines in this category has certainly made many consume them while young.

What is interesting here is that everyone except the OP seems to agree that the premise that allowing wine to breathe improves the wine is not, in fact, a myth. You gentleman have now spent a couple of pages arguing the merits of various methods of aerating, but you all seem to be in agreement that in most cases the wine should breathe, and not for the minute window indicated by the OP.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Yes…it does seem like people are in agreement, though defining “breathe” is difficult. To me almost all threads are interesting for where they can lead; not necessarily “the minute window indicated by the OP”.

And, my “vendetta” (though I wouldn’t call it that…more like a “peeve”) is about the “mass slaughter” of them and other ageworthy wines “at one setting”. I have no problem with consuming potentially great wines as infants…as long as people realize that they are doing that and the limitations. To each his own…but…when great wines are slaughtered en masse, I have a problem that I can’t help articulating at times, somewhat like an addiction. (The emphasis is on “mass” here; I think there is a limit on what one can really process in quantity…and at some point in that number…it becomes a waste. I don’t profess to “know” the limit…though i have my ideas of what I can process and most people I know.)

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Perhaps vendetta is strong, I’m fine with “peeve” or whatever you choose. Perhaps “campaign” splits the difference?

Again, I am not advocating for a position, just suggesting that when individuals drink a wine young they don’t do it to thumb their noses at the idea it will improve, but for other reasons entirely. I’d love to have a perfectly aged cellar from which to draw, but alas, that is not currently the case, and with wine prices being what they are, likely never will be.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
New York magazine published an article by Alex Bespaloff on this subject around 1978. I was in retail then and we had a serious tasting program. So we tested this notion and most of the time the freshly opened wines were most liked. I expected the Barolo to do best with aeration but i was wrong.

Let’s not forget what changes most while a wine is open": the drinkers!!

I cannot say that breathing a wine is always bad idea but I do feel, after experimentation, that 2/3 of the time it is not necessary.

Exactly the same reaction I’ve witnessed during 100’s of tastings over the years.

Mel Knox:

New York magazine published an article by Alex Bespaloff on this subject around 1978. I was in retail then and we had a serious tasting program. So we tested this notion and most of the time the freshly opened wines were most liked. I expected the Barolo to do best with aeration but i was wrong.

Let’s not forget what changes most while a wine is open": the drinkers!!

I cannot say that breathing a wine is always bad idea but I do feel, after experimentation, that 2/3 of the time it is not necessary.

Yup, Mel…inspired by Alex’s article…we did the experiment. It was published as an article in WineSpectator…back when that was a good
publication…a tabloid newspaper put out by BobMorrisey in SanDiego.
We did some 30 wines, 2 btls of each. One decanted 6-8 hrs before. One just opened & decanted. Did the A/B comparison (blind, of course)
and did the statistics on the results. At the 99% confidence level, only two wines of the 30 were found to be improved by breathing. And those
two were marred by excess SO2/H2S. Of course, you can fault that study as the tasters were not qualified, I chose the wrong wines, I should have
decanted 10-12 hrs ahead, we did not all use Reidel, blah/blah/blah.
I repeated the same study w/ Barolo/Barbaresco Nebbs w/ my SantaFe group, using 8 wines. Same result…could not detect an improvement.

So my procedure is to PnP, decant if there is sediment only. That said, I have, of course, found wines that were improved by sitting out on my sideboard overnight.
At least to my perceptions. But I cannot predict, a priori, which wines will benefit from that. To assert that such & such a Barolo '76 must be decanted 7.3 hrs ahead
of serving to show its best…I’m pretty skeptical.

This breathing argument is tedious & boring, so I usually stay out of them. I just PnP and take my chances.
Tom

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Mel, speaking of articles out of NY then…I"m reminded of the famous Martin Gersh, and found this:

there was a wine writer for Vogue magazine named Martin Gersh. Mr. Gersh wrote eloquent and informed articles on all sorts of wine subjects, usually followed by a list of recommended wines, which always included exactly how long the reader should let the wines breathe: 30 minutes, 1 hour 20 minutes, 50 minutes, et cetera. We envisioned Mr. Gersh sitting in his kitchen with a stopwatch tasting all of the recommended wines every five minutes and noting the changes in each until it reached sybaritic Nirvana.

Great Lakes Wine & Spirits – 2 Jan 14

Decanting: Is your carafe half full or half empty?

Although things like appellation boundaries and production rules are finite and not flexible (unless you are in Burgundy), the appreciation of wine, i.e. drinking & enjoying it, is entirely subjective.

Gersh was a retired NY City schoolteacher who was convinced that not only did breathing benefit wines and simulate the aging process…but that there was some relationship between open, full aeration and the time and the amount of maturity one could add. (As I remember it). He wrote his columns for a fairly long time…and gave precise aeration suggestions to readers.

A good friend (still; we’re going to his house for dinner tomorrow), was, in the '80s the Phila. Inquirer’s wine columnist and not only knew Gersh, but went to his home to interview him and experience his experiments. I remember his description of the dinner at the time. Gersh was not insane…that I remember. It was around that time that I got interested in this issue, particularly the one of “slow ox”, as it is now known.

As I suggested on Francois’ other thread A great vertical of Pommard Epenots domaine Parent proves the efficiency of slow ox method - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers , maybe it’s time to move onto a less controversial area of opinions to “prove”: biodynamie (and then move on to the cause of prem-ox).

In all seriousness, I do find it ironic that the wine epiphany of the “creator” (or at least namesake of) the “slow ox” method of aeration was created by something very different: leaving a half empty bottle of 50 year old wine unprotected overnight on Xmas eve to transform into heaven. I know:

Decanting: Is your carafe half full or half empty? - Great Lakes Wine & Spirits

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

how difficult would that be to do? I have no idea what an HPLC column is…sounds like a device…but…how difficult would it be to do…

and…how could you even test a bottle where the only thing that’s done is to remove the cork, without introducing other possible catalysts for chemical change, like removing the sample itself?

HPLC = High Pressure Liquid Chromatography
Its used to identify organic molecules in solution.

Your question is a corollary of the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle if I’m not mistaken:
The act of performing a test changes the thing being tested.
Good experimental design attempts to minimize unexpected influence, thereby improving the likelihood of generating valid data.

The scientific method could easily be applied to this question of wine enjoyment as a function of air time, although it appears to never have been so far.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Neither HPLC or GC are sensitive enough to actually measure composition of a mixture. They are used to separate gross phases of a solution. If you want real molecular composition, you need techniques that are much more sensitive, and able to provide chemical information, like Mass Spec, NMR, maybe IR spectrosopy.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Chris Foley:

The scientific method could easily be applied to this question of wine enjoyment as a function of air time, although it appears to never have been so far.

Not scientific, but a group of us were subjected to a blind flight of three wines, which turned out to be from 3 different bottles of the same wine. Poured in 3 laps around the large table, so the was significant time between the pours of wines A, B and C. Wine B just showed completely different and never normalized. A & C were confusingly similar, both evolving significantly over time, but never showing the same at the same time. The main difference seems to be they were about 5 minutes out of sync.

My point is that 70% of the time most people will enjoy a freshly opened bottle more than something opened five hours in advance. As Andy W points out, the reductive aspects of wine can usually be dealt with in a relatively short period. Tannins in bottled wine are not changed with aeration.

Of course it concerns a percentage of wines which behave like that but it is significant. I would say half and half.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Robert Sand:

The rest is subject to discussion, if you slow-ox, if you decant, double decant or watch the wine in the glass … for how long … all this is a matter of personal preference and we can argue about it – but that THERE IS a change in taste should be beyond any doubt (and this was the headline of this thread) – otherwise we don´t need any corks in our bottles!

Cleary, almost everyone is in agreement with this “mythology”.

A sub-issue/detour here is whether the “slow ox” method, ie, pulling that cork and leaving it for a few hours, in and of itself, does anything perceptible? and, how you would ever separate “slow ox’s” effect from the effect of pouring into the glass, etc. In any case, whether it does or doesn’t…whether there could be enough effect to change an ugly smelling wine into a beauty…in a wine of any age?

So…even if we are in agreement that “breathing” changes a wine, is “slow-ox” really “breathing”? That’s the issue that has intrigued me for 30+ years…though I just called it “popping the cork”, not “slow ox” for most of those years.

And…I would argue that the real reason the cork is there…rather than to preserve the wine from slow ox is to keep the liquid in and protect it from pests and evaporation. (I doubt the Romans or the Cistercians were too concerned with the effects of oxygen.)

(And, I ‘d say that even if Francois hasn’t “proven” that slow-ox actually improves a wine, for the reasons above, he has “proven” that decently significant “breathing” like he did with the Chambertin on Xmas Eve 1978 was beneficial to a 50+ year old wine. There is no other explanation for its tranformation and Francois’ epiphany.)

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Herwig
Yes, it is quite feasible that we’re debating different scenarios. If so, no surprise there are seemingly opposite opinions.

I do enjoy old wines, and am quite tolerant of over-mature wines - accepting some failings for the rare complexity. On the whole I ‘*pop and pour’, though sometimes am forced into decanting by a crumbling cork. Overall there are hits and misses, and not that many more hits than misses, so I’m open to trying an **alternative approach.

regards
Ian

  • This more down to lack of planning / focus on cooking the food, rather than an intentional strategy.
    ** That is, if I ever get myself organised to try it!

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked:
“and the time that it sits in the glass until tasting…can’t be eliminated as even more significant than the “slow ox” in affecting the wine”

Well, yes… if you just look at the glass for 15 minutes or more after pouring some wine into it. Personally, I pour wine into my glass when it is empty and I want more. Then immediately thereafter I satisfy myself with at least a small taste! And then, immediately I analyze the taste!

You could “argue” the same way about decanting. Does the mere act of pouring a wine into a decanter cause immediately the changes we associate with “decanting”? I don’t think that anyone would argue that… that no time is required for the changes to occur.

I think that there is also a significant different between “tasting” a wine versus leisurely drinking a fine mature wine over the course of a couple of hours. What I find is that my perceptions change somewhat too, in the sense that after a brief time I start to look, and taste, “deeper” into the character and flavors of the wine. I want more than the first, obvious flavors. The initial flavors are obvious from the start, but it is the better wines that deliver something special over the longer, several hour period.

Young or not-too-old wines will have freshness and flavor impact when first opened. I am not impressed by these characteristics!! What I am looking for is finesse, nuance, subtlety, compexity. These I find take time to develop and show themselves, and then too, usually only when the wine is at least semi-mature, and has the underlying quality to distinguish it from its peers!

I used to decant everything, and usually the wine was much better after about an hour. Then I read Francois’ accounts and tried not-decanting. With relatively mature Burgs I found that I greatly preferred not decanting. Of course, it takes more time and one has to plan ahead, and again, the results don’t hit you in the head - they are subtlety, nuance, complexity, and usually better freshness along with balance and drinkability! With younger, fuller bodied wines it can take 6, 8, or maybe 12 hours to see a good result. But I don’t drink those sorts of wines very often.

posts.name: Larry Archibald
posts.cooked:
Tom, when you did your experiment way back when, at a time dinosaurs roamed the earth, did you look at your results using a 95% confidence level rather than a 99% confidence level? Would the results have been any different?

99% is a ridiculously high standard. 95% is much more normal for this kind of an experiment, and usually indicative of a positive result. Still quite difficult to achieve.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Tom’s hypothesis appears to have been ‘aeration does not improve wines’.

Taking a statisticians view, I’d be asking the following questions:

  • is there a way of providing a control? The easy control is to put some duplicates into the tasting. Getting results for tasting exactly the same wines, would test the ability / likelihood of tasters mistakenly spotting a difference. The more useful one, but IMO impossible, is to put a control group in that has matched pairs where one wine is ‘better’ than the other. This tests the likelihood of a taster to pick a better wine (or not) when given matched pairs.

  • What were the wines? This is very pertinent to this thread, as the effect on old / ancient wines may well be quite different to that on new wines. In order to get a more useful test, focusing on similar wines can help remove another source of variation e.g, tannic young reds, or non-aromatic ‘mature’ 10 year old whites

  • Did the wines change with aeration? The test was whether the wines were improved, but not whether the wines were affected by aeration.

  • Were the wines tasted in pairs and a judgement made for each pair in turn? If so, this offers massive concerns for the results and the veracity of the statistical test - the tester has the opportunity to allow their predetermined views creep in, and this would IMO invalidate the statistical test. However if the wines had been tasted in a randomised order e.g, Wine 1 aerated, Wine 6 not aerated, Wine 2 not aerated, Wine 4 not aerated, Wine 5 aerated, Wine 1 aerated etc. with the tester scoring the wines without this knowledge, then the test would eliminate the risk of a tester biasing (subconsciously) the results.

regards
Ian

LarryA:

Tom, when you did your experiment way back when, at a time dinosaurs roamed the earth, did you look at your results using a 95% confidence level rather than a 99% confidence level? Would the results have been any different?

99% is a ridiculously high standard. 95% is much more normal for this kind of an experiment, and usually indicative of a positive result. Still quite difficult to achieve.

Actually, I don’t recall for sure if it was 99% or 95%. I’ll have to dig out the article. Maybe I’ll scan it & post it so people can totally tear apart my methodology. I’m not a rocket scientist, you know!!
Tom

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Several more comments:

1/Nobody has done any kind of experiment that proves that breathing a wine is always wonderful with anything approaching statistical significance. One can criticize the studies done WHICH SHOW BREATHING CAN BE COUNTERPRODUCTIVE as not being done to the highest standards of statistics 101.

2/My point would be to do a simple experiment and decide for yourself. Tom and I aren’t drinking the wine for you. Save your magnums of and '45 and '47 Romanee Conti for when Francois comes to dinner. He can measure the cesium for you…or is that Tom’s job??
3/Sometimes wines may not open up right away but come around hours later. But I have also seen wines flower in the first fifteen minutes and then fade.

4/Nobody is denying that oxygen impacts wine; the question is, when is this a good thing??

posts.name: DAVID GIBBS
posts.cooked:
I’ve got no horse in this race, but I do tend to open older bottles pretty much every day. Young ones too, for that matter:)
From my experience, I’ve yet to come across any wine ever that didn’t benefit from a little air. How long and how much is debatable, for sure, but I see it every single night.

And while this is in no way empirical, I’ve popped and poured and had people turn down a wine I was sure they’d love. Later, when they ask for another glass, I often pour them from the bottle they didn’t care for, and 9 times out of 10 they order it.
Could be the wine/food they’ve consumed, or it could be that it just needed to breathe a bit.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

1/Nobody has done any kind of experiment that proves that breathing a wine is always wonderful with anything approaching statistical significance. One can criticize the studies done WHICH SHOW BREATHING CAN BE COUNTERPRODUCTIVE as not being done to the highest standards of statistics 101.

Mel
Indeed that is true. I’ve encountered wines that blossomed, and wines that fell over within minutes, plus wines that were dead and poured down the sink (maybe too soon? too late to find out now!)

It’s not in my nature to have a firm ideology that I must attack Tom (or anyone else) to rubbish their claims, in order to satisfy my belief (not that I have firm views either way on this one, but do want to hear experiences). I have great respect for Tom. I’m interested in the process, as even if I might argue the stats test is not valid, it doesn’t change the observational experience.

The old cliché of ‘Lies damn lies and statistics’ is so often true, and it is crucial to understand the limitations and constraints of tests. So much of stats teaching is about learning the formulae, yet so little time is spent on understanding when they are appropriate and when not, or more usefully how to devise tests to avoid other (unmeasured) variables giving misleading results, or merely clouding the issues. Clarity of what the question / hypothesis being posed is also critical.

regards
Ian

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
As I have not a thick skin as Tom, I am a little sad that people do not listen to experience.

For more than 40 years I have tried to take profit of the situations that I have encountered. I have tried to see the conclusions of the experiments, trying to find what is good or not good, and I have become more and more convinced about what I have observed and used. And a majority does not want to listen to that.

When I participated to ateliers with Alain Senderens a three stars chef, he said that for the type of bread he used to accompany foie gras, the best toast has a thickness of 1.3 centimeter. I listened to him because he had certainly made many experiments. I did not tell him : are you sure of what you say, how many experiments have you made, what proves that you are right, did you make the experiments surely in the same conditions, have you statistical series which are significant, and so on.

He said it and I listened to him.

Some people who have never drunk a really old wine have theories about what should happen to old wines. They know better than me what I should experience.

In a way I feel sad to see that experience is not taken in account. Every wine that I serve is smelt at the moment of opening and at the moment it is served, and meantime. It happens that some wines have lost a part of the freshness, I did not need to calculate which percentage because the case of wine improving their perfume is extremely superior. And most important of all, wines which would be rejected as dead or sufficiently ill to be put away are brought to life as by a miracle by a certain time that I have measured by smelling repeatedly the wine without touching the bottle, which is between 4 and 5 hours.

Hundreds of time wines which would have been put away recover completely after 4 to five hours of slow oxygenation. Alain Senderens was the witness of a 1956 Romanée Conti which I opened and declared dead (it happens that I do not trust enough in my wines) which he smelt and which was served. He tasted it and he said at the whole table : “none of you can imagine that it is the same wine that François has opened 5 hours before”.

In my book published in 2003 I have written : “I am convinced that 50% of all the old wines which were put in the sink were proper wines”. Do not ask me to give statistics proving that it is 48% or 52%, but my experience has shown me that people exclude wines which are good and which would recover only because time of breathing makes miracles.

We are 13 years after my book and since then I have opened thousands and thousands of real old wines. And nothing has to be changed in what I have witnessed and observed. A 1874 Yquem that I had declared dead came back to life and I would not have bet on it. So miracles can be above my expectation.

I have heard hundreds of time the argument of the tiny surface which does not allow contact enough between the liquid and the air. This is irrelevant as experience shows that the recovery of a wine does not depend on the surface, because a molecule which is in the bottom of the bottle can go up whatever the surface is. I have no scientific argument but only experimental argument for that.
So many times, being late at home I open a bottle one hour before tasting and I see that the taste has not the balance that it would have with the proper time. And it is proved that the wine improves during the tasting which is not the case with the proper time between opening and drinking as the wine remains constantly balanced.

I have seen so many different situations that I feel confident in what I have observed. This statement is made for wines which I consider old. Let us say before 1970, the limit having no particular signification. Of course one could say : all what you tell depends on your own taste which is different from others. But I listen to the reactions of people who share wine with me and I would certainly have changed my theories if my reactions would be different from the ones of people who share wine with me.

For young wines, I do not pretend anything because the differences are small. For my taste, for the wines after 1988 (limit with no specific signification) I prefer pop and pour to follow the enlargement of the wine during the time I drink it. But depending on the wine, decanting or slow oxygenation of 10 hours can have their advantages for young wines.

It is only on really old wines that I suggest that my experience has a real value, as I have not been blocked in a unique protocol. I have tested so many variant models that I trust in the purity of the method : opening 4 to 5 hours before, no enlargement of the surface, bottle standing at the proper temperature, and service from the bottle with taking care of the service in order to not develop the sediment. Point.

And I am extremely sad that it is not received and that some people recommend a statistical approach which is impossible. If I open two bottles of the same wine of 1929, the bottles will be completely different and it is impossible to compare the one slow oxygenated and the one pop and poured.

Of course I have not to take things so personal as it has no importance, but after 40 years of tries and tries to see if the method I use is proper, the fact that no care is taken for the result of my experience is really frustrating as I have not a thick skin.

Of course I do not want an approval only because I would be sad. I would like that some people listen to what I say which is the fruit of experience. Negation by people who have never drunk wines before 1945 (limit not significant) or so rarely, hurts me.

For wines after 1970, statistical experiments can be made by counting results of a big group of tasters. But for old wines I have the weakness to say that it would not be bad to listen to the ones who have opened the wines and tested every possible methods.

If a chef tells me that to keep the taste of cooked potatoes, I should proceed in a certain way, I would be inclined to listen to him if my experience is smaller than his one.

Sorry to have such a reaction but I am a sentimental and passionate person who wants to let the world of old wines be better understood because I have found or applied a method which works to help them to be loved.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Francois,

Let us not forget that the way wine is made has changed dramatically since the days of the Marshall Plan. Remember when stainless steel was considered radical?? Temperature control during fermentation?? Nowadays people bubble oxygen into the wine during fermentation, rack into barrel for malo. In Italy we have seen a dramatic revolution in a very shot time.

My point is that the potatoes have changed!

I have probably drunk 80 to 100 wines made before 1945 and will be lucky to drink five or ten more.
But I will drink a lot more of wines made since 1980.

And for those of you who have messaged me about '47 DRC: I’m glad you got the joke.

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked:
Francois,
Do not be sad or frustrated! I’m pretty sure it is safe to say that everyone here, myself included, does not regularly drink the sort of old wines that you do. With even somewhat younger wines the style can vary so much that experiences with them will vary accordingly. I always judge the style and “drinkability” of a wine by carefully considering beforehand the style of the vintage, and the style of the producer, as well as the overall age of the wine. Then I am able to “guess” about the minmum amount of air time the wine will need, or whether it will be just an experiment in frustration to try to coax a fine performance from a young wine, even an expensive one! But I wholehartedly agree with you when it comes to wines with sufficient age, wines with sufficient “delicacy” and maturity. I have also witnessed, entirely to my surprise, old wines that I considered absolutely dead, only to have them miraculously come back to very attractive life 6, 8, or more hours later! But most of my experience is with Burgundy, which is inherently lighter bodied and more delicate than most other varietals, and also with potentially so much finesse and complexity, that it is no surprise to me that they respond so beautifully with your approach to gentle aeration!

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked: Just to add something… with older or sufficiently delicate younger Burgs, I find that a lot of what I value in them is lost when the wine is decanted. Sure, some wines are so aggressive when young that a decant to tone them down may be desirable. But with a delicate Burg. decanting can soften it too much, so that delicacy, finesse, freshness, and complexity is compromised. I have not experimented with various scenarios with breathing time in the bottle, then decanting immediately before serving. That is, when the usual wait time is not given with the wine in decanter. However, as I am not bothered by sediment in long-cellared bottles, I never use that approach…

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
What do you make of Francois’ epiphany experience, then, Paul…described above.

It would be good that I tell again the way I discovered the Audouze method.

It is probably in 1978 + or minus one year I do not remember exactly. For the dinner of December 24th I had chosen in my cellar a Chambertin 1929 and I do not remember the domaine.
My wife does not drink, my kids were very young, and I was the only one to drink.
I drank half of the bottle which was extremely good. I left the remaining standing in the cellar for the Christmas lunch.

When I took the bottle in the cellar to bring it in the dining room I smelt the wine and it was a miracle. The perfume was largely better and the taste was largely better too.
It was as if a wine having 96 points would have been transformed in a wine having 98 or 99 points.
I was so moved than I invited friends to leave their family and come to share a sip of this wine. Three of my friends living in the vicinity left their family and toasted, sharing with me this miracle.

It was then natural to ask myself: what happened. This was not the slow ox method but the power of air to enlarge the wine.

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked: I don’t understand your question? That an older Chambertin could improve overnight? It wasn’t decanted btw, though even decanting won’t negatively affect “every” older Burg. Chambertin is perhaps the most sturdy of Burgs too!

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked:
Well, I taste my mature Burgs initially … and they always improve later on!!

I’m sure Francois too, early on, tasted some of the initially bad smelling wines and found them, well, disagreeable. After all, can you enjoy a wine whose taste is “OK”, but which smells bad!

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Concerning smelling the wine, I have trained my nose to figure what can happen to a wine from opening to service. Of course I can make mistakes, but I want to do it again and again to improve my judgement on wines only by the smell.

I make more pessimistic mistakes announcing dead a wine which will come back to life than the contrary, imagining a recovery which does not occur.

And as such a guess has not any consequence, as the wine is opened and as nothing will change its destiny which is to be either drunk or rejected, I like to train again and again the judgement by the smell.

I am smelling the 1916 Pontet Canet in Bern’s cellar.

Paul thank you for your nice words and for the message which I approve.

Drink the best !

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
This has been a very interesting thread, and I’ve learned some things, although I’m not sure I going to change the way I’ve learned to handle wines over my 40 years of wine drinking.

Perhaps I’ve missed it in the long discussions, but I think a significant variable in this entire process that really hasn’t been discussed at all is the likely wide variation in what happens once the wine is in the glass. When someone states that they pop and pour and let the wine air and open up in the glass and that decanting is unnecessary, what does that mean? do they pour 2 ounces and swirl and enjoy that wine over five minutes and pour some more, or do they pour 6-8 ounces and slowly nurse that glass over an hour? Do they typically nurse a bottle over 4 hours, or do they pour a bottle at a social gathering where everyone gets one glass and the bottle is done? This could account for some of the disagreement about whether decanting is of value.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: A fair point John. For me it’s generally a smaller pour, topped up later.

posts.name: Emily Richer
posts.cooked:
Berserkers, we know better than to look for one-size-fits-all theorems. “All wines benefit from decanting” is an insane statement. “Aerating wines to open them up is either a Myth or it’s not?” BAM, you’re ejected from the booster seat…

We know wine has an important relationship with O2. We know that each vintner/winemaker has a style/target (setting aside whether they hit that target), impacting at what point you would want to aerate/decant and for how long. We also know that’s a moving target for obvious reasons as a wine ages.

I used to joke in this forum that I would print a panty-hose chart on my back-label advising time in decanter on vertical axis with time in bottle on horizontal, with a nice downward sloping curve…

Remember, a wine built to age (ABSOLUTELY these are typically wines with good acid balance, low acid wines don’t age well) is going to benefit and develop from slow oxygenation coming through the cork over years and years. You can accelerate this aging process by opening the bottle Then you can accelerate further by rolling the bottle around on the counter after you’ve poured a bit out to get O2 beyond the nickel-sized opening in the neck, then leaving the bottle open awhile, and accelerate aging even further by splashing the wine around (I like fanning young wines down the side of the decanter, or coffee pourer, or measuring cup, whatever you’re using), or if you’re really feeling aggressive, put it through that fun-sounding aerator gizmo. I love it when people joke about using a burr mixer or blender. Though I doubt that’s a good idea - some shear likely to occur…

If you buy a cheap young red wine, bottled at high pH/low acid, fruity, given plenty O2 exposure in the winery during the production process, then you’ve purchased a ready-to-drink wine, and you better drink that puppy right from the bottle, down the hatch in an hour over dinner because that’s what the vintner intended for your $20ish bottle of Cab. It’s gonna fall apart if left to day 2. They bottled it for you at its peak. Grab and go.

If you have a complex, interesting, discussion-worthy age-able wine, you might need “3 days on the counter or 3 hours in decanter” like I used to say about my 2007 on release, or you might, after cellaring it for 5-6 years, find it wee tight on opening, but gains weight (my fave expression I learned from King Cab Mike Pobega himself) gets deep and dark and rich in 20 minutes in the glass, flavors just bursting out from within and blowing your mind Imagine the flavors and textures laid down in the liquid so flat and tight together that your tongue cannot get at them, all you taste at first is the acidity of the liquid surrounding them, and some of the tannin structure poking out of those tightly-stacked and packed flavor molecules. Now you introduce O2, and it weaves through and opens and separates and allows your taste buds access to all kinds of yummyness that balances out the acidity and alcohol in the wine. (I also like the metaphor of a memory foam mattress topper: every buy one? Comes all hard and flat in the box, rolled up tight, so difficult to peel open then you let it sit for 48-96 hours and O2 weaves into the cells and it rounds up and becomes soft and plushy.) Back to the acid envelope (as I describe it) you experience on a just-opened wine: you want and need acid, but not alone! That’s why splash-racking a white, quickly so it stays cold, is good advice by that Santa Monica somm. I do it on my rose’ - totally needed when young, less and less so as it opens up in bottle over a year and more.

One comment above said EVERY BOTTLE is different; oh now that’s overwhelming - don’t give up. Because that’s kinda splitting hairs vs the difference in producer intention and vintage. Of course, I do agree that the further away from bottling date (where conditions were virtually the same for every bottle coming off the line), the more bottle variation potential does exist in a single vintage by a single producer. So wines from 1961 stored in various ways, more of a crapshoot. Wines shipped direct from a producer warehouse where they’ve aged side-by-side and only differ in their shipping and storage environment of end user, more likely to be similar.

That Decanter article is frightening not only in its oversimplification from an organization that should know better, but that they were decanting 30-year olds to aerate them. Ack! You all would only carefully decant a 30-year old wine for sediment.

Lemme add: one of the ways we vintners can tell someone who “gets wine” from someone who hasn’t yet is they always buy more than one bottle if they think the wine is going to be one they’ll like. So they can learn where its at on the first one–and in a community like this, share the knowledge to help others plan ahead. Good tasting notes always give the vintage, the date tasted, and whether allowed to open up or not, by what method, and how long.

If you ever taste a wine totally “reduced” as we say in the cellar from lack of O2 exposure (to protect it from aging prematurely for a wine expected to age and develop character in bottle) and then taste it after racking (kinda like decanting but macro scale), well, you know there’s no myth about wine having a PROFOUND relationship with O2.

That said, I have customers who suffer the “good is the enemy of great” pitfall. They say “Oh, Emily you always tell us to decant, but we don’t, I’m sorry, and we love your wines.” Mmmm, too bad, cuz a little patience and oh, so incredible, c’est la vie…

Lastly, if O2 had no effect, you could keep an open bottle on the counter for weeks and it wouldn’t oxidize into yuckyness, eh?

Class over. To summarize:

  1. Did the vintner build it to age?
  2. Where are we in that continuum?
  3. How do YOU like your wines? i.e., where do your preferences fit along the continuum.

Keep experimenting. Keep notes. I guarantee anyone opens a bottle of my wine - and tastes it right away, then pours half into a decanter, fanning down the sides for max O2 exposure, then gas the other half (I use Private Preserve, argon), leaving it standing upright on the counter next to the decanter, maybe even pour some of the decanted wine into a glass and swirl that every 15 mins to represent hyperO2 version. (Even better if you could siphon to decanter from the standing bottle because the act of pouring out half gave the other half a nice gulp of air…) Taste all three an hour later.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Emily Richer:

they always buy more than one bottle if they think the wine is going to be one they’ll like. So they can learn where its at on the first one-

Shazam! You don’t know until you know. Exactly why I try to buy an odd bottle extra. 3, 6, or 12 to rack and the extra as a test bottle for a few years down the road when you suspect it’s getting close to the drinking window. Or grab a 375ml as your guinea pig.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Great post Emily. The one size fits all stance is just as flawed on either side of the debate.

I applaud those who have done the blind tasting experiments. Your conclusions are less subject to bias. But they are valid only for the wines you studied under the conditions you imposed. There is no reason to believe that the results are generalizable to a more diverse group of wines, conditions or tasters.

Emily Richer:

Berserkers, we know better than to look for one-size-fits-all theorems. “All wines benefit from decanting” is an insane statement. “Aerating wines to open them up is either a Myth or it’s not?” BAM, you’re ejected from the booster seat…

We know wine has an important relationship with O2. We know that each vintner/winemaker has a style/target (setting aside whether they hit that target), impacting at what point you would want to aerate/decant and for how long. We also know that’s a moving target for obvious reasons as a wine ages.

I used to joke in this forum that I would print a panty-hose chart on my back-label advising time in decanter on vertical axis with time in bottle on horizontal, with a nice downward sloping curve…

Remember, a wine built to age (ABSOLUTELY these are typically wines with good acid balance, low acid wines don’t age well) is going to benefit and develop from slow oxygenation coming through the cork over years and years. You can accelerate this aging process by opening the bottle Then you can accelerate further by rolling the bottle around on the counter after you’ve poured a bit out to get O2 beyond the nickel-sized opening in the neck, then leaving the bottle open awhile, and accelerate aging even further by splashing the wine around (I like fanning young wines down the side of the decanter, or coffee pourer, or measuring cup, whatever you’re using), or if you’re really feeling aggressive, put it through that fun-sounding aerator gizmo. I love it when people joke about using a burr mixer or blender. Though I doubt that’s a good idea - some shear likely to occur…

If you buy a cheap young red wine, bottled at high pH/low acid, fruity, given plenty O2 exposure in the winery during the production process, then you’ve purchased a ready-to-drink wine, and you better drink that puppy right from the bottle, down the hatch in an hour over dinner because that’s what the vintner intended for your $20ish bottle of Cab. It’s gonna fall apart if left to day 2. They bottled it for you at its peak. Grab and go.

If you have a complex, interesting, discussion-worthy age-able wine, you might need “3 days on the counter or 3 hours in decanter” like I used to say about my 2007 on release, or you might, after cellaring it for 5-6 years, find it wee tight on opening, but gains weight (my fave expression I learned from King Cab Mike Pobega himself) gets deep and dark and rich in 20 minutes in the glass, flavors just bursting out from within and blowing your mind Imagine the flavors and textures laid down in the liquid so flat and tight together that your tongue cannot get at them, all you taste at first is the acidity of the liquid surrounding them, and some of the tannin structure poking out of those tightly-stacked and packed flavor molecules. Now you introduce O2, and it weaves through and opens and separates and allows your taste buds access to all kinds of yummyness that balances out the acidity and alcohol in the wine. (I also like the metaphor of a memory foam mattress topper: every buy one? Comes all hard and flat in the box, rolled up tight, so difficult to peel open then you let it sit for 48-96 hours and O2 weaves into the cells and it rounds up and becomes soft and plushy.) Back to the acid envelope (as I describe it) you experience on a just-opened wine: you want and need acid, but not alone! That’s why splash-racking a white, quickly so it stays cold, is good advice by that Santa Monica somm. I do it on my rose’ - totally needed when young, less and less so as it opens up in bottle over a year and more.

One comment above said EVERY BOTTLE is different; oh now that’s overwhelming - don’t give up. Because that’s kinda splitting hairs vs the difference in producer intention and vintage. Of course, I do agree that the further away from bottling date (where conditions were virtually the same for every bottle coming off the line), the more bottle variation potential does exist in a single vintage by a single producer. So wines from 1961 stored in various ways, more of a crapshoot. Wines shipped direct from a producer warehouse where they’ve aged side-by-side and only differ in their shipping and storage environment of end user, more likely to be similar.

That Decanter article is frightening not only in its oversimplification from an organization that should know better, but that they were decanting 30-year olds to aerate them. Ack! You all would only carefully decant a 30-year old wine for sediment.

Lemme add: one of the ways we vintners can tell someone who “gets wine” from someone who hasn’t yet is they always buy more than one bottle if they think the wine is going to be one they’ll like. So they can learn where its at on the first one–and in a community like this, share the knowledge to help others plan ahead. Good tasting notes always give the vintage, the date tasted, and whether allowed to open up or not, by what method, and how long.

If you ever taste a wine totally “reduced” as we say in the cellar from lack of O2 exposure (to protect it from aging prematurely for a wine expected to age and develop character in bottle) and then taste it after racking (kinda like decanting but macro scale), well, you know there’s no myth about wine having a PROFOUND relationship with O2.

That said, I have customers who suffer the “good is the enemy of great” pitfall. They say “Oh, Emily you always tell us to decant, but we don’t, I’m sorry, and we love your wines.” Mmmm, too bad, cuz a little patience and oh, so incredible, c’est la vie…

Lastly, if O2 had no effect, you could keep an open bottle on the counter for weeks and it wouldn’t oxidize into yuckyness, eh?

Class over. To summarize:

  1. Did the vintner build it to age?
  2. Where are we in that continuum?
  3. How do YOU like your wines? i.e., where do your preferences fit along the continuum.

Keep experimenting. Keep notes. I guarantee anyone opens a bottle of my wine - and tastes it right away, then pours half into a decanter, fanning down the sides for max O2 exposure, then gas the other half (I use Private Preserve, argon), leaving it standing upright on the counter next to the decanter, maybe even pour some of the decanted wine into a glass and swirl that every 15 mins to represent hyperO2 version. (Even better if you could siphon to decanter from the standing bottle because the act of pouring out half gave the other half a nice gulp of air…) Taste all three an hour later.

Emily,
Nicely written response…with which I find very little w/ which to quibble. A few comments I might add:

  1. Reductive wines: I generally regard the smell of reduction in a young wine as a good sign that the wine will age well. Going back before your time, I often used the term the “Burnap stench” and the
    “Rasmussen stench” because those wines (SantaCruzMtnVnyds and KentRasmussenWnry), upon release, often showed a burnt rubber/funky/fecal stench to them. But, with age, they invariably
    lost that stench and turned into a beautiful wine, even their Chards.

  2. I don’t think anybody claims that air/O2 has no effect on a wine. What many people think when a wine is exposed to air/O2, that all sorts of chemical/oxidation reactions immediately start
    shredding the wines to pieces. I claim the the oxidation reaction rates in young wines is so small that oxidation plays an insignificant role. What are the products of oxidation?? Browning in
    white wines and acetification and formation of sediment. You can leave a half opened btl of young Chard on the sideboard for several weeks and there will not be any observable browning. You can leave
    a hlf opened btl of young Syrah for several weeks on the sideboard and there will be no noticible browning nor precipitation of sediment. You can leave a half-opened btl, white or red, sitting on the sideboard
    and it’s not going to “oxidize into yuckiness”. That said, it’s not going to be a very good wine, but probably not yucky…just not very drinkable. My feeling is that it’s merely the loss of fruit & volatiles
    into the headspace that makes it not very good. No science behind that claim…just my suspicions.
    For an old wine, 'tis a whole nuther matter. The wine is highly reduced from all that time in the btl and then the oxidation reaction rates are much faster. Decanting to take it off the sediment…sure.
    But decanting it to “breathe” to improve…then it becomes sort of a crap-shoot. Sometimes it improves, sometimes it’s all downhill. I’ve left 20 yr old Zins that have good/decent color, maybe a slight
    bricking, in the decanter on the sideboard (ahhhhh…you should see what all’s in my sideboard!!). I go back to it in a few hrs or the next morning and it’s turned an ugly/murkey brown color. Often there will
    have sediment appearing in the bottom of the decanter. Clearly…that’s oxidation.
    But my feeling is that the changing of a wine after being exposed to air/O2 is not oxidation, but merely a loss of volatiles into the air or headspace. Again…no science behind that conjecture.

  3. Related to that discussion is “preserving” a half consumed btl. You endorse the use of PrivateReserve (argon) gas. Others swear to the efficacy of the VacuVin for preserving the wine. If my assertion
    that oxidation plays no/minor role in the deterioration of the half-opened wine, then both systems are useless in terms of preserving the wine. If my assertion that loss of volatiles into the headspace
    is the primary reason for the deterioration of the wine, then neither PrivateReserve or VacuVin is going to make a bit of difference. Well, maybe the PrivateReserve has a (very) slight edge because the
    VacuVin may be sucking the volatiles out of the wine into the evacuated headspace…but the VacuVin vacuum is so weak and the seal so poor that you won’t see a difference.
    My group, yrs ago, did the experiment: Three btls of the same wine opened and half poured out. Apply PrivateReserve, VacuVin, and nothing (air headspace). Left for two days in that state. Blind tasting
    the three. On the 95% confidence level, my people were not able to tell a difference in the three samples. That, primarily, is the basis of my belief that it’s the loss of volatiles and not oxidation that
    is responsible for the deterioration. Rigorous science?? Obviously not…but better data than most people have to work with…which is just believe what the wine authorities tell us to be true.

So…I have a btl of Ronchi di Cialla Schioppettino ‘07 that I’m getting ready to crack. Decant/breathe or don’t decant?? I have no freakin’ clue. So, assuming no sediment (it probably doesn’t have much,
if any), my standard procedure is simply to PnP and drink the wine over several hrs (unless it’s so friggin’ good that it’s gone within the hour). Do I miss out on some greater glories of some wines by that
procedure?? I have no doubt that I do in some cases. So be it, I guess.
Tom

(Even better if you could siphon to decanter from the standing bottle because the act of pouring out half gave the other half a nice gulp of air…) Taste all three an hour later.

Nice points but… I do not agree. In my 30 years of wine drinking and holding wine tastings, P&P wines were consistently better/rated higher than the exact same wine decanted. I’m talking about an extremely large sample size with high end wines(meant to age) with serious and professional tasters. In theory, one may choose to decant and give perfectly reasonable explanations to why a wine should be better, but in reality (actual tastings over 30 years) that is, in most cases, simply not the case. Emperor, clothes, etc…

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

Nice points but… I do not agree. In my 30 years of wine drinking and holding wine tastings, P&P wines were consistently better/rated higher than the exact same wine decanted. I’m talking about an extremely large sample size with high end wines(meant to age) with serious and professional tasters. In theory, one may choose to decant and give perfectly reasonable explanations to why a wine should be better, but in reality (actual tastings over 30 years) that is, in most cases, simply not the case. Emperor, clothes, etc…

That’s your particular reality. If Obi Wan Kenobi were a wine drinker, he’d say “…many of the truths we cling to depend greatly on our own point of view… and our own palate”.

I’ve had two bottles of the same wine side by side several times - one decanted, one pop ‘n’ pour - and the decanted bottle tasted better than the p&p bottle. It wasn’t just me who thought so either. Everyone at any of those dinners agreed. Twice it was young Cabs, twice it was aged Burgundy. I’m sure you’ll counter with bottle variation. I won’t be tossing out my decanters based on that though.

This could go on & on & on & on. You believe what you believe; I believe what I believe. Neither side will change either’s opinion.

link_counts.title: What do you know about assessing wine quality?

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
I think it also depends on what one is looking for: a quick rush with pop and pour that can dissipate pretty quickly, but is nice for a while (of course pop and pour always risks sediment in wines where you don’t want sediment). Aeration at a tasting…can make a wine seem developed and more complex. But, aeration is tricky stuff to get “right”.

And, if it with really young wines (“meant to age”)…I’m not sure it matters much which method you use. Not a criticism…just reality: no development to coax out by aerating; no likely sediment needing to be cleaned.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked:
What about decanting practices, which can vary widely? Splash decant, double/triple/quad decant, hyperdecanting, Vinturi…so many variables.
My take: this is part of what makes wine fun and engaging. It can be interesting to see what happens to wines under various circumstances, and that’s intriguing by itself.
FWIW, though, I’ve never regretted decanting young wines. Double-decanted a couple of young Syrahs 8 hours in advance this weekend, both were vastly improved.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

Nice points but… I do not agree. In my 30 years of wine drinking and holding wine tastings, P&P wines were consistently better/rated higher than the exact same wine decanted. I’m talking about an extremely large sample size with high end wines(meant to age) with serious and professional tasters. In theory, one may choose to decant and give perfectly reasonable explanations to why a wine should be better, but in reality (actual tastings over 30 years) that is, in most cases, simply not the case. Emperor, clothes, etc…

I am incredulous.

I find it an obvious truth that, for me and my tasting group, wines evolve in the glass, typically for the better.

Nothing to do with emperor’s clothing.

Do your tasters bother to swirl? If so, does it do harm?

For you, through the course of an evening, you find the wines you drink consistently decline as the bottle is open?

What is your usual time window from the P & P goodness to the decline in rating? Seconds, minutes, hours?

I may be incredulous, but still interested in your experience!

I would love to see a rating curve of your experience and watch the negative change you notice!

posts.name: John Davis
posts.cooked:
Some people are convinced that they’re right and with issues like this no one is changing anyone’s mind. If you think it helps (I do to a great extent) then obviously you’re going to look at the wine more favorably. If you don’t think aeration is that big of deal you aren’t going to care. Geeks can argue over this issue as long as they want but rarely do you see anyone’s mind get changed.

JD

posts.name: John D. Zuccarino
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

The world of fine wine, as a matter of fact, is full of myths or mistaken notions, often passing for gospel. Take the idea of letting a wine “breathe” – that is to say, opening a bottle or decanting it into another container long before drinking it, ostensibly to allow it to “improve” with the exposure to oxygen. There is indeed many a wine expert (especially wine boors) who would never dream of enjoying a good bottle of red wine unless it has been given a chance to “breathe” for at least an hour.

In a December 1997 issue of Decanter – a highly respected wine periodical published in the U.K., which also happens to bill itself as “The World’s Best Wine Magazine” (and many wine experts would not disagree) – there was a report on a blind tasting involving six of the world’s most discriminating wine judges. These judges included Hugh Johnson (the author of more best selling wine books than anyone), Steven Spurrier (the original organizer of the infamous “Paris Tasting” of 1976), Serena Sutcliffe (a Master of Wine), and Patrick Léon (a famous winemaker for several Bordeaux châteaux, such as Mouton-Rothschild).

In this tasting, the esteemed judges were asked to assess the quality of several red wines from France’s classic Bordeaux region (including 1961 Mouton-Rothschild, a 1982 Clerc-Milon, a 1980 d’Armailhac, and a 1990 Mouton-Cadet) that were

Uncorked a few minutes ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked a few hours ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few minutes ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few hours ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked, and then immediately poured into glasses and tasted (that is, no “breathing” at all).

For which wines, across the board, did these wine judges ultimately show the most preference? You guessed it: the wines that were uncorked, immediately poured and tasted. It turns out that “breathing” – whether for a few minutes, or a few hours – doesn’t really “improve” wine at all. At least not according to this stellar panel.

Lodi Wine Commission - Blog - Does price (and letting wines “breathe”) matter?

I will say this that wine judges almost never like the wine decanted … it’s how they judge wines… a big frustration when you enter wine competitions is the wines are sampled with no decanting … so every award winning wine was most likely judged without decanting …

I did an experiment with two wines on an International wine comp to prove my point … same wine one wins Gold the other does not even score bronze because it was decanted…

Just because wine judges deemed the wines better does not mean the consumer will agree… sure there will be wine people who like ‘tension’ on the palate … this ‘tension’ or tightness is an indicator that the wines will open up…

so what does a wine do that opens up ???

For specifics… a sparking wine has about 4,000 mg/l of CO2 thus making it sparkling …

Still red wines have a value of 2,000 mg/l CO2

CO2 in wine is a carboxylic acid …

Decarboxylation is a chemical reaction that removes a carboxyl group and releases carbon dioxide (CO2).

So stop the presses ::: Decanting does what ??? Decarboxylation …

So now what does decanting do ??? makes the wine smoother via science … and the consumers generally love balance … to achieve balance one must decant most young red wines to have the best flavor profile, unless you’re apparently a wine judge …

When we Decant and how much CO2 is dissipated ??? 200 to 300 mg/l on a macro splash decant and this renders the wine less acidic , thus a smoother mouth-feel …now is this better for the taster ? subjective…that said 70% of my consumers like a decanted wine but only 25% will decant them for whatever reason… this is an informal study over a decade that I am still gathering info on …


As for the article … taken with their perspective and with a grain of salt

Salute !!!

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
This is such a big topic that I am surprised wine bars etc don’t do more experiments on this.

Everyone always says, Well, I’ve been drinking wine since first growths sold for $40 a case and…


My experiments:

1/certainly have not shown that as much as 50% of all wines improve with aeration
2/that peoples’ predictions (Barolo, funky Burgundies will do better with aeration) are usually wrong

It’s hard to prove that 100% of all wines do not improve with aeration. But i think it s like stealing bases in baseball. It should be at least successful more than 2/3 of the time. I see no proof of that.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

Nice points but… I do not agree. In my 30 years of wine drinking and holding wine tastings, P&P wines were consistently better/rated higher than the exact same wine decanted. I’m talking about an extremely large sample size with high end wines(meant to age) with serious and professional tasters. In theory, one may choose to decant and give perfectly reasonable explanations to why a wine should be better, but in reality (actual tastings over 30 years) that is, in most cases, simply not the case. Emperor, clothes, etc…

Glad you started this thread which I hadn’t seen until now. I am among the few (judging by the posts here) who are 100 percent with you with regard to this issue as far as my own personal taste goes. And it does not cease to amaze me how different the predominant view of the matter, as manifested on this board, tends to be. Many people here treat wine in a manner that I personally find completely destructive.

I learnt the latter the hard way. When I first started to read about what people with greater wine experience than my own tended to do prior to drinking, I too started to decant certain wines, particularly serious but relatively young red wines, hours before drinking them. To my disappointment, that didn’t improve them at all. On the contrary, I had several wines destroyed (to my perception) by having them sit too long in the decanter (although this was never longer than three hours and usually less than that).

I should have known better than trying since I was already aware at that point that I tended to like a wine less if it came from a half-empty bottle that had been left alone for a day or two (even if in the fridge) than if it came from a bottle that had just been opened. By now, I also know exactly what the problem is. When the wine is given an oxygen shock, as it is by being poured into a vessel of some kind (more than by just sitting in a vessel with a big air-to-wine surface, such as a decanter), the oxygen rather quickly (within a few hours) attacks the alcohol (rather than the tannins), which in turn yields perceptible amounts of acetaldehyde (sometimes described as reminiscent of the smell of rotting apple). I also know by now (e.g., by comparing with fellow tasters, including some wine producers who have served what I consider to be defect wines after first smelling the wine from the open bottle themselves to ensure that it is still in order) that I am far more sensitive than the average person to this smell and that I absolutely detest it.

Before I understood that it is the act of pouring the wine more than the surface exposure to oxygen that causes the problem, I also tried to preserve the wine left in a half-empty bottle by pouring it into a smaller bottle so that virtually no air was left on top and then capping the bottle properly and putting it in the fridge. For reasons that are obvious to me by now, this didn’t help much even though I took care to poor very slowly and gently. Trying to create a vacuum in the bottle by pumping the air out via a rubber cap doesn’t help either. The air seeps back in rather quickly and starts destroying the wine although perhaps more slowly than if it has been poured. What does work is what I now do, i.e., fill the half-empty bottle with inert gas (more specifically air without oxygen, sold as a spray can under the name of http://www.privatepreserve.com/Private Preserve) and then cork/cap it. This keeps the wine fresh, at least if it’s in the fridge, for at least a week, perhaps longer.

Obviously, it is harder to judge the perceptions of others rather than my own, particularly since I have strong reasons to think that I am more sensitive to acetaldehyde than the average person. But as far as I can tell at this point, the hard evidence (blind tests obviously; nothing else really counts as far as I am concerned) suggests that the true (physical as opposed to psychological) effect of oxygen shocks is negative for most people, just as you point out. By implication, the fact that many people seem to enjoy the wine more after exposing them to such shocks must to a large extent be a matter of placebo effects. At the same time, there is of course nothing wrong with such effects as long as it is pure enjoyment we are talking about.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Anton D:

Do your tasters bother to swirl? If so, does it do harm?

In my perception, swirling does not harm, at least not in the relatively short time it usually takes to empty a glass. But I certainly do not swirl in order to increase the exposure to oxygen. I swirl to increase the ability to smell the wine.

Anton D:

For you, through the course of an evening, you find the wines you drink consistently decline as the bottle is open?

No, it doesn’t. The exposure to oxygen along with the time it usually takes to consume the bottle is fortunately not sufficient, as a rule, to harm the wine to any noticeable degree as long as the wine that has not yet been served remains in the bottle.

Anton D:

What is your usual time window from the P & P goodness to the decline in rating? Seconds, minutes, hours?

Depends on the wine as well as the manner in which it is treated. As long as the wine stays in the bottle and is consumed at a normal pace, the wine has to be particularly sensitive in order for the deterioration to be perceptible. I have encountered wines that did deteriorate under those conditions, but only very few. If you decant, by contrast, then the risk increases.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
John D. Zuccarino:

For specifics… a sparking wine has about 4,000 mg/l of CO2 thus making it sparkling …

Still red wines have a value of 2,000 mg/l CO2

2,000 mg/l is above the threshold when a wine starts to feel like it is slightly sparkling. A normal value for a still red wine is more like 400-500 mg/l. See here:

http://www.winewisdom.com/articles/techie/role-of-carbon-dioxide-in-still-wines/

Since CO2 is very weakly acidic, I doubt that any release of it as a result of decanting will noticeably affect the perception of the wine unless the initial amount is excessive (for a still red wine).

However, if there is so much CO2 that wine is indeed slightly prickling, and this is not a desirable feature (when it appears in ordinary red wine, it is usually to be regarded as a fault), decanting may help to get rid of it. However, just opening the bottle some time in advance will help us well, so there really is no need to decant for this purpose. The same applies to certain other unwanted more or less unwanted gaslike compunds, e.g., reductive smells, which may, at least under fortunate circumstances, be reduced if the bottle is opened some time prior to serving.

link_counts.title: Role of carbon dioxide in still wines : WineWisdom
