posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Ed Kurtzman:

Breathing definitely helps premoxed whites. An hour or so in a decanter can “freshen up” a premoxed white, maybe not to where it should be, but can make an undrinkable wine at least somewhat enjoyable.

I have a very hard time believing that a prematurely oxidized wine would somehow recover from that problem by being exposed to still more oxygen and I have never encountered any indication that it would

Ed Kurtzman:

Now that the breathing myth is busted, I wonder if anyone has ever done a study on the myth of the wet cork. That’s the one where people look at me like I’m from outer space when I bring it up, but I don’t believe bottles need to be on their sides or upside down while aging. In the tens of thousands of bottles I’ve opened, I’ve yet to see a dry cork that was so dry it came out of the bottle easily. Just the opposite. You could push a completely soaked cork into the bottle with your pinkie, but a dry cork keeps wine in the bottle and air out better than a wet cork.

Yes, there has been scientific studies about this at a research institute in Australia. Sorry, but I no longer have the link to it and don’t know if it remains on line. But the main result was that storing the wine with the bottles lying down rather than standing up did reduce the risk of oxidation. However, the researcher himself discarded the hypothesis that this is because the cork needs to be kept wet in order to prevent it from drying out. That idea doesn’t hold up for several reasons. First, the cork hardly absorbs any liquid at all. If it did, it wouldn’t work for the purpose it is used. The liquid would slowly diffuse through the cork. Second, even with the bottle standing up, the cork sits about one centimeter from a liquid surface. Obviously, there will be enough water molecules above that surface for the cork to absorb any moisture it might need in order not to dry out.

On the other hand, there are several other ways in which laying the bottles down may help. One hypothesis suggested by the researcher in question is that if the wine is standing up, the oxygen molecules that manage to pass through the cork or on its sides hit the head space rather than the wine itself. This helps the aerobic acetobacter, sitting on the surface of the wine and responsible for the transformation of alcohol into acetic acid, to do their job. If the oxygen that enters the bottle hits the wine directly rather than via the head space, it is another story. Another possibility that, IIRC, was mentioned by the researcher in question is that the oxygen entry itself is reduced if it first hits a liquid rather than the gas in the head space.

Before encountering this piece of research, I was just as sceptical as you about the advantage of having the bottles lying rather than standing up. Since then, however, I do keep them lying, or, if that’s impractical for one reason or another, I put them upside down rather than standing up.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
I would add that many wines with a touch of Brett may see that aspect ‘blow off’ given a decant or time to breathe.

There can be an initial ‘off note’ on many wines that is notable as they are popped, but diminishes or integrates with the rest of the aromas if given a little time.

I have had Burgundies that start off rather astringent and overtly reticent with their fruit that blossom after several hours.

I’ve also run into Cali cabs that seem like they think they are still-in the-barrel fruit abominations when first popped that integrate away from being overly fruity later in the evening as well.

I would say there are many examples of wines becoming more well integrated with time beyond pop and pour.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Well, it sometimes happens that public opinion is at variance with scientific findings. Here’s what Ronald S. Jackson has to say about decanting and breathing in Wine Science: Principles, Practice, Perception, Academic Press 2000, 2d ed., p. 566:

"Decanting and Breathing If wines contain sediment, they should be decanted in advance. Otherwise, repeated tipping of the bottle during pouring will suspend the sediment, resulting in later samples becoming cloudy.

Opening bottles in advance to breathe is unnecessary. The limited wine/air interface in the neck is insufficient to result in any prompt change in sensory character. Even decanting, which more effectively exposes the wine to oxygen, seldom produces marked changes in sensory characteristics within an hour. The main exception involves old wines, in which the minimal fragrance may dissipate rapidly. However, prolonged exposure to air results in table wines developing a stale aspect within several hours, presumably due to the formation of acetaldehyde. The view that breathing has a beneficial effect may reflect the nineteenth-century custom to decant wine to remove sediment. The procedure also allowed time for the ‘bottle stink’ to dissipate, an affliction with which the wines of the period were often afflicted."

The italicized sentence formulates the main reason why I have personally stopped decanting wine (unless it is necessary to remove sediment and then only immediately prior to serving) as well as the reason why I do not let half-empty bottles stand around without replacing the air with inert gas (mostly nitrogen) and capping/corking them properly.

As an aside, I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.

Of course, there is nothing to prevent some people from enjoying the staleness of a wine. It is certainly part of what makes a wine feel older and some may appreciate that aspect. Personally, however, I detest it. Not only do I find it disgusting per se but it also blocks my perception of other olfactory properties.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
I too am amazed that enough people believe the premise of this thread to have given it this much life. It’s probably been mentioned, but oak can seem extremely overt when a wine is first opened and integrate much more with exposure to air. Brett and premox also seem to tame with air, while cork taint gets worse. Young wine is almost always better if I plan in advance. Bottles consumed a week apart will show tremendous variation based on oxygen exposure. My experience all points to the value of exposure to oxygen after opening most younger wines so I will continue to do so where appropriate and possible. It isn’t surprising that those who feel otherwise will do otherwise, only that there are so many of them.

I find attempts to prove this kind of thing with science to be of very limited use. The studies in question seem to set out with their end goal in mind and only to verify what they had initially expected. Just like all of the studies that tell us that $5 wine is as good as $500 wine (admittedly those tend to be social science experiments at best).

Anders Westholm:

Well, it sometimes happens that public opinion is at variance with scientific findings. Here’s what Ronald S. Jackson has to say about decanting and breathing in Wine Science: Principles, Practice, Perception, Academic Press 2000, 2d ed., p. 566:

"Decanting and Breathing If wines contain sediment, they should be decanted in advance. Otherwise, repeated tipping of the bottle during pouring will suspend the sediment, resulting in later samples becoming cloudy.

Opening bottles in advance to breathe is unnecessary. The limited wine/air interface in the neck is insufficient to result in any prompt change in sensory character. Even decanting, which more effectively exposes the wine to oxygen, seldom produces marked changes in sensory characteristics within an hour. The main exception involves old wines, in which the minimal fragrance may dissipate rapidly. However, prolonged exposure to air results in table wines developing a stale aspect within several hours, presumably due to the formation of acetaldehyde. The view that breathing has a beneficial effect may reflect the nineteenth-century custom to decant wine to remove sediment. The procedure also allowed time for the ‘bottle stink’ to dissipate, an affliction with which the wines of the period were often afflicted."

The italicized sentence formulates the main reason why I have personally stopped decanting wine (unless it is necessary to remove sediment and then only immediately prior to serving) as well as the reason why I do not let half-empty bottles stand around without replacing the air with inert gas (mostly nitrogen) and capping/corking them properly.

As an aside, I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.

Of course, there is nothing to prevent some people from enjoying the staleness of a wine. It is certainly part of what makes a wine feel older and some may appreciate that aspect. Personally, however, I detest it. Not only do I find it disgusting per se but it also blocks my perception of other olfactory properties.

Whatta crock, Anders. Most of what they say I agree with. But I’ve left hundreds & hundreds sitting around in a decanter overnight, and even for days. I have never, even once, noted the
development of acetaldehyde (the pure oxidation of alcohol, not in the presence of acetobacter, which gives acetic acid/ethyl acetate and VA) in a wine. Never once. Unless the wine is highly reduced
(a very old wine closed up in a btl), the reaction rates for chemical reactions (including oxidation) is soooooo slow that nuthin’ is gonna happen chemical-wise.

The “staleness” to which they refer is, I’m convinced, merely due to the loss of volatiles into the headspace. Not chemical reactions. Which is why putting an inert gas into the headspace is not
going to do any good. You still get this loss of volatiles, whether it air, pure oxygen, pure argon, or pure Krypton, or pure Xenon. Has nuthin’ to do w/ oxidation.

I can’t believe we’re still havin’ this same tired/tedious discussion on the benefits of “breathing” of a wine. Folks…do the friggin’ experiment. Go out & buy a btl of DryCreekVnyds HeritageVines
Zin '14 (only about $14). Rip that sucker open & taste it. Pour it into a decanter & taste it an hour/2 hrs/5 hrs/a day/5 days later. And then report back here on your experiment. Did it get better
in an hour?? Probably not. In 1 day/5 days…did it get better? Probably not. Did it “soften” on the palate (and form sediment in the decanter) from polymerization of the tannins. Definitely not.
Did it develop acetaldehyde/VA/acetic acid/ethyl acetate/sherry-like character?? Definitely not. And when you’re doing this experiment, take care to keep the temperature constant…because the
character of a wine can be very sensitive to its temperature.

How many are going to do this experiment and report back here. I already know…it will be zero. So many people already “know” what they know to be “truth” and are not gonna change their minds
when given evidence.

That being said…can wines be “improved” by “breathing”? Absolutely…I’ve seen it happen myself…on rare occasions. With every btl of red wine?? I doubt it. I have little doubt that John’s
experience w/ his '96 StGeorges was true. If someone set a btl of '96 StGeorges in front of me and asked me what to do…how the heck do I know that it needed 3 hrs of breathing time??
I wouldn’t. If I set a btl of Bulfon PicolitNeri '12 or a btl of Skerk Terrano '10 or a '96 Foradori Teroldego in front of John, or anybody else, would he be able to tell me how much “breathing” time
it needed to show its best. I’m pretty sure not.

So, my standard procedure is to simply P&P. Given that I had no information on the proper “breathing” time for any given wine. And often follow the wine over several hours
to several days. Do I ever miss out on experiencing a wine at its “peak”. Probably do. But what’s a guy gonna do??

Tom

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: I also forgot to add that wines typically seem tired to me on day two, even if kept in the fridge. I find very, very few wines improve over that length of time. Which is not to say that I find them noticeably oxidized, just tired and lacking any kind of verve. Others may disagree with that conclusion, but for me the point is that oxygen does affect the wine, and that it improves it to a point before it starts sliding down the hill. If oxygen didn’t improve the wine, effectively aging it I suppose, then I’d expect most wines to be as good on day two or five as upon opening. If the wine advances as it is open it must be a progression. I don’t understand the counterargument.

Michael Powers:

If the wine advances as it is open it must be a progression. I don’t understand the counterargument.

Eggsactly my point, Michael. I, too, don’t find them noticeably oxidized. And we’re talking over hunnerds of btls here.
Just a bit “tired”. Which I attribute to the loss of volatiles into the headspace of the btl or the decanter.
Tom

That’s why this thread is weirdly so off the mark. (btw, I didn’t aim to give the 96 three hours of air. I opened it, tasted it, took it to a tasting, and it was sampled over three to four hours.)

Tom–I have enough experience with 15-20 year old burgundy, aged Barolo/barbaresco, Older Rioja, and Dauvissat Chablis in the 8-15 year age to know what happens when they get air, and to know how much more poorly they show when someone in the pop and pour camp serves them. That’s why this thread is weirdly so off the mark. (btw, I didn’t aim to give the 96 three hours of air. I opened it, tasted it, took it to a tasting, and it was sampled over three to four hours.)

Yup, John…I’ll yield to your greater experience w/ those wines. It is exactly those sorts of wines that I observe, on occasion, to be improved by “breathing”.
But if you set that wine down in front of me…I’d have no friggin’ clue as to what to let it “breathe”. So I don’t do anything but P&P…and hope that
I drink it over a long enough period that I can catch it at its “peak”. Most of the wines I drink at home by myself, or with Susan on the weekend,
I drink over a period of hrs or a day, even two. Alas, sometimes the wines are so friggin’ good that they’re gone within an hour, so I miss it.
Tom

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
TomHill:

Whatta crock, Anders. Most of what they say I agree with. But I’ve left hundreds & hundreds sitting around in a decanter overnight, and even for days. I have never, even once, noted the development of acetaldehyde (the pure oxidation of alcohol, not in the presence of acetobacter, which gives acetic acid/ethyl acetate and VA) in a wine. Never once.

Whose “crock”? Certainly not Jackson’s or mine. Your experience presumably just reflects the fact that you are not particularly sensitive to acetaldehyde and other substances responsible for the perception of staleness or that you even like them. My perceptions are different. That perceptions of the same thing vary across individuals is hardly news.

TomHill:

Unless the wine is highly reduced (a very old wine closed up in a btl), the reaction rates for chemical reactions (including oxidation) is soooooo slow that nuthin’ is gonna happen chemical-wise.

Not at all. What happens chemically is well established. The reductive smells can be gone in a matter of minutes. The reactions responsible for staleness take a bit longer, as Jackson points out. We are ordinarily talking hours rather than minutes. But I have never been able to have a wine sit in a decanter for as long as three hours without perceiving the staleness.

TomHill:

The “staleness” to which they refer is, I’m convinced, merely due to the loss of volatiles into the headspace. Not chemical reactions. Which is why putting an inert gas into the headspace is not going to do any good. You still get this loss of volatiles, whether it air, pure oxygen, pure argon, or pure Krypton, or pure Xenon. Has nuthin’ to do w/ oxidation.

See above. Putting inert gas into the headspace of a half-empty bottle prevents oxidation and thus the development of the substances responsible for staleness.

TomHill:

I can’t believe we’re still havin’ this same tired/tedious discussion on the benefits of “breathing” of a wine. Folks…do the friggin’ experiment. Go out & buy a btl of DryCreekVnyds HeritageVines Zin '14 (only about $14). Rip that sucker open & taste it. Pour it into a decanter & taste it an hour/2 hrs/5 hrs/a day/5 days later. And then report back here on your experiment. Did it get better in an hour?? Probably not. In 1 day/5 days…did it get better? Probably not. Did it “soften” on the palate (and form sediment in the decanter) from polymerization of the tannins. Definitely not. Did it develop acetaldehyde/VA/acetic acid/ethyl acetate/sherry-like character?? Definitely not. And when you’re doing this experiment, take care to keep the temperature constant…because the character of a wine can be very sensitive to its temperature.

Such an experiment would be absolutely useless for obvious reasons. The results would just be a matter of the confirmation bias (a very common mental bias) that you are already expressing. The proper way of doing it would be a) tasting blind rather than open and b) tasting at the same time rather than at different times. For that, you would obviously need an assistant.

TomHill:

How many are going to do this experiment and report back here. I already know…it will be zero. So many people already “know” what they know to be “truth” and are not gonna change their minds when given evidence.

If you are right, it’s a bit sad isn’t it? But then people are of course free to enjoy placebo effects as they see fit.

TomHill:

So, my standard procedure is to simply P&P. Given that I had no information on the proper “breathing” time for any given wine. And often follow the wine over several hours
to several days. Do I ever miss out on experiencing a wine at its “peak”. Probably do. But what’s a guy gonna do??

Feel free to do as you see fit. Personally, I already know that the exercise of following a wine over days, rather than a few hours, is pointless. The wine will just get worse in my perception (unless I preserve it by means of inert gas so that it remains pretty much the same).

Of course, my perception of a wine changes over the time I consume it. But that’s a matter of how I rather than the wine changes in the course of drinking. With “serious” wines, especially “serious” reds, the difference in my perception at the beginning and at the end is often quite substantial. That’s why I usually prefer to base my buying decisions when it comes to such wines on sharing a bottle with my wife and/or friends rather than relying on just a sip at a tasting.

The method recommended by François Audouze is, I think, the proper one in this case.

Beyond that, I know of no scientific evidence that supports the idea that wines improves with exposure to oxygen unless you actually like the perception of staleness.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

What phenomenon am I dismissing?

That volatile aromatic compounds are volatile. If you increase the headspace in a bottle they will seek a new equilibrium. If you Coravin or pour out a small amount and re-cork, this may not be significant. A half-emptied bottle (via Coravin) will degrade over time. This is independent of oxygen. It happens much more quickly in an open container. And, of course, the pouring methodology and details of the container and so forth are factors.

When you smell a wine you are smelling volatiles that are actively leaving the wine. This is why in cooking, for example, you might add basil to a dish after taking it off the heat, just prior to serving. Add it earlier and most of those aromatics would be long gone. An easy perception test is with lemon juice. Squeeze some into two cups. Stick one in the fridge, nuke the other to boiling, then chill it down. Compare the two. The cooked one will seem cooked - flat, dull, simpler, no lift. The only difference is loss of the volatile aromatics.

This is obviously a huge factor in decanting, though not the only change that is occurring. You say you’re extraordinarily sensitive to aldehydes. That may be true - not doubting you. But, their level is not changing anywhere near what you think. But, their relative level may be, which could be having a dramatic impact on your perception.

I’m more sensitive to TCA than most, so can find a wine utterly repulsive while others can till sip the wine to assess it despite detecting the flaw. I know others who are uncommonly sensitive to VA and gag at the thought of putting a wine in their mouth, while the other dozen at the table happily sip away, with only a couple even acknowledging there’s an issue. So, it’s important to not draw broad absolutist opinions based on your own perceptions.

posts.name: Anton Dotson

posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Wow… What a thread. It’s obvious many of us have widely varying views regarding letting wines breathe. In no way can I argue with Francios’s experiences which are obviously vast and I do believe what he’s saying. That said, and this is specifically in relation to old burgundies, barolo, and barbaresco; I personally don’t decant for aeration, I decant to remove sediment. I find sediment in old wine, especially older nebbiolo, to be a huge detractor to the palate of the wine. And I do have a reasonable amount of experience with this as I always pour off the sediment/dregs into a glass before washing the empty bottle. I normally like to give it 10-20 minutes once the dregs are poured off before looking at the wine sitting on dregs beside a small “clean” pour in an identical glass. Inevitably the dregs pour has a short, clipped, and bitter finish when compared to the clean pour. I’ve never once looked at the “dregs glass” (for lack of a better term) and thought, “Gee, this is much more interesting than the clean pour beside it”.

I don’t think there’s one set answer for old wine service and while I do believe slow oxing is beneficial I can’t help but think you’re losing maybe 10-15% of the wine by not gently decanting the sediment off first. My process for service of old wine varies from bottle to bottle but it always begins with a gentle decant to remove sediment. You can only assess where an old wine is by actually looking at it in glass, everything else is just guesswork. Once you’re at that point you can attempt to make a somewhat educated decision as to how you should treat the wine. You can serve immediately, leave it in the decanter, double decant back into bottle, put the cork back in the bottle, store in the fridge or at service temperature, etc.

Finally, for those of you who believe decanting kills a wine, have you ever considered the wine was on its death bed already? Put another way, what’s the difference between gently decanting a wine into a fairly slim bowl decanter when compared to gently pouring the same wine into glass from an oxygen exchange perspective? Very little in my mind. Add to that the probability that most of us are swirling the wine in glass regularly I’d actually say that the wine in glass will be exposed to more oxygen from swirling when compared to a careful and gentle decant and/or double decant.

My “formative” experience regarding this was Boxing Day a couple years ago. I opened a 1957 Mommessin Chambertin Clos de Beze for a group of wine friends. It was terrible on opening and threw some pretty off aromas and had a very lackluster palate. So I left it in the wide bowl decanter I had gently decanted it into and placed that decanter in the refrigerator. Three to four hours later the wine had completely changed and became a clean, elegant, long and vinously sweet drop. It was a stunning wine and transformation.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Michael Powers:

I’m not talking about decanting at all, though I think it’s best for all but very old wines. I’m speaking to the OP’s premise of letting wine breathe in any form. Oxygen exposure whether in bottle or out is essentially the same, right? It’s accelerated aging.

No it’s not the same. When a wine is decanted the wine is subjected to a massive oxygen shock. If the bottle is merely opened, the oxygen exposure is much much smaller. How much oxygen there is around affects the reactions that take place and the rate at which they take place. So does the much larger surface provided by a decanter for the acetobacter to do their job.

Michael Powers:

Opened wine will improve to some point then decline, just as it would over time in bottle. If it’s already old it may simply start to fall apart immediately. If oxygen only made wine decline any aging would be detrimental I’d imagine.

No, for reasons already stated, there may be no improvement at all when the wine is opened and exposed to air. This does not mean that any aging, e.g., in barrel or bottle, is detrimental. First, some reactions that take place as a result of aging are not aerobic. Second, there may, at least for a while, be some free oxygen left in the wine that has not yet reacted with it. Third, the rate at which wine is subjected to oxygen in a corked bottle is very low (if the cork or cap is good). Fourth, the wine-makers, as a rule, strive to supply the wine from the outset with the oxygen it needs to mature well.

Wes Barton:

That volatile aromatic compounds are volatile. If you increase the headspace in a bottle they will seek a new equilibrium. If you Coravin or pour out a small amount and re-cork, this may not be significant. A half-emptied bottle (via Coravin) will degrade over time. This is independent of oxygen. It happens much more quickly in an open container. And, of course, the pouring methodology and details of the container and so forth are factors.

I am not dismissing this phenomenon. I just don’t think that it is a major factor in my perception of staleness. One reason that I think so is that I have no perception of staleness whatsoever if I leave a half-empty bottle with inert gas in it over night whereas I am sure to have it if I instead leave it with air inside.

Wes Barton:

This is obviously a huge factor in decanting, though not the only change that is occurring. You say you’re extraordinarily sensitive to aldehydes. That may be true - not doubting you. But, their level is not changing anywhere near what you think. But, their relative level may be, which could be having a dramatic impact on your perception.

What makes you think that the level of aldehydes is not changing anywhere near what I think? I don’t think I have said more about this than that there will be enough change with regard to acetaldehyde (and other substances associated with the perception of staleness) that they are ordinarily perceptible to me when a wine has been sitting in a decanter for say three hours (if it’s a young and serious wine, e.g., a young Barolo or Barbersco, which is what I typically destroyed when I was still trying to come to grips with the impact of decanting; otherwise, it may well be less). Of course, I can’t completely rule out your alternative idea that my perception is to some extent due to the loss of other volatile compounds rather than an increase in the compunds that contribute to staleness but I doubt that this is a major factor.

Another factor that may be important here, however, is the amount of staleness-inducing compunds already in the wine from the outset. If this amount is fairly high, just below the perceptual threshold, then it will of course take very little in the way of further reactions to bring the amount above that threshold.

I have in fact smelled a wine go bad in the course of drinking a glass of it. This was at an informal but still systematic tasting session that I organized, where we were sitting with six glasses with different wines in front of us, trying to compare them. The point of the exercise was to compare different vintages of the same wine, so the glasses were actually paired, each pair containing the same wine but from different vintages. One of the wines (Gianni Voerzio’s Barolo La Serra 2001 and 2004) went stale in the course of the tasting while the others did not. The older vintage did so slightly before the younger but not by a whole lot. So I suspect that both wines contained considerable amounts of staleness-inducing compunds before they were poured, presumably as a result of having been rather freely oxygenated at some stage before they were sealed in the bottle.

Wes Barton:

I’m more sensitive to TCA than most, so can find a wine utterly repulsive while others can till sip the wine to assess it despite detecting the flaw. I know others who are uncommonly sensitive to VA and gag at the thought of putting a wine in their mouth, while the other dozen at the table happily sip away, with only a couple even acknowledging there’s an issue. So, it’s important to not draw broad absolutist opinions based on your own perceptions.

Sure. I have no problem at all agreeing with that.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Anton D:

I am sorry you just can’t get it right.

There is no reason to deplore problems that don’t exist. The only real problem here are your complete lack of reading comprehension and your complete inability to keep track of what your point is from one message to the next. Further examples are provided below.

Anton D:

In the very quote you referenced…

“…presumably due to the formation of acetaldehyde.”

There is no intellectual rigor there.

One could just as easily say, “…presumably due to evil spirits that befoul the sanctity of the wine.”

First, your complaint was that Jackson was discussing fermentation rather than serving. Then your complaint was that he was contradicting himself. Now, it is instead the way he phrases his claim.

Where you went wrong in the first two instances, I have already pointed out. Where you go wrong here is by confusing intellectual rigor with proper caution. The probable reason why Jackson uses the word “presumably” is that he was, at the time, not perfectly sure that acetaldehyde was the only compound responsible for the perception of staleness that results from exposure to oxygen. That’s why I inserted the following statement in the post where the quote from Jackson’s book was included.

“I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.”

Anton D:

Your ‘presumption’ is that immediately upon popping and pouring, oxygen exposure cause Acinetobacter to reanimate and ruin the wine over the course of minutes? This should be easily measured and not require ‘presumption.’

No that’s not my presumption. If you had actually paid attention to anything Jackson or I said, you would have noted that the sentence of Jackson’s that you partly quote above reads:

“However, prolonged exposure to air results in table wines developing a stale aspect within several hours [my emphasis], presumably due to the formation of acetaldehyde.”

You would additionally have noted that I have said nothing different from Jackson in this regard.

Furthermore, that acetaldehyde is formed as a result of exposure to air by decanting is indeed easily measured and has therefore been measured. You completely misunderstand the reason why Jackson inserts the word “presumably”. As already pointed out, the reason is not that he is unsure whether acetaldehyde will actually be formed when a wine is exposed to air by decanting. The reason is that other substances that might contribute to the perception of staleness may also be formed and that it is/was not completely clear what their relative contribution to the perception is.

Anton D:

I Fart In Your General Direction

You certainly do. Fortunately I am out of reach with regard to your actual farts. As to your mental ones, I’d appreciate if you stop since they are getting tiresome and constitute a distraction from those parts of the discussion that are actually worth reading.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
I am sorry but I have difficulties to read when the messages include so many quotes. So I do not know how the discussion developed.

I have just seen the mention of swirling the glass. I never do it for a reason which belongs only to me which is that I want to respect the integrity of the structure of the wine.
I will give an anecdote.
I was invited with other persons for a tasting of Château La Gaffelière in the Chateau. Count Mallet Roquefort was proud to serve a 1904 and used to pour glasses an instrument like this :

We were served and I saw some people around the table swirling. And I said to all of them : if Count Mallet Roquefort used with caution this instrument to let us have the perfect structure of the wine, it hurts my sensibility if you swirl.

Seeing the Count making so many efforts and seeing guests making a rodeo with the wine, it was too much for me.

Considering the discussion, as I did not follow all comments I will not add a new idea. I am so satisfied with the slow oxygenation method which gives so great results that I have difficulties to imagine another method.

But I must say that more and more, for young wines with a rather high alcohol, I prefer pop and pour, to see the expansion of the wine during the time of its consumption.

And concerning sediments, I love so much to drink / eat them that the problem of decanting for sediments does not exist for me.

I guess the other thing I should say is that the context for my views revolves around Burgundy, Barolo, and Barbaresco made between 1949-1980. I personally believe these wines are more resilient than most people believe, especially the Piedmontese wines. Sure, they might not improve much after the first 24 hours of air but they don’t fall apart within 48 hours of being open either. You on the other hand drink wines that I’d go as far as to categorise as ancient François. Maybe these wines are more delicate, I can’t honestly say. All I can say is that from my experience decanting hasn’t hurt the wines I’ve opened and separating the clear wine off the sediment makes these wines better to my palate. My mate Cos and I just today picked up a pair of 1964 F. Rinaldi Barolo from the same supplier. Maybe I can convince him that we should open them simultaneously, one with a quick gentle decant for sediment removal and the other to slow ox. If we do so I’ll report back with our thoughts.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
To open the two old wines simultaneously and treat them differently at the serving stage would in all likelihood be a futile exercise. Bottle-to-bottle variation becomes very significant already with wines that are a decade or so old and the two 1964s you bought are extremely likely to be significantly different before opening, even if the bottles come from the same batch and have been treated identically up to this point.

A more meaningful test would be to open just one of the bottles and then pour half the content into a decanter and let it sit there for some hours while keeping the other half in the recorked bottle prior to serving, ideally with the air in the bottle replaced by inert gas (e.g., by means of the product known as Private Preserve). Of course, you should have an assistant do the job for you so that you don’t know the identity of the two glasses you are eventually served. As the content of this thread suggests, blind tests tend to yield markedly different results than open ones.

link_counts.title: Private Preserve – The essential wine accessory

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
To each his own, I say.

I’m happy with the aeration methods I’ve used over the last 33 years with young and older wines alike.

But, to suggest that there’s a right or wrong on this is ludicrous. Way too many variables, including timing…of aeration and tasting to even so argue.

And, re: sediment…it doesn’t always taste bad. The problem is when it does and it’s floating around in an uncontrolled manner on the palate, it can cause problems. If you’re expecting it and like it, particularly that from the end of a bottle, that’s different. Free-floating sediment on “older” wines, from the tannins precipitating out…can simulate many flaws to the unwitting --and to the witting.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked: If it were ludicrous, wine science wouldn’t be interested. But it is. To measure what happens to a wine, chemically speaking, when it is served under this or that condition is of course perfectly possible. To measure how these changes affect the perceptions of wine consumers is of course perfectly possible as well. Since perceptions are subjective, the results of the latter type of studies can of course only determine what people on average feel is better or worse. Then you are of course free to say that you are in the minority who radically deviate from that average.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Benjamin Goldberg:

Daniel, the sediment from old Bordeaux doesn’t taste bad at all. I’ll decant a bottle of Bordeaux so that the sediment doesn’t get poured into people’s glasses, but then (when nobody is looking), I’ll take the last swig from the bottle and chomp down on the sediment. I would definitely not do the same thing with old Nebbiolo.

I don’t hesitate to taste them even if they derive from old Nebbiolo (as they would more often than not) in my case. The sediments largely consist of phenolic substances that have reacted with each other in the course of aging, e.g., tannin chains that have become so long and heavy that they are no longer kept afloat. These are far less aggressive than younger and shorter tannins and as a rule don’t taste much at all.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Wine is usually bottled cold, so more CO2 is retained. With many whites CO2 is deliberately retained to give the wine a bit of a bounce.

But I don’t think tannic cabs have that much retained CO2 and those are the wines people want to aerate in the sad and mistaken belief that the tannins will smooth out over a small period of time…two or three hours.

Those of you who watched Mondovino will remember Michel Rolland telling somebody to get lots of oxygen into the fermenting wine. This oxygenation is done then so as to encourage a transformation of the tannins into something smoother and more stable. That’s when O2 is important for Cabernet, etc.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Andrew Hamilton:

I got a bit overzealous chomped into some sediment that came from a bottle of 1952 Francesco Rinaldi Barolo on Saturday that was separated from the wine via a double decant over 24 hours before. I say overzealous as I probably went in a bit hard… Granted we were more than a few glasses in by the end of the afternoon so I had ample liquid courage. Safe to say the bitterness was off the charts. I actually had to rinse my mouth out over a sink to get the gritty bitter sediment of my tongue. Here’s a photo of the remnants. I’m not sure if I’ll be attempting this test again anytime soon.

Well, those sediments look quite substantial all right. Don’t know if I have ever seen something quite like that. But if sediments occasionally display the strong bitterness you describe (which I have never personally encountered in any wine), then it is presumably due to the wine having immature (“green”) tannins from the outset. I have never seen anyone claim that bitterness is somehow created as a result of bottle aging. Why would that be? And although Nebbiolo wines tend to be more tannic than most others, I don’t think they are known for having “greener” tannins than other wines. On the contrary, since the tannins are so prominent, the wine producers usually try their very best to avoid any sign of bitterness.

Now I don’t know what the situation was like in that regard back in 1952. But for the many Nebbiolos of somewhat more moderate age that I am familiar with, I have so far found no reason to fear a bit of sediment. It’s perhaps not that I directly enjoy chewing on them, like François Audouze, but I don’t let good wine go to waste just because there’s a bit of sediment in it.

Safe to say the bitterness was off the charts. I actually had to rinse my mouth out over a sink to get the gritty bitter sediment of my tongue. Here’s a photo of the remnants. I’m not sure if I’ll be attempting this test again anytime soon.

To me this is the case of ALL sediment. It’s ALL bitter and unenjoyable…and that’s why I have a hard time understanding why folks to better care for their aged bottles. Driving 50-100 miles with a 30 year old Bordeaux and then proclaiming it a lost wine that’s past it’s prime is a real head scratcher to me. I’ve found this particularly true of old Pinots. The sediment seems more fine, and to me needs more time to truly settle before we should be pulling the cork. Do I always follow my own advice/beliefs…hell no. Impatience gets the better of me sometimes…and I usually regret it later.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

That acetaldehyde develops when wine is suddenly exposed to significant amounts of oxygen is a trivial part of wine chemistry and no news to anyone with the slightest grasp of the subject

I’ve refrained from commenting so far, but you keep insisting on this point, and chiding people in this and other threads about the impossibility that wine can survive being opened for any length of time (at least that’s what I think you’re saying).

Put simply, ethanol does not react with O2 to form acetaldehyde in any quantity at room temperature without the presence of a catalyst, or enzyme. If it did, your bottle of vodka would not last very long once opened.

posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

Well, it sometimes happens that public opinion is at variance with scientific findings. Here’s what Ronald S. Jackson has to say about decanting and breathing in Wine Science: Principles, Practice, Perception, Academic Press 2000, 2d ed., p. 566:

"Decanting and Breathing If wines contain sediment, they should be decanted in advance. Otherwise, repeated tipping of the bottle during pouring will suspend the sediment, resulting in later samples becoming cloudy.

Opening bottles in advance to breathe is unnecessary. The limited wine/air interface in the neck is insufficient to result in any prompt change in sensory character. Even decanting, which more effectively exposes the wine to oxygen, seldom produces marked changes in sensory characteristics within an hour. The main exception involves old wines, in which the minimal fragrance may dissipate rapidly. However, prolonged exposure to air results in table wines developing a stale aspect within several hours, presumably due to the formation of acetaldehyde. The view that breathing has a beneficial effect may reflect the nineteenth-century custom to decant wine to remove sediment. The procedure also allowed time for the ‘bottle stink’ to dissipate, an affliction with which the wines of the period were often afflicted."

The italicized sentence formulates the main reason why I have personally stopped decanting wine (unless it is necessary to remove sediment and then only immediately prior to serving) as well as the reason why I do not let half-empty bottles stand around without replacing the air with inert gas (mostly nitrogen) and capping/corking them properly.

As an aside, I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.

Of course, there is nothing to prevent some people from enjoying the staleness of a wine. It is certainly part of what makes a wine feel older and some may appreciate that aspect. Personally, however, I detest it. Not only do I find it disgusting per se but it also blocks my perception of other olfactory properties.

Hi Anders,

The text of the 3rd edition of Wine Science by Ronald S. Jackson on p 666-667 regarding the above quote you’ve supplied has changed significantly. Specifically, the passage you italicised regarding acetaldehyde is no longer present. My guess was that the author wasn’t too keen on leaving presumed conclusions in a wine science tome. Here’s what the section on breathing now states.

Opening bottles in advance to “breathe” is unnecessary and undesirable. The limited wine/air interface generated by cork removal is minimal. Even decanting, which exposes the wine to more oxygen, does not rapidly generate noticeable changes. However, the equilibrium between weakly bound aromatics in the wine and the headspace shifts. This can result in the release of aromatics – what is termed the wine’s “opening.” It is not infrequently noted in finer quality, young wines. This is a fascinating phenomenon. It should not be wasted by having it occur unnoticed in a decanter. It is best observed in the glass as the wine is periodically swirled. With very old wines, though, the fragrance may be so evanescent that it may dissipate completely with a few minutes. In this case, tasting should commence as soon as possible after decanting (to separate the wine from any sediment).

Tip of the hat goes to both the University of Latvia and Google for making this information available.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

I’ve refrained from commenting so far, but you keep insisting on this point, and chiding people in this and other threads about the impossibility that wine can survive being opened for any length of time (at least that’s what I think you’re saying).

No that’s not a correct rendering of what I have said. Nor have I been “chiding” anyone. To tell people that I disagree about something and why I do so is not to “chide”. It is to argue, and in my view, that’s in part what forums like this are about. If you care to know what I have actually said, please consider earlier posts in this thread.

Alan Rath:

Put simply, ethanol does not react with O2 to form acetaldehyde in any quantity at room temperature without the presence of a catalyst, or enzyme. If it did, your bottle of vodka would not last very long once opened.

There is a group of bacteria known as acetic acid bacteria. If you consider their ability to thrive with and without oxygen, you find part of the reason why the ethanol in wine starts turning into acetaldehyde and eventually acetic acid when suddenly exposed to a sufficiently large dosis of air. If you additionally consider the ability of these bacteria to thrive in liquids of different alcohol strength, you find the answer to the question of why vodka can stay very long in an open bottle without developing much in the way of acetaldehyde or acetic acid whereas wine can’t.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Andrew Hamilton:

Hi Anders,

The text of the 3rd edition of Wine Science by Ronald S. Jackson on p 666-667 regarding the above quote you’ve supplied has changed significantly. Specifically, the passage you italicised regarding acetaldehyde is no longer present. My guess was that the author wasn’t too keen on leaving presumed conclusions in a wine science tome.

Hi Andrew,

I am of course well aware of the fact that it has changed. As I mentioned in my reply to Anton D here, I have the third edition of Jackson’s book in my bookshelf so you could just have asked me to supply the quote rather than turned to the University of Latvia. The reason why I preferred to quote the second edition is provided in my reply to Anton.

As I mentioned in another reply to Anton here, where we were discussing the details of what Jackson said and why, I additionally mentioned that the reason for his use of the word “presumably” most likely is that he wasn’t quite sure that acetaldehyde was the only substance responsible for the perception of staleness. I would guess that he was already aware of the ongoing research in this area that I was indirectly referring to when I inserted the following statement in the post where I originally quoted the second edition of Jackson’s book:

“I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.”

In the third edition, he of course still discusses the problems of oxidation, including but not restricted to oxidation after the bottle has been opened. He just does so in other places where he can go into further detail about the matter based on findings that were not yet available when the second edition was published. See for example p. 663 and p. 446.

What the text you quoted from the third edition adds is further emphasis on the dangers of decanting (other than immediately before serving for the purpose of removing sediments), not just for old wines (as in the second edition) but also younger ones, due to the gradual loss of valuable aromatic compounds.

posts.name: John D. Zuccarino
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
John D. Zuccarino:

For specifics… a sparking wine has about 4,000 mg/l of CO2 thus making it sparkling …

Still red wines have a value of 2,000 mg/l CO2

2,000 mg/l is above the threshold when a wine starts to feel like it is slightly sparkling. A normal value for a still red wine is more like 400-500 mg/l. See here:

http://www.winewisdom.com/articles/techie/role-of-carbon-dioxide-in-still-wines/

Since CO2 is very weakly acidic, I doubt that any release of it as a result of decanting will noticeably affect the perception of the wine unless the initial amount is excessive (for a still red wine).

However, if there is so much CO2 that wine is indeed slightly prickling, and this is not a desirable feature (when it appears in ordinary red wine, it is usually to be regarded as a fault), decanting may help to get rid of it. However, just opening the bottle some time in advance will help us well, so there really is no need to decant for this purpose. The same applies to certain other unwanted more or less unwanted gaslike compunds, e.g., reductive smells, which may, at least under fortunate circumstances, be reduced if the bottle is opened some time prior to serving.

sorry you are 100% wrong … standard CO2 value is 1,500 to 2,000 m/g L CO2 for tight wines in bottle that will live 100 years , not cheep wines… Rieslings can have 2200 m/g l before they are splashed

the wine from a bottle with only 400 m/g L CO2 would be flat dead thin no aromatic liftoff… this is all science and practical science as I have studied these values hands on for 20 years …

sorry you are 100% wrong … standard CO2 value is 1,500 to 2,000 m/g L CO2 for tight wines in bottle that will live 100 years , not cheep wines… Rieslings can have 2200 m/g l before they are splashed

Please note that we are talking about ordinary (still) red wine. Hence, Riesling, which I can certainly enjoy with a bit of effervescence, is irrelevant here.

Further, merely referring to yourself as an authority on the subject counts for absolutely nothing, as you would know if you had any experience with science. I already gave you a proper reference. You provided none. Here is another. Let me quote some of what it says:

“The reference text for understanding the sensory impact of dissolved carbon dioxide in wine is French enologist Emile Peynaud’s 1983 classic, “The Taste of Wine.” In one context after another, he presents CO2 as a double-edged component capable of making wine sing or making wine bite. In the proper balance, Peynaud argues, CO2 can lift the aromatics and add freshness and verve, especially to whites; in big reds, on the other hand, even much lower levels of CO2 can make a wine hard, acidic and overly tannic. If dry white wines are too low in CO2, they start life dull and quickly get duller; but for sweet whites, lower levels of CO2 enhance richness.”

…

“Following Peynaud and other researchers, the ballpark numbers are pretty well known. Wine emerges from fermentation with about two grams per liter of dissolved CO2, and it declines from there. At 500 milligrams per liter, the presence of CO2 is noticeable; at 1,000 mg/L, there is a slight perception of prickliness. The textbook recommendation is that age-worthy reds should be bottled with no more than 100-200 mg/L; light, fruity reds could benefit from about 500 mg/L, and whites, depending on stylistic intent, might range anywhere from 500 mg/L to 1,800 mg/L, from slightly punched up to noticeably spritzy.”

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
It’s been a while since I pinged onto this thread. This is really FWIW, but…

WineBerserkers – 11 Jul 16

TNs--WineFest IV, July 9, 2016---109 (NO THAT'S NOT A MISPRINT) wines from my...

So. The great quadrennial Cellar Depressurization has come and gone. I need to first express how truly lucky I am to have the best parents any son could ask for. My mom and dad opened their home and worked right alongside me with all the planning...

At this event, the wines were all mine and the locale was my parents’ home, so I had more or less complete control over when I opened everything. For better or for worse, you’ll see my listing of the wines I chose to give some air prior to the event date and time itself.

Almost everything left over was transferred to 375s. You will see that I tracked some of those wines over the course of about 5 days.

If you do decide to read, I would commend you to Loren’s notes as well on the event, as he had no knowledge whatsoever of what was opened early and what was not. Otherwise, I recognize that they are only one person’s opinions. I will say that I found that part of the process and prep and results very intriguing indeed and was glad to have the chance to try “decanting” and “oxygenating” out on a number of wines. My only, perhaps, regret, is that I couldn’t take a small sip of everything right on opening and then again when I tasted it.

Mike

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

There is a group of bacteria known as acetic acid bacteria. If you consider their ability to thrive with and without oxygen, you find part of the reason why the ethanol in wine starts turning into acetaldehyde and eventually acetic acid when suddenly exposed to a sufficiently large dosis of air.

I don’t know what wines you are drinking, but mine aren’t riddled with acetobacteria. I routinely (meaning with almost every bottle I open) keep wine that’s been opened for 1 or 2 days, sometimes even more (particularly white wines, refrigerated), without any problem at all. The notion that wine begins to deteriorate immediately upon opening and exposure to oxygen (at least relatively young wines) is completely counter to years of personal experience.

Me:::


agreed CO2 values for whites can be double and they are producer specific… as for the values that one needs, this depends on the style of wine etc…

the reason for the excess CO2 is because the wines are tight due to limited racking, please note when making wine for two decades one encounters such things as CO2 and it’s interaction, function in wine in relation to aging etc… I firmly disagree with anyone who dismisses a producer who allows CO2 in their wine and calls it a Flaw, as long as they disclose it, without disclosure one would not know that it needs a macro splash to open-up… the wines that have the highest CO2 will live the longest and be much less oxidation… many producers have such a low CO2 that the wines are flat, lifeless in relation to having the proper CO2 in glass…

so as a producer one knows that tight wines improve with air, that tight wines that get decanted macro splash style release CO2 and thus the acidity drops rendering the mouth-feel smoother, unctuous and savory …

The saturation concentration of carbon
dioxide at 50°F is about 2,000 mg/L (100 times that of
nitrogen) or the equivalent of 3.6 g/L T.A. as tartaric
acid. Recommendations for residual dissolved CO2 for
wines range from 200 to 800 mg/L for reds and from
700 to 1,800 mg/L for whites to avoid “flabby” tasting
wines. In the United States, the legal limit for CO2 in
“still” wine is 3,920 mg/L, a concentration difficult to
reach without carbonation or refermentation; in the
OIV countries, it is 1,000 mg/L, about half the
saturation at cellar temperature.
Reference

Principles and Practices of Winemaking. R. B. Boulton
et al, p. 434, Springer 1996.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Anders Westholm:

There is a group of bacteria known as acetic acid bacteria. If you consider their ability to thrive with and without oxygen, you find part of the reason why the ethanol in wine starts turning into acetaldehyde and eventually acetic acid when suddenly exposed to a sufficiently large dosis of air.

I don’t know what wines you are drinking, but mine aren’t riddled with acetobacteria. I routinely (meaning with almost every bottle I open) keep wine that’s been opened for 1 or 2 days, sometimes even more (particularly white wines, refrigerated), without any problem at all. The notion that wine begins to deteriorate immediately upon opening and exposure to oxygen (at least relatively young wines) is completely counter to years of personal experience.

What wines you and I are drinking is completely irrelevant since acetic acid bacteria can be found in any wine. The wines may not exactly be riddled with these bacteria as long as they are not exposed to much oxygen and are treated with sulphur. But some of these bacteria are nevertheless around and will happily multiply rapidly when given the proper conditions to thrive. They are in the air too, so there’s no way you can get around them even if they wouldn’t be in the wine from the outset.

With regard to your experiences with half-drunk bottles left for a day or two, they of course do not constitute evidence in this context since they ignore much of what science knows about perceptions. What counts here are blind tests like those reported earlier in this thread. But you are obviously free to do and think what you like nevertheless.

As to your final claim, I have never argued that wines in general deteriorate notably immediately upon opening, although for the oldest and most fragile wines, the process may be pretty quick. For the rest, what happens in the first few hours is usually not a problem.

Yup…mine too, Alan. To my understanding, acetaldhyde is simply the oxidation of alcohol and does not need acetobacter to be formed. I’ve had plenty of wines
that were highly aldehydic (Spanish/French Rancios/Madeiras), but showed zero signs of VA/EA that you get when acetobacter is present.
I oftentimes leave a half-full decanter of red on my countertop (you’d be amazed at what goes on on my countertop!!) within 1’ of two big bottles
of active vinegar cultures (EmilioCastelli acetobacter dating back to 1865) that must be spewing out (stoppered w/ a loosely inserted paper towel) tons
of acetobacter into the surrounding air. Even over the period of a week, I pick up no traces of VA/EA in the decanted wine. Nor acetaldehyde, for that matter.

I agree w/ Anders comment:

Anders:

not just for old wines (as in the second edition) but also younger ones, due to the gradual loss of valuable aromatic compounds.

that the loss of volatiles is the cause of “staleness” (I’m not exactly sure what that term means). So it makes no difference if the headspace is air, nitrogen, argon, xenon, radon, whatever.
You’re still losing those volatiles at some uncertain rate.
Tom

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
TomHill:

Yup…mine too, Alan. To my understanding, acetaldhyde is simply the oxidation of alcohol and does not need acetobacter to be formed. I’ve had plenty of wines that were highly aldehydic (Spanish/French Rancios/Madeiras), but showed zero signs of VA/EA that you get when acetobacter is present.

Acetaldehyde can be formed without the help of acetic acid bacteria provided that oxygen and the right enzymes are present. But acetaldehyde is a stepping stone in the transformation of ethanol into acetic acid even when these bacteria are present. The longer you wait, the more your perception will be dominated by VA rather than acetaldehyde.

posts.name: John D. Zuccarino
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
John D. Zuccarino:

agreed CO2 values for whites can be double and they are producer specific… as for the values that one needs, this depends on the style of wine etc…

the reason for the excess CO2 is because the wines are tight due to limited racking, please note when making wine for two decades one encounters such things as CO2 and it’s interaction, function in wine in relation to aging etc… I firmly disagree with anyone who dismisses a producer who allows CO2 in their wine and calls it a Flaw, as long as they disclose it, without disclosure one would not know that it needs a macro splash to open-up… the wines that have the highest CO2 will live the longest and be much less oxidation… many producers have such a low CO2 that the wines are flat, lifeless in relation to having the proper CO2 in glass…

so as a producer one knows that tight wines improve with air, that tight wines that get decanted macro splash style release CO2 and thus the acidity drops rendering the mouth-feel smoother, unctuous and savory …

For reds, I don’t know if it helps much if they disclose it unless the disclosure takes the form of a very clear label, e.g., “frizzante”, so that consumers can recognize right away that it is not an ordinary still red wine they are dealing with. I don’t want to “macro splash” my reds when opening them inasmuch as that has negative side effects and I see no point in loading such wines with CO2 that has to be released by such means before you can drink them. For whites, it seems that we agree that there isn’t much of a problem.

John D. Zuccarino:

The saturation concentration of carbon
dioxide at 50°F is about 2,000 mg/L (100 times that of
nitrogen) or the equivalent of 3.6 g/L T.A. as tartaric
acid. Recommendations for residual dissolved CO2 for
wines range from 200 to 800 mg/L for reds and from
700 to 1,800 mg/L for whites to avoid “flabby” tasting
wines. In the United States, the legal limit for CO2 in
“still” wine is 3,920 mg/L, a concentration difficult to
reach without carbonation or refermentation; in the
OIV countries, it is 1,000 mg/L, about half the
saturation at cellar temperature.
Reference

Principles and Practices of Winemaking. R. B. Boulton
et al, p. 434, Springer 1996.

I have no problem with any of that. It is pretty much in line with what the two references I provided say.

excellent exchange … I see we agree more than disagree…

Salute !!!

posts.name: Anders Westholm

That is not at all what Emile Peynaud suggested in Le Gout du Vin. He described a bottle decanted the day prior losing its freshness. posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
Andrew Hamilton:

Hi Anders,

The text of the 3rd edition of Wine Science by Ronald S. Jackson on p 666-667 regarding the above quote you’ve supplied has changed significantly. Specifically, the passage you italicised regarding acetaldehyde is no longer present. My guess was that the author wasn’t too keen on leaving presumed conclusions in a wine science tome.

Hi Andrew,

I am of course well aware of the fact that it has changed. As I mentioned in my reply to Anton D here, I have the third edition of Jackson’s book in my bookshelf so you could just have asked me to supply the quote rather than turned to the University of Latvia. The reason why I preferred to quote the second edition is provided in my reply to Anton.

As I mentioned in another reply to Anton here, where we were discussing the details of what Jackson said and why, I additionally mentioned that the reason for his use of the word “presumably” most likely is that he wasn’t quite sure that acetaldehyde was the only substance responsible for the perception of staleness. I would guess that he was already aware of the ongoing research in this area that I was indirectly referring to when I inserted the following statement in the post where I originally quoted the second edition of Jackson’s book:

“I might note that the exact reason for the staleness mentioned has been subject to further study after this edition of the book was published and that other substances than acetaldehyde may also be involved, such as furfural and hexanal.”

In the third edition, he of course still discusses the problems of oxidation, including but not restricted to oxidation after the bottle has been opened. He just does so in other places where he can go into further detail about the matter based on findings that were not yet available when the second edition was published. See for example p. 663 and p. 446.

What the text you quoted from the third edition adds is further emphasis on the dangers of decanting (other than immediately before serving for the purpose of removing sediments), not just for old wines (as in the second edition) but also younger ones, due to the gradual loss of valuable aromatic compounds.

My opinion differs from yours Anders. I believe Jackson used the word presumably as he didn’t have the scientific evidence to back up the claim. I also believe he would have kept the reference to acetaldehyde in the 3rd edition if he was convinced it was responsible (even partially). To remove it all together makes me inclined to believe he felt he overreached and that needed to be wheeled back. Even the supplementary quote you point to uses the phrase “may also be involved” which doesn’t read as definitive in my mind.

I checked on pages 663 and 446 as you suggested but I don’t read those sections as providing definitive evidence of acetaldehyde being a byproduct of extended decanting. Specifically, on page 446 the following is what I assume you’re referring to:

In addition to the loss or modification of grape and yeast aromatics, new compounds > may be > generated. Some of these > appear to be > the result of oxidation. The best-known of these is acetaldehyde. > However, the importance of acetaldehyde to the oxidized odor of white wines has recently come under question (Escudero et al., 2002)> . In contrast, the production of furfural and hexanal > appears to be > particularly important to the development of an oxidized odor. Their odor > may > play a significant role in masking the wine’s natural fragrance. Exposure to high temperatures also generates additional compounds (Silva Ferreira et al., 2003). However, the descriptive terms for these compounds > appear > more related to expressions > used to describe an aged bouquet than an oxidized odor> . In addition, quinones generated during oxidation > may > react with amino acids.

And on page 663:

Significant flavor changes also develop upon bottle opening. Within the normal time frame of meal consumption, this is undetectable (Russell et al., 2005). However, after several hours, the wine begins to lose its original character. The escape and oxidation of ethyl and acetate esters, and to a lesser extent volatile terpenols, > may be > involved in these changes (Fig. 8.54).

Presence of an ethyl acetate off-odor is less common than in the past (Sudraud, 1978). At concentrations below 50 mg/liter, ethyl acetate can add a subtle fragrance. However, at about 100 mg/liter, it begins to have a negative effect. This > may > result from its masking of the fragrance of fruit-smelling esters (Piggott and Findlay, 1984). At above 150 mg/liter, its own acetone (Cutex-like) odor becomes marked. It is usually more readily obvious in white than red wines.

The bolding and underlining was obviously done by me. Personally I don’t believe the words “may be” and “appear to be” constitute a scientific certainty. If Jackson was truly convinced of these chemical processes occurring in wines subjected to an extended decant my expectation would be he’d use more definitive terminology. Also the more I read on this the more it seems that these passages are more in reference to white wines instead of reds.

Finally, I should mention that the shout out to the University of Latvia was due to the fact that when I googled Jackson’s book title their FTP server link to the PDF of the 3rd edition was the 5th returned result. Please don’t think I went to Latvia to dig up these quotes!

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
I think you misinterpret both me and Jackson.

First, my position has been all along that decanting leads to a perception of staleness within the time span roughly indicated by Jackson (several hours). As I have tried to indicate all along, however, I remain uncertain about the chemical compounds mainly responsible for that perception. This is why I added the statement you mistakenly take to be a “supplementary quote” from Jackson’s book to the post in which I quoted him.

Second, I think Jackson’s position regarding these things is largely in agreement with mine.

Third, it is not correct to say that the passages on pp. 446 and 663 that you quote are generally more in reference to white wines than reds. There are a few sentences on p. 446 which are specifically about white wines. The rest of the text is not. Furthermore, what happens to white wine is certainly not irrelevant with regard to the topic of this thread.

If we try to summarize it all, there is, as far as I can tell, a good deal of scientific support for the idea that decanting, if done quite some time before drinking, is likely to imply deterioration due to two basic mechanisms: the development of “off-odors” and the loss of valuable aromatic compounds.

As to the University of Latvia, you can rest assured that I never thought you went there to dig up the quotes. Of course I realized that it merely helped you out via the Internet, one way or the other.

There is obviously no question that wine deteriorates once opened, we know that because eventually it will become undrinkable. What that time course is depends on the particular wine, its age, its condition, the environmental conditions, etc. But, except for very old wines that are already teetering on the edge of viability, my personal experience has been that most wines are pretty sturdy, and will hold up very nicely - even improve - for a day or two without developing any negative characteristics.

I think I’ll leave it at that, because I doubt we’ll be able to convince each other much further.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Anders Westholm:

First, my position has been all along that decanting leads to a perception of staleness within the time span roughly indicated by Jackson (several hours).

Anders, do you believe the same about just pouring from an open bottle, putting the cork back in, and pouring later from that bottle? Because, again, I (and most of us) do that all the time, and have done for years and years, including drinking from bottles that were opened 1, 2, and sometimes more days. While there are definitely some wines that don’t seem as fresh as they once were, many, many hold up just fine, and some even improve (most young wines in my experience, for example).

Yes and no. What differs is the time span. In the case of decanting, we are talking hours. If you leave the wine in the bottle, we are talking days.

The reason why the process is much faster in the case of decanting is not so much the greater surface exposed to air once the wine is in the decanter as the heavy dosis of air introduced into the wine just by pouring it. It doesn’t help if you pour the wine back into the bottle after decanting. The wine has already got all the oxygen it needs to go stale within a matter of hours anyway. The reason why it doesn’t go stale immediately is that it takes some time for the reactions to take place.

If the wine remains in the bottle, by contrast, it takes quite a while for it to “absorb” enough oxygen to go perceptibly stale. You can also interrupt the process by replacing the air in the head space by inert gas and then recorking. This is what I do when a bottle is left half-emptied. If I merely recork before putting the bottle in the fridge, then I invariably find that the wine is worse 24 hours later. If I first replace the air by inert gas, the wine remains fine for at least a week.

Now, as I have indicated a few times already in this thread, I am well aware that I am more sensitive than the average person to the off-odors that develop as a result of oxidation. Hence, the time it takes before I perceive that the wine has gone stale is shorter than average no matter which particular form of aeration we are talking about.

Albert Bouffard:

Probably so! The wines seem to need a little air; whether that’s best via decanting or Audouze I am not sure. I haven’t much tried Francois’ method, though I doubt many people have as much experience as he has with bottles of that age. I have had very good luck with a short decant on a lot of these older bottles (especially the 59s) so I’ve never felt much pressure to try the slow-ox. To be honest, I’m not sure I have the bravery to open a bottle that old and let it sit for several hours!

I’d say that you are braver if you decant than if you just open the bottle in advance as François suggests. Decanting means that you give the wine a massive dosis of air just by pouring it and this is of course irreversible. Furthermore, you leave the valuable aromatic compounds free to leave the wine at a much faster rate. The method François advocates is much gentler and gives you more control over the process of aeration.

posts.name: Anders Westholm

Tvrtko C.:

So let me explain. What wine science says is that there is no need to open a wine some time ahead of drinking it unless it suffers from certain wine faults, specifically excess carbon dioxide and some of the off-odors associated with sulfur compounds. It additionally says that decanting wine some time ahead of drinking it (and even at the time of drinking it except for the purpose of removing sediments that might detract from the enjoyment) tends to be harmful rather than helpful due to the development of off-odors associated with oxidation and the loss of valuable aromatic compounds. Most views I see expressed on this board, including many in this thread, are at variance with these claims. Why wouldn’t those expressing these views be interested in finding out how things actually stand?

posts.name: Jay T.
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

So let me explain. What wine science says is that there is no need to open a wine some time ahead of drinking it unless it suffers from certain wine faults, specifically excess carbon dioxide and some of the off-odors associated with sulfur compounds. It additionally says that decanting wine some time ahead of drinking it (and even at the time of drinking it except for the purpose of removing sediments that might detract from the enjoyment) tends to be harmful rather than helpful due to the development of off-odors associated with oxidation and the loss of valuable aromatic compounds. Most views I see expressed on this board, including many in this thread, are at variance with these claims.

This quote presents nub of the argument. I understand that you are an academic, Anders. But you are clearly not a physical scientist, unlike several of your interlocutors. Chemistry cannot say either of these things. Chemistry can measure changes in the wine and the dispersal of aromatic compounds, but it cannot say whether those chemical changes produce distasteful “off-odors” or whether the “loss” of aromatic compounds makes a particular wine smell or taste worse. Whether or not something smells or tastes good is subjective and not directly measurable by a lab experiment. While “science” can be used to reach broader conclusions about the most common subjective responses to the presence of certain aromatic and flavor compounds in wine, it will always depend on trying to measure individual responses (typically through a survey of some sort), and the results will always be limited by both context and the inherent issues with survey responses.

For example, I am not aware of any rigorous scientific study involving the subjective responses to decanting aged Barolo. But I consistently experience subjective improvements in the smell and taste of aged Barolo from decanting. I have tested my own observations on two occasions by pouring out old Barolo into a small decanter, recorking and refrigerating the remainder, and then blind tasting the two side by side at various intervals with other tasters. While there were differences in both experiments in terms of various tasters’ peak drinking time, everyone agreed on both occasions that the decanted, unrefrigerated wine improved over several hours (up to 12 hours). Our experiences are also consistent with the almost universally shared experiences of people who regularly drink aged Barolo.

The lab experiments you cite may or may not help to explain these subjective experiences, but they can’t disprove them. And if there were a well-designed scientific study that showed that the vast majority of drinkers actually prefer aged Barolo (as opposed to some other variety) without decanting it and soon after opening, I assume we would have seen that by now. One can challenge the rigor of my own informal experiments or the shared evaluations of other board members. But arguing that the available science contradicts my subjective experience (or those of other posters) reflects nothing more than a fundamental failure to understand the limits of your own evidence. It also is the likely cause of people – including the actual chemists on this forum – declining to argue with you further.
