posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Jay T.:
Anders Westholm:

So let me explain. What wine science says is that there is no need to open a wine some time ahead of drinking it unless it suffers from certain wine faults, specifically excess carbon dioxide and some of the off-odors associated with sulfur compounds. It additionally says that decanting wine some time ahead of drinking it (and even at the time of drinking it except for the purpose of removing sediments that might detract from the enjoyment) tends to be harmful rather than helpful due to the development of off-odors associated with oxidation and the loss of valuable aromatic compounds. Most views I see expressed on this board, including many in this thread, are at variance with these claims.

This quote presents nub of the argument. …

Jay,

On what grounds do you think that there is any need to tell me that chemistry alone cannot say either of these things? I certainly never thought it could and certainly haven’t said so either. But wine science does not involve chemistry alone. It involves a number of disciplines, an important one of which being perceptual psychology. In fact, without perceptual measurements, wine science wouldn’t be of much use since certain perceptions is what winemakers try to achieve and what consumers enjoy whereas others are the opposite. If you knew anything about perceptual psychology, you would know that it regularly performs lab experiments, just as chemistry does. Now, since perceptions are known to vary across individuals, the outcome of perceptual experiments will as a rule take the form of a distribution rather than a single figure. This does not make the results less scientific or prevent them from being useful.

You also seem to believe that I am arguing that wine science can disprove your subjective experiences. But that is a misunderstanding of my position as well as that of wine science. What the evidence provided by wine science suggests is that certain subjective experiences with regard to wine breathing are unlikely to be reproduced in a blind test and that if they were indeed reproduced, they would constitute an outlier.

As to your own experiments, I can’t comment on them in any meaningful way without knowing more. If you report them according to scientific standards, I’d be happy to take look. At this point, it is not even clear to me that they contradict the scientific evidence at our disposal. As I have pointed out, certain wine faults can indeed be reduced by aeration and I would guess that you cannot tell me if the wines you experimented with had any of these faults or not.

It might be that some of my interlocutors are chemists although none of them has explicitly said so. If they think my knowing more about their professional credentials would simplify the discussion, it would be helpful if they told me what they are. Since, however, argument from authority isn’t a particularly strong argument, it would be preferable if they told me in which specific respects and on what specific grounds my reasoning, or that of wine science, is in error as far as the chemistry is concerned when and if it actually is. So far, I haven’t seen anyone do so. Please let me know if I am wrong.

If some people decline to argue with me because of misunderstandings like yours, that is unfortunate. All I can do in this respect is to point out such misunderstandings when they appear. Personally, however, I don’t see any reason to refrain from contributing to a public debate like the present one on such grounds. My decisions about what to say and not in such debates are based on what I believe is in the interest of those readers who understand and respect the norms of rational discourse rather than my thoughts about the person whose post I am responding to. Consequently, I may well choose to take issue with someone I have no hope of personally convincing for the benefit of other readers.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Jay T.:

I apologize if I misrepresented your statement, Anders. Your post, which I quoted directly, says nothing about psychology but does make claims based on chemistry.

So let’s have another look at the part of my post that you quoted. The two relevant sentences read:

“What wine science says is that there is no need to open a wine some time ahead of drinking it unless it suffers from certain wine faults, specifically excess carbon dioxide and some of the off-odors associated with sulfur compounds. It additionally says that decanting wine some time ahead of drinking it (and even at the time of drinking it except for the purpose of removing sediments that might detract from the enjoyment) tends to be harmful rather than helpful due to the development of off-odors associated with oxidation and the loss of valuable aromatic compounds.”

These sentences are obviously based on findings that relate what happens chemically (and to some extent biologically) to what happens psychologically, i.e. with regard to perception. For example, to establish that there are stronger off-odors associated with certain sulfur compounds that sometimes appear in wine than with the compounds into which they transform when oxidized require that we establish how the smell of the various compounds are perceived by human beings.

Jay T.:

The only “wine science” you have specifically discussed anywhere in this thread is to a book by a botanist. The actual passages you quoted from that book discussed chemistry, not psychology.

Like my own statements above, the passages I quoted from that book were based on findings that relate what happens chemically (and to some extent biologically) to what happens psychologically, i.e. in terms of perceptions. And I find absolutely nothing strange or suspect about the fact that a book about wine science is written by a botanist. Nor do I think this fact by definition makes what the author says about chemistry or psychology less credible. In many applied sciences involving more than a single discipline, the scholars involved have their training and/or position in a single discipline but have, thanks to their research activities, acquired sufficient skills in the other disciplines involved to do their job properly. I don’t know if it was even possible at the time Jackson got his education to get a Ph.D. in wine science. Perhaps it is possible now at certain select universities, like UC Davis.

Jay T.:

And you yourself described your source as “a scientific textbook on wine chemistry and biology.”

Yes I did, in a post (this one) which specifically discussed chemistry and biology rather than perception. The discussion at that point was about why certain reactions take place rather soon when wine is exposed to oxygen but not when a distilled spirit, such as scotch, is exposed to oxygen.

Jay T.:

But if your representations as to the consensus in the field of “wine science” regarding the detrimental effect of decanting on subjective experience are actually rooted in “perceptual psychology,” I apologize. I look forward to your presentation of experiments conducted by perceptual psychologists establishing that extended decanting tends to be objectively detrimental to wine enjoyment for all but specifically flawed wines.

I am sorry, but I am not going to do that job for you. Instead, I suggest that you start by considering the evidence already referenced in this thread, which includes three relevant experiments, two of which includes the wine (Barolo) where you think the evidence in support of the claims of wine science is particularly weak. I am thinking about the OP by S. Stevenson here, the post by Mel Knox here (and several later posts as well), and the post by ThomHill here.

One final question: Why do you put wine science and perceptual psychology within quotation marks?

posts.name: Anders Westholm

Bob Hoelting:

The fact that I enjoy every wine immensely more after letting it breathe is not a myth.

Nor is the fact that people who are given sugar pills but told by a physician that it is a medical drug against the disease they are suffering from tend to get better, or at least think that they get better. That’s why medical studies of drug effects are routinely done blind.

If the drug being tested is effective, then those who got the drug will tend to get even better than those who got the sugar pill. Consequently, the maximum total effect is achieved when beliefs about the effect are in line with the effect brought about by chemical means.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Relevant here are also experiments showing how perceptions and evaluations vary with expectations, more generally. Here’s a summary of some of the pertinent examples as far as wine is concerned:

The Atlantic – 28 Oct 11

'You Are Not So Smart': Why We Can't Tell Good Wine From Bad

Presentation, price, marketing, and service -- it all leads to an expectation of quality. The actual experience of tasting the wine is less important.

If you think that you are served red wine, you will describe it as one, even if it is white. If you are told that you are served an expensive wine, you will evaluate it more positively than if you are told it’s cheap. And if you believe decanting in advance is beneficial, you’ll like the wine more if you know it has been sitting in the decanter for a while even though blind tests yield the opposite result.

link_counts.title: 'You Are Not So Smart': Why We Can't Tell Good Wine From Bad - The Atlantic

posts.cooked:
Don’t want to wade into this one but Jackson, even though he called his book “Wine Science”, wasn’t claiming to be the final word. I have his book and it’s basically a summary of the literature. He provides his sources - I’ve looked some of them up over the years, but in every case that I’ve checked, there’s also further work that’s been done.

Where he has the data, he refers to sensory evaluations made by various panels. But we all know that those are of limited use - a random group of tasters coming from a rave might arrive at some consensus regarding a wine, but that would be meaningless to most on this board.

The passage quoted is neither complete nor definitive, but it provides a summary of some of the things that happen. It doesn’t answer the question of perception with finality for anyone though and I doubt that he intended it to be a final answer.

My suggestion to Anders is to try the experiment with different wines at different times. Get two bottles of the same wine and have someone serve them to you blind in two separate glasses. Sometimes one will have been decanted for six hours or a day, sometimes not, and sometimes both glasses will have the same wine from the same bottle.

I’ve done this a lot. It’s instructive. See what you find for yourself. From my own limited experience I would venture to say that whether decanting helps or not depends entirely on the wine in question.

posts.name: Pat Burton
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Pat Burton says that in science the burden of proof is on those making the hypothesis. The hypothesis here is that wines improve with aeration.

There are no studies I can find that deal with exposure to oxygen of already aged wine. The following are some quotes I was able to find from peer reviewed articles that indicate that oxygen exposure is essential to wine evolution in the bottle, glass, or decanter. Italics are my comments / additions

“The production of fermentation-related VSCs [volatile sulfur compounds], such as H2S, methanethiol (MeSH), and ethanethiol (EtSH), can result in the development of undesirable odors, often described as rotten egg, putrefaction, sewage, and burnt rubber, that are obviously detrimental to wine quality. These odors are generally most evident at low oxygen concentrations and are described to be sulfidic off-odors. Wines that display such odors are described as having reductive character.” Kreitman et al. 2016. J Agric. Food Chem.

“Wine aroma changes dramatically during bottle aging, through a complex array of chemical reactions that are only partly understood. Table 1 summarizes the main changes occurring in key wine aroma compounds during aging, highlighting the possible contribution of oxygen. In most cases, oxygen contributes to the evolution of these key aroma compounds.”

“oxygen exposure allows expression of optimal aroma attributes.” Lopes et al. 2009. J Agric. Food Chem. (admittedly this article is more focused on bottle aging wine, but draws the conclusion that oxygen is a key component of bottle aging.)

“It is undeniable that some oxidation during wine making and aging is required in order to reach wine optimum quality…At low concentrations these aldehydes may add to the complexity of a wine, but at higher levels, they are responsible for the loss of freshness(11) and for the development of specific oxidation-related off-odors.(12)” Bueno et al. 2016 J. Argic. Food Chem. (to present both sides fairly, these authors find that exposure of wine to oxygen is beneficial up to a point before it becomes harmful. Here it seems clear to me that you have to consider perception of the wine at different levels of oxygen exposure.)

I ran out of time, but the above is a start to respond to Mel’s comment, which I think is a fair one. When I have time, I’ll try to add to this.

But if your representations as to the consensus in the field of “wine science” regarding the detrimental effect of decanting on subjective experience are actually rooted in “perceptual psychology,” I apologize. I look forward to your presentation of experiments conducted by perceptual psychologists establishing that extended decanting tends to be objectively detrimental to wine enjoyment for all but specifically flawed wines.

I am sorry, but I am not going to do that job for you. Instead, I suggest that you start by considering the evidence already referenced in this thread, which includes three relevant experiments, two of which includes the wine (Barolo) where you think the evidence in support of the claims of wine science is particularly weak. I am thinking about the OP by S. Stevenson here, the post by Mel Knox here (and several later posts as well), and the post by ThomHill here.

What a joke, Anders. You come in here asserting that “wine science says…there is no need to open a wine some time ahead of drinking it” and “additionally says that decanting wine some time ahead of drinking it … tends to be harmful rather than helpful.” You belittle everyone who challenges you because they don’t present their own “wine science.” You tell me and others that we don’t understand science and the ability of “perceptual psychology” to prove your assertions. You tell me that my own experiments need to be “report[ed]” “according to scientific standards” for you to consider them. But when I ask you to show me your “wine science,” you tell me that you won’t do it. And while you tell me that you can’t consider my own informal experiments, you rely on the contradictory, informal experiments of others that support your view.

Here’s why you won’t show me the “wine science” that justifies your actual, sweeping claims. It doesn’t exist. And you know it. I know you know it because if you had actual “wine science” that established your claims, it wouldn’t require any work of you to show it to me. Your admission that it would require work to find scientific support for your claims indicates that you don’t actually have that scientific support and don’t know where to find it.

In other words, you are a fraud. I’m here for fun. While I’m interested in science as it applies to wine, I’m willing to participate in a discussion about science on this forum only to the extent that it is both conducted honestly and remains enjoyable as a lay wine enthusiast. If you had just stated a personal conclusion based on a synthesis of reading and personal experience, we could have had a discussion. But you decided to present your personal conclusion as scientific consensus. It’s not. You defended yourself by imposing a standard of scientific rigor on others in a non-scientific forum that you miserably fail to satisfy yourself and made the discussion ugly in the process. Because you are both dishonest and rude – which means that I am unlikely to learn anything from you and won’t enjoy discussing wine with you – I’ll leave it to others to direct me to relevant literature, to the extent it exists.

Feel free to have the last word, and if you eventually present relevant scientific evidence, I trust it will make its way to me through others.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
GregT:

Don’t want to wade into this one but Jackson, even though he called his book “Wine Science”, wasn’t claiming to be the final word. I have his book and it’s basically a summary of the literature. He provides his sources - I’ve looked some of them up over the years, but in every case that I’ve checked, there’s also further work that’s been done.

No scientist worthy of the name would ever claim to have the final word on any general proposition about the real world. When it comes to empirical science, as opposed to logic and mathematics, final proof is an impossibility. What Jackson’s statements reflect is the fact that so far, noone has been able to come up with any evidence that really contradicts it when put to the test. Furthermore, Jackson’s conclusion essentially echoes that of Emile Peynaud (Le goût du vin/The Taste of Wine, 1983) more than 30 years ago although most of the studies of the subject have in all likelihood been conducted after rather than before the publication of Peynaud’s book. That tells us something about the robustness of the verdict.

And yes, there is always further work that’s been done and may not be in the list of references, particularly in a textbook treatment like Jackson’s. But have you ever come across anything that seriously, and on valid grounds, contradicts his claims?

GregT:

My suggestion to Anders is to try the experiment with different wines at different times. Get two bottles of the same wine and have someone serve them to you blind in two separate glasses. Sometimes one will have been decanted for six hours or a day, sometimes not, and sometimes both glasses will have the same wine from the same bottle.

I’ve done this a lot. It’s instructive. See what you find for yourself. From my own limited experience I would venture to say that whether decanting helps or not depends entirely on the wine in question.

Thanks, but of course I have already done a number of experiments of this kind on myself. When first encountering the steady stream of decanting advice provided by forums like this, I was sure that there must be something to it. So I too began to decant hours in advance only to find, much to my disappointment, that it was never helpful (unless the wine had a fault of the kind where aeration is known to help, e.g., excess carbonic acid) and always detrimental if the wine was allowed to stay in the decanter sufficiently long, say three hours, even for the sturdiest of wines (young serious tannic reds, mostly young Barolo or Barbaresco in my case).

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Pat Burton:

There are no studies I can find that deal with exposure to oxygen of already aged wine. The following are some quotes I was able to find from peer reviewed articles that indicate that oxygen exposure is essential to wine evolution in the bottle, glass, or decanter.

As a matter of fact, none of the quotes you provide say anything about how wine evolves in a drinking glass or decanter. They all deal with stages prior to that one. Consequently, you did not manage to come up with anything at all of relevance to the present discussion.

Furthermore, no one has claimed that oxygen is always detrimental or useless prior to the stage of serving. If it were, no one would ever age wine in barrels.

There are some very important differences between the exposure to oxygen that may be beneficial to a wine at stages prior to serving and the one it is given when decanted. In the latter case, the wine is given a sudden and massive dosis of oxygen. At prior stages by contrast, every measure is taken to avoid precisely that. That’s why it is called microoxygenation. Decanting is the very opposite of that.

Pat Burton:

I ran out of time, but the above is a start to respond to Mel’s comment, which I think is a fair one. When I have time, I’ll try to add to this.

It is interesting to see you contradict yourself so blatantly here. When Mel asks you to back up the claim that decanting is beneficial, you think it is a fair request. When I ask you to do exactly the same, i.e., provide evidence against a claim to the contrary, you think it is up to me to do the job. Moreover, you do so in spite of the fact that, unlike you, I have already provided a valid reference, which, as GregT points out, simply summarizes what is known based on the studies done on the subject.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
First of all, I think Anders’ link to the article about the psychology of tasting wine is right on. The New Yorker published some articles on the same subject by Adam Gopnik a few years ago, all well worth reading.

When I got into the biz, the accepted wisdom was that old wines should be served immediately and young wines should be allowed to breath. The exception to this rule was for Barolo and Barbaresco. These wines were bottled on an as-needed basis so VA tended to be high. Corti suggested opening the bottle a day in advance and then leaving it next to the radiator!

When the Piemontese got rich enough to bottle whole tanks at one time, VA levels went way down. Imagine that. Of cours,e the fact that they work with a variety high in tannin and low in color means that it seems they change traditions every ten years. Indeed, Barolo was a pinkish aperitif wine until Pasteur and pals developed yeast strains that worked here.

Nobody denies that oxygen is an important agent for change in wine, but what we are arguing about is, Does it make the wine tASTE BETTER…and that is a issue that dips into the world of psychology.

For example, winemakers are taught to avoid flor yeast etc unless they are making Sherry. But since wines from the Jura have become a fave rave, that has changed. The science here did not change, but what was shunned has become hip.


My point is that we think oxygen smooths out the tannins so by golly it does. Our palates and minds adjust and voila, breathing wine is wonderful. Our little experiment throws these ideas into question.
My thought was always that what really happens is that the tasters adjust to the wine. What seemed rough on first taste seems smoother after the palate has adjusted to it. It’s not that the wine has gotten smoother; we have gotten tougher.

What really happens? I don’t know. I’m just a barrel pimp.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

My thought was always that what really happens is that the tasters adjust to the wine. What seemed rough on first taste seems smoother after the palate has adjusted to it. It’s not that the wine has gotten smoother; we have gotten tougher.

Of course you are right about that. I don’t know how many statements I have seen about how the wine changes in the course of drinking it (“after an hour, it opened up” etc. etc.) but few seem to realize that their perceptions of the wine change as a result of drinking it, adapting to the more astringent elements and becoming more and more anesthetized by gradual intoxication.

Personally, I find awareness of this fact important not only in the present context but also when evaluating wine for purchase. I realized long ago that merely tasting a wine, e.g., at the producer, was not always a reliable guide to how I would eventually evaluate it when drinking it. This is particularly true about serious reds, e.g., Barolo or Barbaresco. Consequently, my preferred way of deciding if I like such wines enough to buy more of them is to share a test bottle with someone. By so doing, I know how I feel about the wine not only when I first sip it but also when I am fully “into it”.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
Mel Knox:

My thought was always that what really happens is that the tasters adjust to the wine. What seemed rough on first taste seems smoother after the palate has adjusted to it. It’s not that the wine has gotten smoother; we have gotten tougher.

Of course you are right about that. I don’t know how many statements I have seen about how the wine changes in the course of drinking it (“after an hour, it opened up” etc. etc.) but few seem to realize that their perceptions of the wine change as a result of drinking it, adapting to the more astringent elements and becoming more and more anesthetized by gradual intoxication.

I think most people here understand quite well how their perceptions adapt and change over a sitting, as well as the result of other factors. Yes, that includes the power of suggestion, but also things like what one has eaten, mood, sound, medication, illness, hydration level, and so on.

An experiment of sorts my blind tasting group does routinely is examine a flight of wines over time. Always pop-n-poured, they evolve over time (or not, in some cases), some for the better, some for the worse. The changes can be quite dramatic, or not. Preconceptions go out the window with this sort of tasting. You simply can’t tell which wines will do what with much certainty. Not all wines that improve showed flaws to begin with. Weak and seemingly unstable wines can come together. Firm, ungiving wines may open up nicely or turn into awkwardly tannic unstable messes.

The reason you’re all having trouble looking for the science is because it’s more specific. We’ve already touched on how oxygen isn’t the only factor in how a wine changes once opened. You’ve discussed how a wine is treated re oxygen exposure has a major impact on how it changes. You note reduction as a flaw that does need air to blow off. But, what about when it isn’t a flaw? A moderately reductive wine can show no obvious sulfur compounds, but merely tight, ungiving. Some wines are intentionally made this way. Then, it is factual that such a wine would become less reductive as a factor of oxygen exposure and time. Of course, there are other changes occurring at the same time (each with their own sets of scientific studies). Perception is individual and dependent on quite a large number of variables, but there are certainly wines that do improve with decanting to the legitimate perception of most people. Perhaps, with your admittedly unusual sensitivity to whatever it is would observe such a wine transition from one unpleasantness to another, without any sweet spot in between.

Winemakers control oxygen exposure of wines in a number of ways for a number of reasons. Anything from type and specifics of vessel(s), micro ox, splash racking, to timing of bottling. Sometimes they even know what they’re doing. In bottle, permeability of corks vary greatly. Winemakers don’t intentionally make wines that show best upon opening, then fall apart from there; and they especially shouldn’t behave that way prior to anticipated maturity. So, think of a wine that doesn’t show “right” upon opening as a puzzle as to how and if it can be brought around. Audouze method? You can very gently siphon a mature wine off its sediment into a clean bottle or narrow decanter, and even sparge both vessels while you’re at it. You might want moderate or aggressive oxygen exposure. There’s even those aerator pourer thingies. What doesn’t make sense is to accept a winemaker using a practice to resolve an issue at some point before bottling, and de-legitimize the same practice to resolve the same issue on the consumer end. If X amount of oxygen is required for a wine to show its best, you certainly don’t want 5X, nor do you want .1X. If pouring that wine into a certain decanter means the wine will approach X amount of oxygen in 3 hours, what’s wrong with that? Wouldn’t the wine be too reductive for the negative changes you cite to occur in that time range?

My thought was always that what really happens is that the tasters adjust to the wine. What seemed rough on first taste seems smoother after the palate has adjusted to it. It’s not that the wine has gotten smoother; we have gotten tougher.

Of course you are right about that. I don’t know how many statements I have seen about how the wine changes in the course of drinking it (“after an hour, it opened up” etc. etc.) but few seem to realize that their perceptions of the wine change as a result of drinking it, adapting to the more astringent elements and becoming more and more anesthetized by gradual intoxication.

What I was talking about here was how perceptions change in the course of drinking. That’s something else than the kinds of awareness you mention above. As to the power of suggestion or expectation, this thread demonstrates quite clearly that they are not well understood by most people here, or that if understood on a more general level, nevertheless ignored in practice.

Wes Barton:

The reason you’re all having trouble looking for the science is because it’s more specific.

Don’t know quite what you mean by this general statement. Could you please elaborate a bit.

Wes Barton:

We’ve already touched on how oxygen isn’t the only factor in how a wine changes once opened.

Yes, the other factor is that volatile compounds gradually leave the wine. Are there any others you think we should take into account?

Wes Barton:

You’ve discussed how a wine is treated re oxygen exposure has a major impact on how it changes. You note reduction as a flaw that does need air to blow off. But, what about when it isn’t a flaw? A moderately reductive wine can show no obvious sulfur compounds, but merely tight, ungiving. Some wines are intentionally made this way. Then, it is factual that such a wine would become less reductive as a factor of oxygen exposure and time.

Could you please provide an example of a wine that is intentionally made to show more tight and ungiving when opened than it could have been made to show? I am perfectly open to the possibility that the sulfur compounds at issue here are in some cases not obvious but merely make the wine mute, just like a small amount of cork taint might. In fact, I posted about exactly that possibility in another thread here just a few days ago. I’d consider excess sulfur a wine fault, although if easily eliminated by a bit of aeration or by just allowing a bit more of bottle age prior to consumption, a minor one.

Wes Barton:

Of course, there are other changes occurring at the same time (each with their own sets of scientific studies). Perception is individual and dependent on quite a large number of variables, but there are certainly wines that do improve with decanting to the legitimate perception of most people.

What other changes with what set of scientific studies do you have in mind? And what do you mean by “legitimate” as opposed to “illegitimate” perception?

Wes Barton:

Winemakers control oxygen exposure of wines in a number of ways for a number of reasons. Anything from type and specifics of vessel(s), micro ox, splash racking, to timing of bottling. Sometimes they even know what they’re doing. In bottle, permeability of corks vary greatly. Winemakers don’t intentionally make wines that show best upon opening, then fall apart from there

I think they do try to make wines that show best upon opening. Doing so does not imply any risk that the wine will go perceptibly worse within the period of time it usually takes to empty the bottle.

Wes Barton:

So, think of a wine that doesn’t show “right” upon opening as a puzzle as to how and if it can be brought around. Audouze method? You can very gently siphon a mature wine off its sediment into a clean bottle or narrow decanter, and even sparge both vessels while you’re at it. You might want moderate or aggressive oxygen exposure. There’s even those aerator pourer thingies.

If there is time for it, as there always is if you plan ahead, I certainly think that the Audoze method is preferable. That is the method I would use if it is important that I serve a particular wine at a particular time. Ordinarily, however, I just pop and pour. If the wine does not show right and I suspect a flaw that might be reduced by a bit of air, I would set it aside for a bit of slow-ox treatment and pick up another to drink.

Wes Barton:

What doesn’t make sense is to accept a winemaker using a practice to resolve an issue at some point before bottling, and de-legitimize the same practice to resolve the same issue on the consumer end.

No it wouldn’t make sense so that’s not what I do. When winemakers consciously give wine oxygen, they do so in very small amounts over a long period of time. Decanting is the very opposite of that.

Wes Barton:

If X amount of oxygen is required for a wine to show its best, you certainly don’t want 5X, nor do you want .1X. If pouring that wine into a certain decanter means the wine will approach X amount of oxygen in 3 hours, what’s wrong with that? Wouldn’t the wine be too reductive for the negative changes you cite to occur in that time range?

I think the Audoze method (slow oxygen exposure) minimizes the risk that there will be any noticeable negative side effects of the aeration.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
A splash racking is definitely not “very small amounts (of oxygen) over a long period of time.” It’s an aggressive adjustment. A naturally reductive variety such as Syrah may need it. A wine being aged in tank, rather than barrel, may become reductive. The point here is if you know how reductive a wine is, you know how much oxygen you can safely introduce to normalize it. It doesn’t matter if it’s a baby in tank or barrel or a baby in bottle relative to its anticipated drinking window. What matters is to attain a goal, a significant amount of oxygen is needed.

Why the Audouze method works has been argued, but it’s obviously the point to minimize oxidative effects relative to other processes that occur once a cork is pulled on very old wines. Not really relevant to wines needing a lot of oxygen.

(An interesting experiment would be to rack a very old wine off its sediment into a larger vessel, without any exposure to oxygen. You could even simultaneously transfer to two vessels, one with significant headspace, on with dime-sized oxygen exposure, and leave a little for an immediate sampling to gauge the “before”, too.)

A wine made where it’ll begin to peak in 25 years will definitely not be a wine that’ll show its best at 5 years. At maturity, it most likely wouldn’t need a decant, and could easily suffer from it. At 5 years, it probably needs significant oxygen exposure to show well.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
I just reread the section on sulfur off-odors in the book on wine science by Jackson that I’ve already quoted (3d ed., pp. 499-502). It says that “electively removing hydrogen sulfide and mercaptans from wine is complicated” (p. 501) and doesn’t mention splash racking as a viable technique. The reason might be that it isn’t. Here’s a quote from a fairly recent article studying several techniques to reduce VSC (Volatile Sulfur Compounds).

“An aerative or splash racking technique was employed, which involved recirculating the wine in a 400-L conical-bottomed stainless steel tank and splashing the wine around the sides of the tank, which increased the DO 100-fold to approximately 4000 μg/L. No statistical difference was measured between VSC concentration of the Control and of the oxygenated wine at any of the time points investigated (Figure 3).”

http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ajgw.12172/full

And no, a wine successfully made to show its best at age 25 will by definition not do so at five. But that does not mean that the lack of sufficient age can be compensated by simply exposing the young wine to a heavy dosis of oxygen upon opening and then wait some hours or days. If it were that simple, noone would be particularly interested in aging wine over long periods of time or buying old age-worthy wines at the very high prices they usually command.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

I just reread the section on sulfur off-odors in the book on wine science by Jackson that I’ve already quoted (3d ed., pp. 499-502). It says that “electively removing hydrogen sulfide and mercaptans from wine is complicated” (p. 501) and doesn’t mention splash racking as a viable technique. The reason might be that it isn’t.

You continue to speak with a tone of authority while doing nothing more than demonstrating your ignorance. This makes you seem incredibly foolish. I am sure you will refer me back to the one article you cited, as if one very limited study is proof of anything, further demonstrating your inability to correctly assess information. I have trouble believing that you are able to take yourself seriously while saying such stupid things.

From Concepts in Wine Chemistry by Yair Margalit, Ph.D.:
“If hydrogen sulfide was produced during fermentation (in the case of nitrogen deficiency or due to other reasons), it should be removed from the wine as soon as possible. The simplest way to do it, is by aeration (and some oxidation) namely, the whole volume of the wine is pumped from one tank to another one (or the same tank) while the wine is splashed over the top of the receiving tank. In most cases this measure is enough and the “stinking” wine looses its off-odor right after this operation.”

Of course, there’s no citation for that, even though the book references original research to back up many other assertions, likely because this is so widely known that Margalit didn’t think it necessary. As this book is just a summary of other people’s research and knowledge, it’s just as authoritative as a lot of what you’ve referenced, claiming equal peer review on all publications by reputable publishers, which is laughable, but there it is.

Carry on with pretending to be an authority on this topic. I’m done here.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
Once a wine has developed a significant VSC problem it is difficult to remove. The point of racking or sparging a wine, particularly a variety like Syrah that is prone to the problem, it to get it off the lees which promote VSC formation (in the case of racking), to reduce levels of VSCs when they are still low, and to get oxygen into the wine to make it less reductive.

If you read the paper that Anders cited, the main point of the article is the value of introducing oxygen into the wine to avoid VSC problems. Aeration won’t solve a full-blown VSC problem, but it is very useful for avoiding the problem in the first place.

To quote the conclusion of the paper: “The risk of producing a wine with high VSC concentration that negatively impacts wine aroma as well as with a low concentration of fermentation products associated with positive red fruit aromas was decreased by treating wines with O2 during fermentation”.

-Al

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
In case it’s of interest, I just did an experiment with a small tasting group I’m part of last week that addresses this issue. We had three wines, a Clos du Papes '03, a Malescot St Exupery '83, and a Beaune Greves '72 from Pierre-Yves Masson, all from magnum. Using a coravin, all four of us had five small glasses of each wine (all from the same bottle, to prevent bottle variation), poured at the following intervals before tasting:

  1. 6 hours
  2. 3 hours
  3. 1 hour
  4. 15 minutes
  5. 1 minute

Each of the wines were tasted blind, with stickers under the base that identified wach wine (we all jumbled up the order for one another so we didn’t know which was which). We also made the notes blind and listed the glasses in order of preference, before comparing. For the Clos du Papes and Malescot we all(!) agreed that 4) and 5) were the best (three of us placed 4 on top, one 5). After that most of us preferred 3 with 2 and 1 taking an equal share of last place. For the Beaune, 3 and 4 shared top spot, followed by the others in no clear order (the wine was slightly maderised so the air probably had less of an effect on the wine).

From this we surmised that decanting for more than a little while might do more harm than good. However, the preponderance of 4 of the top spot indicated that a little air was beneficial to each wine, allowing it to open up. In my opinion the CdP closed down drastically, and the Malescot became less complex, with prolonged air exposure. Although this is of course in no way a conclusive result, I did feel somewhat reassured; I will continue slow-oxing all my bottles for 4 hours or so wherever possible in the future (this is the method I think is most closely replicates 15 minutes oxygen exposure in the glass).

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked: Congratulations on a nicely designed little experiment as well as a good report of it Eric. I particularly appreciate the smart way in which you managed to eliminate bottle variation (always to be reckoned with and particularly so with rather old to very old bottles like these) while still varying the in-glass “decanting” time by means of the Coravin. Your report is very to the point as well. Short but still including all the pertinent details. A pity the Beaune was a bit oxidized (“maderized”) already in the bottle but such things are hardly unheard of, especially for 40+ year old wines, and I think you draw the right conclusion as to why the outcome was slightly different in that particular case.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Mike Grammer:

Tasted quickly a 2008 Bouchard Meursault Genevrieres and 2012 Kutch McDougall Pinot Noir that I opened last night. The Kutch I have left open to slow-ox, the Bouchard I recorked this morning. Also have open a halfsie of 2001 Rieussec that has been opened since Monday night with slow-ox and tasted initially then. On this thread I will report any differences from my initial impressions.

Sorry for the delay in reporting—my life has been seeing films morning, noon and night for the past 5 days.

The 08 Bouchard Genevrieres–when opened, very crystal clear, clean and sharply defined. Next day, it was so much more expressive, plenty of fruit and some toffee accents and very much the wine that I hoped to get and have loved in the past.

The 2012 Kutch McDougall was earthy and quite structured on opening. Tasted after a full day’s slow-ox, it shows lovely expressiveness too, still retaining the structure and earthy/root components, but balancing them with fine red cherry and berry fruit. For me, the wine definitely benefitted from air.

The 01 Rieussec was fairly closed when I opened it although it promised much. The 2.5 days later, the Botrytis is in full ascendancy with this wine now (out of half) but the greatness of the wine is that there is so much underneath in terms of honey, coconut oil, tropical fruit and vanilla. Took the leavings down the next day to my friend Heather, and we loved the deliciousness of the last sips.

This is only my opinion, but none of these wines suffered at all and were, in fact, markedly better for the air time I gave them. But what do I know?

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Alan C h a n:

Anders, out of curiosity, what is your take on wines made in an oxidative style? Are there some you enjoy?

Well, it depends a bit on exactly what you take “oxidative style” as well as “enjoy” to mean. I certainly don’t seek out wines made in such a style but I don’t mind having a glass of sherry and keep a bottle in the fridge for cooking purposes. Also, I enjoyed the one Vin Santo I can remember trying but wouldn’t think it was made in a very oxidative style (and Vin Santo can vary quite a bit in this regard according to what I have read).

If we shift to more oxidative versus more reductive wine-making in general, I don’t think I know much enough about how the wines I enjoy more and those I enjoy less vary in that regard to tell which way I tend to like best. What I do know is that I have, with time, tended to drift away from wines that, at least to my perception, are made to have an “aged” character already on release, presumably by giving them quite a bit of air at one stage or another. What I also know is that it has become increasingly important to me that I perceive the wine as pure, clean, and fresh, and I would think I am less likely to perceive a wine that way if the producer has chosen to give it a lot of oxygen. This of course shouldn’t be taken to mean that I don’t like properly aged wine. On the contrary, quite a few of the bottles I cellar are there in order to mature rather than to be drunk within the next few years.

posts.name: Alan C h a n
posts.cooked:
I had in mind wines like Lopez de Heredia, that get controlled oxidation, on through to Jura wines, etc. I’d be curious to know whether the effect of air time on your enjoyment of those wines is any different.

It strikes me that, as you’ve stated, you are more sensitive than most to certain effects of opening the bottle upon wines, leading to things like what you describe as acetaldehyde. Others are less sensitive, or at least less bothered, by some of these effects. So even though It may be true for you that wines rarely get better with more than nominal air time, it is not necessarily so for others. I think you are partially right in that I do think wine geeks have become ‘trained’ to think decanting or some kind of extended air time works to make wines taste “better”, but as many on this thread have admitted, that is not always the case. I do believe there is a lot of truth to the claim that the taster may change more than the wine actually does, in many cases.

That said, it also strikes me that you are so wedded to the scientific aspect of wine that you are ignoring the ‘art’ side of things. The truth is, there’s so much science going on (not sure if I will use the right terminology, as I’m not a scientist, but basically there are so many chemical compounds changing at once, undergoing so much change, that we don’t understand from a chemical standpoint. What the air does to what a taster experiences as cherry, or earth, or tar, or sour, or sweet, need not scientifically be getting worse from an aesthetic enjoyment standpoint.). What you automatically view as deterioration from a scientific standpoint does not necessarily means “tastes worse”, especially when you factor in that how good the wine tastes is a complex mix of all these, and then there’s each different taster’s differing personal chemistry, the effect of alcohol upon their system over time, interaction with food, psychological effects of the social setting, etc. Just because these other factors exist does not mean the phenomenon of “tastes better now” is all fallacy. There is still so much we don’t understand about wines from a scientific standpoint that we don’t know why some wines get better with age, why some wines ‘shut down’ for a period, etc., and denying the art part of it, or at least what we don’t know about the science part, seems to be your blind spot. You appear at this point to be so committed to your thesis, and being this bringer of a great truth and opening everyone else’s eyes, that you are forgetting to listen for and observe anything that might contradict your thesis.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Alan C h a n:

And no, a wine successfully made to show its best at age 25 will by definition not do so at five. But that does not mean that the lack of sufficient age can be compensated by simply exposing the young wine to a heavy dosis of oxygen upon opening and then wait some hours or days. If it were that simple, noone would be particularly interested in aging wine over long periods of time or buying old age-worthy wines at the very high prices they usually command.

Yes, Captain Obvious. Without bothering to parse my previous posts, I’m pretty sure I made it clear I’m not a fan of cradle robbing for that very reason. My point, which you ignore, is that to show their best at that very sub-optimal point in their evolution, they tend to need significant oxygen.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
Alan C h a n:

You appear at this point to be so committed to your thesis, and being this bringer of a great truth and opening everyone else’s eyes, that you are forgetting to listen for and observe anything that might contradict your thesis.

Your post suggests that there is a considerable part of agreement between us. And I am pretty sure there is even more of it than you think.

When you use the term “art” to refer to things we don’t know on a scientific basis, I think you might be borrowing the term from this article by Jamie Goode. And I find little reason to disagree with what he says there.

Therefore, I am a bit surprised when you conclude your post with the sentence quoted above. Could you please examplify what I have said in this thread (or failed to say) that gives you the impression that I forget to listen to or observe anything that might contradict “my thesis”, a thesis of which I claim no ownership but simply reflects the current position of wine science (and which like any other generalization about the real world can potentially be shown wrong by future studies). Please try to be as specific as possible since that will make it easier for me to understand and comment on what you say.

There are over a thousand aromatic compounds that can be found in wines. We have several hundred different aroma receptors. Each of which very from person to person, from null to significant. A single compound may be received by more than one receptor type, in some cases, meaning more variation than just the “volume” of the message received. Many perceived smells are actually composites of more than one compound that our brains have been trained to group together. On a different sniff, your brain may group the same inputs differently, and certainly different people perceive the same smells differently. Some people will lack some significant receptor entirely, others are unusually sensitive. On the brain (data processing) side, most people’s brains have been taught, at some point, to be particularly attentive to certain smells. They can trigger some pleasant memory or a negative reaction. My brother can’t get passed his brain associating wine with rotting fruit. Of course, whatever ancient experience it was that triggers that, it’s not directly the ferment-y aspect of that rotting fruit that was so off-putting, but it surely accentuated it and got associated with it. Many of us have more specific, seemingly random, sensitivities/triggers that’ll ruin an otherwise perfectly good wine - for us, and no one else in a group tasting.

I know people who can’t perceive TCA. I know people who are completely put off by the slightest touch of VA. I’ll like a little EA in some wines where half the group is put off by it. With other wines I’ll find it absolutely disgusting, while everyone else likes the wine, and most can’t even identify it’s there. Perception and appreciation are very complex.

I’m sensitive to bitterness. So, it’s weird when people who complain about green notes in less ripe wines, as well as pyrazines, just fawn over a highly extracted wine that, to me, is the extreme in harsh concentrated sappy greenness, bitter and astringent. Can’t get more extremely green than that, but it’s a different green, and they don’t perceive it at all. (Though, anecdotally, I’ve had a few of the same wines with moderate age, where they’d shed their primary fruit, and no one found them enjoyable. So, that seems like a relativity of perception issue, where it’s the data processing rather than the receptors blocking them from perceiving the harsh bitterness in the young wines.)

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

Yes, Captain Obvious. Without bothering to parse my previous posts, I’m pretty sure I made it clear I’m not a fan of cradle robbing for that very reason. My point, which you ignore, is that to show their best at that very sub-optimal point in their evolution, they tend to need significant oxygen.

I didn’t ignore that point. As you would have seen if you had bothered to parse prior posts, I questioned it, here and here.

As I have tried to indicate already, there is to my knowledge little empirical support for the proposition that finished (in the sense of bottled) wine needs much oxygen at all. Much of the aging process can take place anaerobically. What is currently still unclear is whether the very small amount of oxygen ingress allowed by a really good cork remains beneficial or not. But even if minute amounts of air supplied over many years would indeed be beneficial to at least some wines, I have seen nothing to suggest that this extremely slow oxygenation can be made up for by a massive dosis of oxygen allowed to act for a few hours or days after opening the bottle.

Here’s what Andy Waterhouse, professor of wine chemistry at UC Davis has to say about the matter. Let me quote the most relevant passage:

"Mr. Waterhouse thought the elimination of sulfur aromas is all that these accessories — or, for that matter, aeration — had to offer.

‘A number of sulfur compounds are present in wine in traces and have an impact on flavor because they’re very potent,’ he said. ‘Some are unpleasant and some contribute to a wine’s complexity. You can certainly dispose of these in five minutes with a little oxygen and a small area of metal catalyst to speed the reactions up, and change your impression of the wine.’

But Mr. Waterhouse maintained that no brief treatment could convert the tannins to less astringent, softer forms, not even an hour in a decanter.

‘You can saturate a wine with oxygen by sloshing it into a decanter, but then the oxygen just sits there,’ he said. ‘It reacts very slowly. To change the tannins perceptibly in an hour, you would have to hit the wine with pure oxygen, high pressure and temperature, and powdered iron with a huge catalytic surface area.’

So why do people think decanting softens a wine’s astringency?

‘I think that this impression of softening comes from the loss of the unpleasant sulfur compounds, which reduces our overall perception of harshness,’ Mr. Waterhouse said.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

I’m sensitive to bitterness. So, it’s weird when people who complain about green notes in less ripe wines, as well as pyrazines, just fawn over a highly extracted wine that, to me, is the extreme in harsh concentrated sappy greenness, bitter and astringent. Can’t get more extremely green than that, but it’s a different green, and they don’t perceive it at all. (Though, anecdotally, I’ve had a few of the same wines with moderate age, where they’d shed their primary fruit, and no one found them enjoyable. So, that seems like a relativity of perception issue, where it’s the data processing rather than the receptors blocking them from perceiving the harsh bitterness in the young wines.)

I apologize for the thread drift, but in over 20 years of online wine discussion, I don’t recall ever having seen an association of extraction with greenness in this way, nor had I ever made the connection myself. I can think of a few wines off the top of my head where I picked up a significant greenness mixed with intensely ripe fruit (overripe to my taste) and very harsh tannin, and wonder whether I was mistakenly attributing all of the bitterness to the tannin.

Other experienced tasters I was drinking with didn’t comment on the greenness, though some expressed dislike for the ripeness and tannin, nor have I seen many references to greenness in tasting notes for the wines. The clearest examples I can recall are 2005 and 2009 Pavie and a couple of K Vintners syrahs. I found each extremely unpleasant and specifically noted my incredulity that a wine with such ripe, extracted fruit could be so unpleasantly green (as if the winemaker macerated rotting grass clippings in cough syrup with a few handfuls of powdered tannin is one description I recall).

I’m embarrassed to admit that it never crossed my mind that the greenness I picked up may be attributable to a particular compound or compounds that I may be sensitive to, as I’ve lectured friends about differing physiological thresholds to TCA, VA, pyrazines, and other compounds.

Thanks, Wes, for giving me something to think about.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked: I am a bit surprised that you haven’t seen this relationship spelled out before Mike. It’s hardly an unusual topic as far as I can tell. How you extract/macerate (e.g., with regard to timing, temperature, alcohol and “agitation” of one kind or another) as well as for how long you do it determine how much you extract as well as exactly what you extract. Some of the phenolic compounds are good and you want those extracted whereas others are not so good and therefore unwanted. Furthermore, grapes can be very ripe, even overripe, in some ways (sugar, fruit aromas, acid) while still not being ripe phenologically. This is a problem that wine makers have to struggle to avoid all the time, especially in very hot years, like 2003.

Why the need for quotation marks around theoretical and practical? My observation is simply that practical wine making knowledge sure goes a long way into helping better understand the practical wine tasting experience. Now, I have limited wine making experience (my end product was truly revolting) but it has been my experience that winemakers tend to really understand why their own wines taste a certain way and what you can do to possibly enhance the drinking experience. For them, it’s a closed loop experiment that they get to perform year in and year out.

That being said, I don’t believe any amount of scientific research/reports will ever influence how the vast majority of people drink wine (“do-decanters” vs “don’t-decanters”). No matter how many studies you trot out, you simply want change some people’s minds.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
I put quotation marks around “theoretical” because I don’t know of any wine knowledge that is purely theoretical. What science knows about the matter is based on empirical studies. By implication, I put quotation marks around “practical” too since I am not sure what you think of as the polar opposite of something that, to my mind, does not exist.

As to wine tasting, I agree with you that some knowledge about wine making may be helpful in making sense of your experience as a wine drinker. But I think that’s true quite irrespective of whether the knowledge is footed in your own practical experiences with wine making or not. What matters is whether the knowledge is actually knowledge rather than mistaken beliefs. As to professional wine makers, I have a lot of respect for what they know while at the same time recognizing that they too are fallible, just like the rest of us. There are reasons to think that many professional wine makers agree or they wouldn’t send their heirs to school to learn more about oenology and viticulture as many of them now do.

Right now, there’s an interesting parallel thread running entitled “The Myth of Native Yeast”. As I am sure you are aware, many wine makers these days like to tell us that they prefer to use native as opposed to commercial yeasts. As the article referenced in the OP of that thread informs us, however, what wine makers think they are using may not be the same as what they actually use. And it would be difficult for the wine makers to know that such is the case without laboratory measurements.

As to decanting practices, you might be right that most wine drinkers are unlikely to change their habits. I can’t say I care much. Everyone is of course free to do as he or she sees fit.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Eric Egan:

In case it’s of interest, I just did an experiment with a small tasting group I’m part of last week that addresses this issue. We had three wines, a Clos du Papes '03, a Malescot St Exupery '83, and a Beaune Greves '72 from Pierre-Yves Masson, all from magnum. Using a coravin, all four of us had five small glasses of each wine (all from the same bottle, to prevent bottle variation), poured at the following intervals before tasting:

  1. 6 hours
  2. 3 hours
  3. 1 hour
  4. 15 minutes
  5. 1 minute

Each of the wines were tasted blind, with stickers under the base that identified wach wine (we all jumbled up the order for one another so we didn’t know which was which). We also made the notes blind and listed the glasses in order of preference, before comparing. For the Clos du Papes and Malescot we all(!) agreed that 4) and 5) were the best (three of us placed 4 on top, one 5). After that most of us preferred 3 with 2 and 1 taking an equal share of last place. For the Beaune, 3 and 4 shared top spot, followed by the others in no clear order (the wine was slightly maderised so the air probably had less of an effect on the wine).

From this we surmised that decanting for more than a little while might do more harm than good. However, the preponderance of 4 of the top spot indicated that a little air was beneficial to each wine, allowing it to open up. In my opinion the CdP closed down drastically, and the Malescot became less complex, with prolonged air exposure. Although this is of course in no way a conclusive result, I did feel somewhat reassured; I will continue slow-oxing all my bottles for 4 hours or so wherever possible in the future (this is the method I think is most closely replicates 15 minutes oxygen exposure in the glass).

A great experiment.

But I’m not surprised that those wines didn’t benefit from extended decanting. Grenache is oxidative by nature, so it’s risky to leave a CdP with some age out too long. And 2003 is such a weird year everywhere, it’s hard to draw conclusions.

If the 83 had been a super second or first growth Bordeaux – or an 83 Northern Rhone or an 82 Barolo – I think the result might be different.

Interesting that the Beaune benefited from a little more time. At that age, I’d be wary of extended decanting with a Burgundy.

None of this is meant as criticism; just some thoughts on why things came out the way they did. I applaud you for the experiment.

posts.name: Stan Stevenson
posts.cooked:
Put 3 glasses of water on the table. One hot, one cold and one room temp. Stick your left finger in the cold water than into the room temp one. The room temp feels warm. Now try the same procedure with your right finger starting with the hot water, then the room temp. Now the very same water feels cool.

Perception does not exist in a vacuum. It is all affected by what preceded it. So to say a wine improves with air, think of this experiment.

In my experience, some bottles improve a little with a aeration, many improve a great deal, some not at all, and a small percentage actually get worse. Your little experiment has absolutely no correlation with my experience. I should also note that, in my experience, many bottles need more than a few hours of decanting to show improvement.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
But perhaps you recall seeing statements summarizing available evidence about the matter, like those of Ronald S. Jackson and Andrew L. Waterhouse. If you have evidence that their summary is wrong, please feel free to provide it. Perhaps you additionally saw some actual experiments being reported, like for example the one in the OP of the thread. If you know of evidence that clearly contradicts the results of such experiments, please feel free to provide it.

I saw the quotes from a couple of editions of Jackson’s text. Here is the same section from the Fourth Edition (from a section on wine assessment, not quite the same as wine enjoyment).

Opening bottles in advance to “breathe” is usually unnecessary and, from my perspective, undesirable. The limited wine/air interface generated by cork remove is minimal. Even decanting, which exposes the wine to more air, does not rapidly generate noticeable changes. However, the equilibrium between weakly bound aromatics in the wine and headspace gases in the bottle begins to shift upon opening as aromatics disperse into the surrounding air. This results in their increased liberation from the wine - what is informally termed the wine’s “opening” - the initiation of development (see Herson et al., 2012). It is not infrequently noted in young, finer quality wines.

As one of a wine’s more fascinating attributes, it should not be missed by having it occur unnoticed in a decanter. It should be allowed to progressively reveal itself is the wine is periodically swirled and sampled during assessment. Commercial devices, designed to speed this process, have the same logic as rushing to the end of a murder mystery to find out prematurely “who done it”. With very old wines, the fragrance may be so immediate and evanescent that it may dissipate completely within a few minutes. In this situation, assessment needs to commence as soon as possible after opening, decanting being conducted only when and if needed, to separate clear wine from any sediment that may have accumulated.

Hirson, GD, Heymann H, Eberler, SE, 2012. Equilibration time and glass shape effects on chemical and sensory properties of wine. Am J Enol Vitic 63, 515-521.

That’s not a summary of available evidence, certainly not of systemic empirical studies, nor are any such studies cited in his references. As written, he’s stating an opinion. To be sure, it’s an opinion of an experienced person who understands some of the relevant wine science. But, it’s still an opinion much the same as others have expressed in the thread.

You have posited that there must be studies behind his opinion, but he doesn’t summarize, cite, or even refer to them. There have been some “one-off” experiments conducted and I have noticed those described in the thread (although I recall they came to different conclusions depending on who conducted them). But, I don’t think systematic and comprehensive studies have been conducted. They would be difficult and expensive to conduct for a wide variety of wines of various ages. Also, as you and others have noted, it’s ultimately about what we perceive when we smell and taste a wine, and our individual sensitivities and preferences vary widely. This would make any such studies even more difficult, and ultimately not universal except in some average way for an average taster. In any case, before you can ask people to refute these studies, we need to have them cited.

I actually agree with many of the scientific things you have posted, and mine own decanting practice is not terribly different. In particular, I think the changes that happen after a wine has been decanted or poured is likely more about volatiles and loosely bound molecules being liberated (both dissipating unpleasant aromas and liberating interesting aromas), and about perceptual changes over the course of drinking, than about specific chemical reactions. Where I disagree is when you take these facts and some quotes and synthesize a definitive statement that established science supports your opinions as opposed to those of others.

As far as my own practice, with younger wines, I generally open and pour a glass, then start drinking a short time later (eg, after cooking dinner). I like to follow the development over a long period (recognizing that some of the changes are in my palate as much as the wine). In my experience, some young wines can even clamp down and become less expressive after a period of opening. I splash decant young wines with noticeable CO2. I decant older wines I expect may have developed sediment. I generally do this shortly before starting to drink the wine, with a few exceptions. One exception might be older Northern Rhone from a bigger year, eg, a 1990 Hermitage. But I wouldn’t do this terribly far in advance. The most notable exception, which has been mentioned in the thread, is with old Nebbiolo based wines like Baroli or Barbareschi. As has been noted, these often develop a very fine sediment that settles extremely slowly and is also rather unpleasant. In addition, they also often present some off-odors when first opened that dissipate over time. FWIW, this isn’t referring to 10 or 20 year old Piemonte wines, but 40 to 50 year old wines like from the great vintages of the 1960s.

-Al

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Al Osterheld:

I saw the quotes from a couple of editions of Jackson’s text. Here is the same section from the Fourth Edition (from a section on wine assessment, not quite the same as wine enjoyment).

Is there anything in the quoted passage that makes you think that whatever distinction might exist between enjoyment and assessment is of any importance in the present context?

Al Osterheld:

That’s not a summary of available evidence, certainly not of systemic empirical studies, nor are any such studies cited in his references. As written, he’s stating an opinion. To be sure, it’s an opinion of an experienced person who understands some of the relevant wine science. But, it’s still an opinion much the same as others have expressed in the thread.

The book in question is an academic textbook, meant for teaching the subject. In books of that kind, specific references are as a rule not provided for matters where there is a scientific consensus. They are provided only when there is controversy, where the findings are new and not yet widely known, when the evidence is not yet so clear, and so on.

Al Osterheld:

There have been some “one-off” experiments conducted and I have noticed those described in the thread (although I recall they came to different conclusions depending on who conducted them).

Which ones reported in this thread would you say lead to conclusions that are at clear variance with that in the OP?

Al Osterheld:

But, I don’t think systematic and comprehensive studies have been conducted. They would be difficult and expensive to conduct for a wide variety of wines of various ages.

Of course, it can always be argued that further evidence is needed because the variation in terms of wine type and age is insufficient. In this case as in others, science cannot provide ultimate truths. But, again, do you know of any studies that seriously contradict Jackson’s, Waterhouse’s or Peynaud’s claims or the results of an experiment like that in the OP?

Al Osterheld:

Also, as you and others have noted, it’s ultimately about what we perceive when we smell and taste a wine, and our individual sensitivities and preferences vary widely. This would make any such studies even more difficult, and ultimately not universal except in some average way for an average taster. In any case, before you can ask people to refute these studies, we need to have them cited.

Even though perceptions of wine characteristics do vary between individuals, sometimes dramatically, this does not exclude the possibility that there is so much commonality with regard to certain effects (e.g., that of prolonged aeration) that it is possible to generalize without much reservation, as both Jackson and Waterhouse do.

Al Osterheld:

I actually agree with many of the scientific things you have posted, and mine own decanting practice is not terribly different. In particular, I think the changes that happen after a wine has been decanted or poured is likely more about volatiles and loosely bound molecules being liberated (both dissipating unpleasant aromas and liberating interesting aromas), and about perceptual changes over the course of drinking, than about specific chemical reactions. Where I disagree is when you take these facts and some quotes and synthesize a definitive statement that established science supports your opinions as opposed to those of others.

As I have stressed on multiple occasions, the statements in question are not definitive in the sense of claiming to be the ultimate truth. They just reflect the standard position of wine science at the moment and remain open to question just like any other general statement about the real world.

Al Osterheld:

As far as my own practice, with younger wines, I generally open and pour a glass, then start drinking a short time later (eg, after cooking dinner). I like to follow the development over a long period (recognizing that some of the changes are in my palate as much as the wine). In my experience, some young wines can even clamp down and become less expressive after a period of opening. I splash decant young wines with noticeable CO2. I decant older wines I expect may have developed sediment. I generally do this shortly before starting to drink the wine, with a few exceptions. One exception might be older Northern Rhone from a bigger year, eg, a 1990 Hermitage. But I wouldn’t do this terribly far in advance. The most notable exception, which has been mentioned in the thread, is with old Nebbiolo based wines like Baroli or Barbareschi. As has been noted, these often develop a very fine sediment that settles extremely slowly and is also rather unpleasant. In addition, they also often present some off-odors when first opened that dissipate over time. FWIW, this isn’t referring to 10 or 20 year old Piemonte wines, but 40 to 50 year old wines like from the great vintages of the 1960s.

My own practice is not much different. When drinking plans are known/made ahead of time, I would open at least certain types of wine several hours ahead of drinking, pour myself a small sip, and then leave the wine alone. However, if there is time, I prefer not to decant, even for the purpose of getting rid of CO2, but simply leave the bottle open. Also, when drinking Baroli or Barbareschi that are old enough to have a significant amount of sediment, I have yet to notice that it tastes bad (bitter) but this may of course be a matter of individual perception. That said, I of course prefer to drink the wine without having sediment mixed in it to the extent possible.

posts.name: Anders Westholm

I’ve previously stated my credentials in reference to my knowledge of scientific textbooks (which are indeed considerably lengthened by citation). You, on the other hand, are not a natural scientist. I believe you study political science. Please correct me if I am wrong.

As I have previously pointed out, and as you would know if you had any experience with science, disputes are not settled by means of credentials but by substantive arguments and evidence. Further, since wine science must by necessity rest on natural science as well as behavioral science, why would a natural scientist have stronger credentials when talking about it than a behavioral scientist? Finally, I did not claim that textbooks are not lengthened by citation. What I claimed is that if they would provide references for every claim made, they would be much much longer. Now since we agree that they don’t, as a rule, provide references for things that are, as you put it, considered fact, i.e., claims that are considered uncontroversial, I see no reason to discuss this point any further.
