posts.cooked:
John Morris:
Anders Westholm:
Pat Burton:

Yes, I corrected my typo. I am not surprised you misunderstood my meaning though. I meant to imply that the text you cite is not a good example of a scientific textbook. I certainly did not mean to imply that anything about wine science is considered fact.

Thanks for making your position clear at long last. It follows from that position, i.e., your claim that nothing about wine science can be considered fact, that academic textbooks on the subject should not be published, regardless of whether they provide references for each and every claim or not. It also follows that academic institutions teaching the subject, such as the department of viticulture and enology and UC Davis, should be closed. While you are entitled to your opinion, I find no reason to discuss wine science with someone who does not even consider such a science a possibility.

Now you’re being silly, Anders. All he’s saying is that the book in question is not a reliable source.

If anyone here is being silly, it is certainly not I. What Pat Burton said is exactly what I quoted above: That he did not mean to imply that anything about wine science is considered fact. That is clearly a statement about wine science is general, not a statement about a specific book about wine science. And the implications are those I spelled out. If you think differently, please tell us why.

Now if Pat Burton actually thinks that wine science is OK but that the book on the subject by Ronald S. Jackson is not a reliable source on it, then he had better a) provide a substantive basis for distrusting this particular book, and b) point to a better source. He has so far done neither.

Furthermore, as you can see if you carefully read the exchange between Pat and me above, we do not disagree about how textbooks in general handle citations, i.e., that specific references are omitted for claims that are considered to be widely known as well as uncontroversial. So the reason why he thinks Jackson’s book is poor is that, in his view, wine science, unlike other sciences, has failed to come up with any sufficiently well established facts for references to be omitted anywhere in Jackson’s book. If that would actually be true, it would certainly not make much sense to teach the subject at academic institutions or write a textbook about it.

Let me repeat a few words from a prior post. Perhaps you are familiar with Tom Stevenson. If not, you find some information about him here. This is what he has to say in his review of Jackson’s book:

“This is my bible because nobody gets close to Jackson in broadening the science of wine without diluting it, extending this vast and diverse subject back to the origin of vines and forward to wine laws, geographic origin, terroir, sensory perception and health issues.”

What do you think about the book yourself? Do you consider it a reliable source? If not, on which grounds?

Finally, Jackson’s claims are largely in line with those of at least two other wine scientists who have made statements about the topic of this thread: Andrew L. Waterhouse and Emile Peynaud. So even if we, for some unknown reason, should distrust Jackson, that does not help much in undermining his claims.

link_counts.title: Tom Stevenson - Wikipedia

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Oliver McCrum:

There are two possibilities, Anders, when you find yourself disagreeing with almost everyone in a discussion; either everyone else is wrong, or…hmmm.

Yes, there are those two possibilities. But in what way is that relevant in deciding what the correct position is?

Because I do hope that your intention is not to provide an example of this infamous type of argument, which, if it had been taken seriously by everyone, would never have allowed scientific progress to take place. Many scientific claims have been, and still are, met with distrust by the majority. A well-known example is the transition from the geocentric to the heliocentric view of the planetary system. Regrettably, it was taken sufficiently seriously by sufficiently many in that case for the individual who first insisted on the heliocentric view, Giordano Bruno, to be burnt to death. Fortunately, his efforts were not in vain and the heliocentric was eventually accepted by close to everyone.

I also do hope that you realize that this is not a dispute between me personally and other wine berserkers although it may look that way. It is a dispute between a set of wine scientists and a set of wine berserkers. What I am doing is simply to report what the wine scientists are saying. And quite a few wine berserkers apparently find it more convenient to attack me rather than the scientists whose message I am relaying.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Wine Berserkers!

posts.name: Pat Burton
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
Pat Burton:

Yes, I corrected my typo. I am not surprised you misunderstood my meaning though. I meant to imply that the text you cite is not a good example of a scientific textbook. I certainly did not mean to imply that anything about wine science is considered fact.

Thanks for making your position clear at long last. It follows from that position, i.e., your claim that nothing about wine science can be considered fact, that academic textbooks on the subject should not be published, regardless of whether they provide references for each and every claim or not. It also follows that academic institutions teaching the subject, such as the department of viticulture and enology and UC Davis, should be closed. While you are entitled to your opinion, I find no reason to discuss wine science with someone who does not even consider such a science a possibility.

I did not state that there are no facts in wine science. You made an assumption about my comment and then took it to an absurd level. That is not a reasonable method for conversation. It is a means of bullying to quiet those you disagree with.

Allow me to clarify for you what I meant, since this is apparently necessary. You took my statement that facts in science do not need reference to imply that uncited opinion in the “textbook” you hold out as “the bible” must be fact. I was responding to your claim that if one does not cite, it must be fact. Instead, I argue that the book in question is not a proper scientific textbook. Such texts do not contain editorials and citations are found in every sentence that makes claims of certainty.

For further clarification, the facts I refer to are those that have been studied for decades and to which all questions have been answered (e.g. Mendelian Genetics, Mitosis, Cell Respiration). More recent findings believed to be true are cited despite consensus (e.g. the phylogenetic relationship of animals, the mechanisms by which specific cancers act). A book that alludes to recent findings and claims them as facts is not a proper scientific textbook regardless of what universities/courses/experts provide quotes about and/or use it in their classroom(s).

Of course, one could turn this around and argue that Anders Westholm has mischaracterized a portion(s) of a highly regarded text for his own purposes.

I believe both are at play - the text has been taken out of context and the text does not adhere to the strict definition of a scientific textbook (note here that this does not negate the possibility that the text in question is of considerable value in reference to teaching, etc.). The failure to use proper citations proves the latter, I’m not interested in determining the motivations of Anders Westholm nor reading the text in question further to prove the former.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:
Oliver McCrum:

There are two possibilities, Anders, when you find yourself disagreeing with almost everyone in a discussion; either everyone else is wrong, or…hmmm.

Yes, there are those two possibilities. But in what way is that relevant in deciding what the correct position is?

Because I do hope that your intention is not to provide an example of this infamous type of argument, which, if it had been taken seriously by everyone, would never have allowed scientific progress to take place. Many scientific claims have been, and still are, met with distrust by the majority. A well-known example is the transition from the geocentric to the heliocentric view of the planetary system. Regrettably, it was taken sufficiently seriously by sufficiently many in that case for the individual who first insisted on the heliocentric view, Giordano Bruno, to be burnt to death. Fortunately, his efforts were not in vain and the heliocentric was eventually accepted by close to everyone.

I also do hope that you realize that this is not a dispute between me personally and other wine berserkers although it may look that way. It is a dispute between a set of wine scientists and a set of wine berserkers. What I am doing is simply to report what the wine scientists are saying. And quite a few wine berserkers apparently find it more convenient to attack me rather than the scientists whose message I am relaying.

Yes, Anders, you’re a martyr for science, and we’re the Inquisition.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Oliver,

Why did you leave my question about the relevance of what you said unanswered? If you are not like the Inquisition, why do you make use of argumentum ad populum? And do you actually think that this type of argument (“the majority is always right”) is a proper one? If not, why do you use it?

So far, your only contributions to this thread are an attempt at argumentum ad verecundiam (see here, here and here) followed by an attempt at argumentum ad populum. Is that your normal way of “contributing” to a discussion? Or do you have anything in the way of valid substantive arguments to contribute. If so what?

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Pat Burton:

First, you state that there is no reason to discuss this further with me but then go on to discuss it at length. Please try to be consistent.

I am entirely consistent. I did not discuss the matter further with you after saying I saw no reason to. It is certainly not my fault that you choose to continue the discussion with me, thereby breaking your promise not to engage with me again. Please try to make an effort to avoid these repeated inconsistencies and self-contradictions.

Pat Burton:

Second, I did not state that there are no facts in wine science. You made an assumption about my comment and then took it to an absurd level. That is not a reasonable method for conversation. It is a means of bullying to quiet those you disagree with.

You said exactly what I quoted. And I by no means took it to an absurd level. If there are no “considered facts” in wine science, it makes no sense to teach the subject on an academic level or write textbooks about it. And if you think there are indeed “considered facts” in wine science, facts that do not need specific references in textbook treatments, why didn’t you say so instead of making insinuations to the contrary.

Pat Burton:

Allow me to clarify for you what I meant, since this is apparently necessary. You took my statement that facts in science do not need reference to imply that uncited opinion in the “textbook” you hold out as “the bible” must be fact. I was responding to your claim that if one does not cite, it must be fact. Instead, I argue that the book in question is not a proper scientific textbook. Such texts do not contain editorials and citations are found in every sentence that makes claims of certainty.

On the basis of exactly what evidence are you claiming that Jackson’s book contains “editorials” and that it is not a proper scientific textbook? So far, I haven’t seen you substantiate this in any fashion. Moreover, if Jackson’s book is poor, what better alternative can you point to? And finally, what about Emile Peynaud and Andrew L. Waterhouse? Are they to be discredited as well for saying roughly the same thing as Jackson? If so, on what grounds?

Further, why do you contradict yourself when it comes to how citations are conventionally handled in textbooks. Until now, you have maintained that they are not required for things that are “considered facts”. Now you instead maintain that they are found in every sentence that makes claims of certainty. Things that are “considered facts” are those that come closest to some kind of certainty (although no general statement about the real world is certain). So which way do you want to have it?

Pat Burton:

For further clarification, the facts I refer to are those that have been studied for decades and to which all questions have been answered (e.g. Mendelian Genetics, Mitosis, Cell Respiration). More recent findings believed to be true are cited despite consensus (e.g. the phylogenetic relationship of animals, the mechanisms by which specific cancers act). A book that alludes to recent findings and claims them as facts is not a proper scientific textbook regardless of what universities/courses/experts provide quotes about and/or use it in their classroom(s).

It is never the case in science that all questions have been answered. There are simply uncontroversial claims and controversial or potentially controversial claims.

Pat Burton:

Of course, one could turn this around and argue that Anders Westholm has mischaracterized a portion(s) of a highly regarded text for his own purposes.

I believe both are at play - the text has been taken out of context and the text does not adhere to the strict definition of a scientific textbook (note here that this does not negate the possibility that the text in question is of considerable value in reference to teaching, etc.).

What you personally believe is irrelevant if you can’t substantiate it. And so far you haven’t been able to substantiate either of these things. You have just voiced a subjective opinion without any kind of substantiation. As to the passage from Jackson’s book that I quoted (in this post for those new to this thread), it speaks entirely for itself. It is obvious from the quoted portion itself that I have not quoted it out of context.

Besides, you contradict yourself yet again. On the one hand you say that this is not a proper scientific textbook, on the other hand you claim that it is highly regarded. So which way do you want to have it?

Pat Burton:

The failure to use proper citations proves the latter,

This is something you have yet to show. So far, as already pointed out, it’s nothing but an unsubstantiated subjective opinion of yours.

Pat Burton:

I’m not interested in determining the motivations of Anders Westholm nor reading the text in question further to prove the former.

As already mentioned, the motivations that I or anyone else has for saying what we say are irrelevant for the purpose of determining the validity of what we say. Hence, you are right in not being interested in them. What is not right, though, is to speak about these motivations in spite of not being interested in them, in spite of not being in a position to know them, and in spite of their irrelevance, as you did earlier in the thread.

As to your lack of interest in the text, you contradict yourself yet again. In an earlier post of yours (see here), you wanted further detail about Jackson’s text in order to say anything useful about it. Now you say that you are not even interested in it, yet passing judgment on it.

posts.name: Stan Stevenson
posts.cooked:
Emily Richer:

.

Keep experimenting. Keep notes. I guarantee anyone opens a bottle of my wine - and tastes it right away, then pours half into a decanter, fanning down the sides for max O2 exposure, then gas the other half (I use Private Preserve, argon), leaving it standing upright on the counter next to the decanter, maybe even pour some of the decanted wine into a glass and swirl that every 15 mins to represent hyperO2 version. (Even better if you could siphon to decanter from the standing bottle because the act of pouring out half gave the other half a nice gulp of air…) Taste all three an hour later.

Just did that with a group of seasoned wine folks. P&P won 10 -1 -1.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Two years of this!!
Here is an idea: many of you own or run wine shops with tasting rooms.
Why not promote an experimental tasting along these lines?? It will bring people in and cause lots of conversation.

When we did the experiment–so long ago that Tom Hill was looking for neutrinos in his spare bedroom–I thought certain wines eg Barolo, white Burgundy, would benefit from breathing. Wrong.

When you do the experiment blind then the power of suggestion that a wine improves with breathing goes away.

How many times do I hear people say, Wow, it’s always the last part of the bottle that tastes the best. I wish I had decanted it or decanted it more, etc. Hmm, people are drinking wine…their palates are getting used to tannin…alcohol is seeping into their brains…

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Somewhere way back a comment was made about it depending on the wine.
A particular category I have been wondering about is serious young whites. E.g. 1er Cru Chablis less than say six or seven years old. They often benefit from a double decant if they are to be drunk fairly soon after opening, or in the alternative wait an hour or so after opening which is not always possible. Pop n pour often leaves much of the complexity hidden.
I guess opinions differ. We had interesting experiences a couple of weeks on two successive nights in Quebec City. One sommelier decanted a 2012 Chablis premier Cru without having to be asked. That allowed the complexities to shine through. Another restaurant the next night we were having a 2014 Chablis 1er Cru and I asked for it to be decanted and the reaction of the waiter was not to do it. Due to the circumstances, I did not press the point. , and it was a waste.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: Meant to add it may depend on the particular taster too. Some like secondary or tertiary fkavours of old wines others do not. That preference is likely to affect the preference for breathing or not (whatever the wine in question).

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
I wished Jamie Goode would participate here. He can put the ideas here into coherent science. His prose brings up more questions than answers many times. That to me is science.

Much of what is debated here I would not consider science at all. There are some phenomena that science has difficulty describing. The “breathing” and subsequent textural changes(even color changes) in wine is one of these phenomena.

Last night i opened a bottle of 1989 Lynch Bages. Not even one off aroma but there was very little on the nose. I assume the esters just needed to get some increase in temperature so that they became more volatile with the alcohol. The palate was linear and showed no fruit. With 2 hours, the wine was round. Aromatic. Terrific.

There is something going on here. “This is no humbug.” (The first words after Morton gave one the first anesthetics in the theater at Mass General) Even today we don’t know how anesthetics work really. We have lots of science and are getting closer I think but every few years a new idea comes up. It just brings up more questions. Some new calcium channel in the brain.

Interesting discussion but no one has the “truth” yet to me. Probably never will.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Don,
We 've all had your experience. The question is, What is going on? Is the wine changing or are your senses adapting to the wine?

Not too many people can afford to do my experiment with 29 yo wine, but it could be done.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Mel–most of us who drink 15-30 year old wine frequently (in fact at least 1/2 of the time) don’t open and gradually sip away at the bottle, allowing our palate to adjust. I think the majority of the time, we open and taste (generally but not always a very closed wine, not infrequently something that inexperienced folk would think was dead), then either decant or leave the bottle alone for any number of hours (1 to whatever depending upon the wine). When we go back at that point to drink the wine it has often transformed-even on the first sip. There’s no adapting. The wine has changed significantly.

So while I agree that airing young wines is of no use, the repeated experience of older wines that seem dead on arrival but open dramatically with time being open makes me skeptical that this is a myth. It certainly could be something other than exposure to “air”, or oxygen (maybe just more “space” to equilibrate in, who knows)

Mel - was your experiment validated by Anders?

That should be good for a few pages.

BTW - where’d he go?
Why won’t this thread die!!! Why???

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I don’t know why people won t just agree with me. I am always right.

I did this experiment in the 70s. At the time I thought other retailers would do the experiment and kill the myth but now I realize it is in peoples’ economic interest to keep the myth: you are pouring a wine for people at your winery and it’s their first glass of wine of the day. The impact of a 3.8 pH wine on people who just brushed their teeth cannot be overstated. So you say the wine is great, it opens up after three hours and they should decant an hour or two in advance.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Normally I should not intrude in a discussion which will go to nowhere as many people are convinced that their truth is the truth.

Since 2000, I have drunk 5167 wines having 35 years or more, the age of a wine being calculated as the difference between the year of the wine and the year when I have drunk it. A 1965 drunk in 2000 has the same age as a 1983 drunk in 2018.

And the average age of these wines having 35 years or more is 64 years.

If I say that opening a wine 4 to 5 hours before drinking has an enormous effect on the wine and helps to cure the bad smells and enlarges the wine, I have absolutely no chance to be believed as everyone has his own truth. So I will not say more than that. I have an incredible ratio of good bottles and so many miracles that normally I should be believed. But it is hopeless. Why would I argue when people think that nothing happens?

(If I am so negative it is because this discussion has 11 pages)

posts.name: Stan Stevenson
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Two years of this!!
Here is an idea: many of you own or run wine shops with tasting rooms.
Why not promote an experimental tasting along these lines?? It will bring people in and cause lots of conversation.

When we did the experiment–so long ago that Tom Hill was looking for neutrinos in his spare bedroom–I thought certain wines eg Barolo, white Burgundy, would benefit from breathing. Wrong.

When you do the experiment blind then the power of suggestion that a wine improves with breathing goes away.

How many times do I hear people say, Wow, it’s always the last part of the bottle that tastes the best. I wish I had decanted it or decanted it more, etc. Hmm, people are drinking wine…their palates are getting used to tannin…alcohol is seeping into their brains…

All correct as has been the case over the decades when I’ve done this. It is wine’s biggest myth.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
So how do you explain the aged wine that seems totally dead upon opening to the point where some folks would pour it down the drain, only to 3 hours later be wonderfully aromatic and hauntingly beautiful. (and you haven’t tasted it in the interim to become acclimatized to it)? How do you explain the dauvissat chablis that is totally closed, with no nose, some folks think is off or premoxed, yet three hours later is classic chablis?

I get the fact that we can easily delude ourselves, but it really seems impossible with wines like these. When I raised this issue earlier in the thread (perhaps a year or two ago), folks said they really hadn’t tried the blinded method with wines like these. My guess is most of these blinded examples are with younger wines that most of us would agree don’t benefit from “air”. Does anyone have examples of doing this blinded with 20-30 year old Barolo, Burgundy, Rioja?

(If this has already been answered, just reference the post)

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
There is a very interesting book that Jamie wrote called “I Taste Red”. Any generalizations of tasting phenomena are laid to rest by this great book.

He does realize that the whole tasting of a wine is highly subjective and driven to a great extent by personal genetics.

The ability to assess PTU(propylthiouracil) bitterness, tastebud density, pyrazine sensitivity and now temperature tasting.

In 2000, it was found that 40% of the population will get an increase in the sensation of sweetness just by raising the temperature in a certain part of the tongue.

Put together with the myriad of anosmic holes with certain aromas and you have the setting for total subjectivity.

Only 1 micromole of oxygen is active when a bottle is opened according to research (mostly at Cal Davis) . This goes mainly to consuming off aromas. (This is how Msr Audouze uses this) It could partially revive certain bottles I would think as long as the volatile compounds are not dead ends and unreactive.

The whole textural change and increase in the volatile organic compounds has yet to be adequately explained in my mind. It is not “humbug”. It is a real phenomena no matter whether you do it blinded or not I would think. It is not an accomodation to the wine. A heightening of the senses. Those are usually dumbed down by repeat exposure not heightened. (Take for instance a room full of terrible aromas. They will be strong at first but then you acclimate. Downscale your olfactory perception. It doesn’t work in the other direction.)

Wished Jamie was here to talk about this. He is quite articulate on this subject.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked: I have never tasted a wine that was dead upon opening but tasted fabulous three hours later on except when I heated my tongue with a special oenological tongue warmer.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

(Even better if you could siphon to decanter from the standing bottle because the act of pouring out half gave the other half a nice gulp of air…) Taste all three an hour later.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
john stimson:

So how do you explain the aged wine that seems totally dead upon opening to the point where some folks would pour it down the drain, only to 3 hours later be wonderfully aromatic and hauntingly beautiful. (and you haven’t tasted it in the interim to become acclimatized to it)? How do you explain the dauvissat chablis that is totally closed, with no nose, some folks think is off or premoxed, yet three hours later is classic chablis?

I get the fact that we can easily delude ourselves, but it really seems impossible with wines like these. When I raised this issue earlier in the thread (perhaps a year or two ago), folks said they really hadn’t tried the blinded method with wines like these. My guess is most of these blinded examples are with younger wines that most of us would agree don’t benefit from “air”. Does anyone have examples of doing this blinded with 20-30 year old Barolo, Burgundy, Rioja?

(If this has already been answered, just reference the post)

My closest is with a regular group that tastes such wines and others in that time range, with a vertical or horizontal theme. (Usually all from the same cellar, purchased on release.) Sitting in front of 8-10 glasses of themed mature wines, following them over a couple hours. It seems profound when all are sound excellent wines. Some take time to open up. Some improve over time, while others decline and others just evolve in how they show. Break that down into various metrics and multiple changes are occurring in many of the wines. Some of the same metrics, from a perception standpoint, are increasing in one wine while decreasing in another.

Certainly our perceptions play a role. And various factors can effect our perceptions, like changing wine temp, ambient temp, glass shape, as well as our perceptual plasticity - some of which is built in* and some relates to things like our hydration, what else we have or are currently tasting and smelling, etc.

*Our brains will tone down dominant smells over time, enabling us to pick up more subtle aromas, which is a key evolutionary survival trait. You can notice this as you continue smelling a wine and start picking up more nuances a few seconds in. Then again, if you take a short break with some fresh air and return to a glass you might “get” again something that had faded.

An extreme example of the effect of ambient temperature was a Ridge Monte Bello event (a decade ago) on what was probably the hottest day of the year. They’d bring up a freshly decanted wine from the cool cellar just-in-time to the shaded courtyard. Wine temp was proper. But the '95, '97 & '99 MBs all tasted 20 years older than they should have, according to published notes, and in contrast to notes from the cooler prior day. (Those of us who’d posted those notes were invited up for a re-taste.)

These days it should be pretty easy to do with a Coravin. Pour out a glass, let it air for an hour (or whatever time you want to use). Then pour out another glass, do the immediate comparison. Of course you still have palate saturation to deal with just comparing two glasses, but do it enough times in random order, with enough different wines, and you should be able to draw some conclusions. My own experience says that it is probably pretty random. Some aged bottles improve with air, some don’t, some get worse.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

I think barometric pressure is also important but that is another story.

I drank three different quality wines last week at 7,000 feet, and they all tasted exactly like they should taste, and were wonderful. I’ve never understood the “barometric pressure” argument.

I think that over a period of time I have read most of this thread, but I remain somewhat baffled.

My disclaimers: I have drunk a lot of old (40+ years) nebbiolo, and I generally open, decant, taste, then allow it to sit undisturbed for several hours. In my experience this generally improves the wine, sometimes quite remarkably. I don’t drink wines which are too young in controlled circumstances (my home), so I cannot speak to this. I am not a scientist of any sort, so I cannot seriously speculate on actual causes or events.

My bafflement is this: I think everyone acknowledges that if you open and decant a wine, then leave it sitting open for a few days, it will probably change radically. Maybe for better, maybe worse, but certainly different. So, all indications are that opening and decanting a non-fortified wine begins a process which continues for hours/days/weeks during which evidence is there will be more or less contnuous change.

Drinkers will disagree about velocity, stages, nature of change, advisability, but to me everything else is just drawing the line (“I think this wine tastes better after 8 hours, begins to fade at 12, and is undrinkable after 3 days (YMMV)”). To me it just makes no sense to suggest that nothing at all is happening between decant and drinking hours later, and the question of whether whatever is happening is improvement or deterioration is highly subjective, and logically almost unsettleable.

I read repeatedly about oxygen exposure, calculations, etc., and sometimes aromatics, but wine evolution in and out of bottle is very complicated, there are anaerobic reactions also, are there not? Inorganic reactions?

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
What is rather strange for me is that if a very serious person says that a shark is able to smell blood at one mile distance, everybody says : OK it is possible.

And if I say that a wine opened 4 to 5 hours before serving, which is not decanted but let quiet in its standing position will let all the bad smells evaporate, nobody will accept what I say.

I have opened thousands and thousands bottles having more than 40 years and it is a constant fact that a sane wine becomes larger and balanced and that a wine which has bad smells, if it is not dead, will completely recover as the bad molecules will evaporate.

Why would you believe the one who says that a shark smells blood at one mile, which means that perfumes can travel and not believe a fact of experience which is that the bad perfumes travel outside the bottle.

Of course I could admit that I have “the green hand” as the ones who succeed in growing plants, but as it always works (a dead wine remaining a dead wine), I am sad that some people refuse facts that I can prove any way any time.

And I repeat again, no decanting just slow oxygenation.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

I would believe a marine biologist who told me that a shark could smell blood from a mile away because I would assume he would have an experimental basis (not the same thing as your experience, Francoise) for making the claim. If another marine biologist contested the claim, I would try to the best of my abilities to judge the quality of the evidence they adduced. Here, people are contesting your claim and the rest of us are making our best judgments. But no one listens to a person who says you should just listen to me.

It is more than that. There is also confirmation bias against scientific analysis. I believe it, I have convinced myself it is so, and so when I perform this ritual, I get the results I expect and so must be correct. And yet, is there a control to your experiment? Are you blinding groups of others on pop and then 4 hours later? What “bad molecules” are you referring to? As aromas of decomposition evaporate (presumably that’s the smell in a 120 year old bottle), are not also phenolic compounds of the wine’s remaining fruit profile?

I cannot speak to the science behind it, Francois, but am simply trying to explain why many others may remain skeptical. One may say, a shark can smell a drop of blood in the water from a mile away, and then provide a scientific explanation of the olfactory organs of a shark, hypersensitivity to same, and then evidence from repeated studies “proving” the hypothesis.

I would love to see tests of the molecular composition of gas in the head-space of a 120 year old wine, relative to the same once the bottle has be “Audozed” for 4 hours. You may very well have a point. You may not have a point, though, for a 15 year old bottle of wine.

These personal anecdotal experiences, no matter how frequent, are not reliable. We think they are because of confirmation bias, but they aren’t. I mean, we have massive battles on this forum about “travel shock” and whether a wine improves after being open (and with increasing head space) on the counter for 4-6 days. We’re all fairly ready to let ourselves know we’ve got it all figured out, but most of us can’t even blind taste our way out of a paper sack on wines we’ve had. Most can’t blind on theory alone. Most have missed corked bottles before, and certainly more times than they know. Some love brett and don’t think it’s a flaw. Some think premoxed wines can come back to life with more oxygen. Some think no-sulfur wines are a good idea. Some think heat damage and resolve with time. Some here even like still rose!

Blind tasting comments aside, my question is why? Why do some of us think those things? Because we believe in our own senses. Often to a flaw. Add belief in your own senses with your preconceived notions about wine and you’ve patted yourself on the back for your rightness.

But that’s why blind tasting experiments exist (which repeatedly prove that cheap wine is not the same as expensive wine)!

posts.name: larry schaffer

It continues to amaze me that some people are perfectly fine with wide assessments based on a single bottle and other people want to see detailed scientific experimentation.

The more that I make and try to understand wine, the winemaking process, and how wines breathe an age, the less I seen to truly be able to wrap my head around things.

Yes, at times, things seem to be somewhat predictable. But more often than not, I am surprised that a wine shows better while after it was supposed to, or does not show well at all when it is supposed to, or shows poorly but then comes around hours and hours later.

One of that sagest pieces of advice I’ve heard on this board is one should not make any assumptions on any other bottle of wine based in the performance of a particular bottle.

Cheers

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I did the experiment with two wine groups. To eliminate bottle variation, I opened 6 magnums of different Bordeaux from the nineties, all left Bank and served them double blind. The top half was poured into a decanter and allowed to breathe for three hours. The bottom half was poured into fifth to the top excluding as much oxygen as possible and recorked.

Results were pretty mixed. At first, nobody figured out what was going on, when I told them, only one person was able to match all the wines. The less experienced group seemed to prefer the decanted wines, while the experienced one preferred the ones straight from bottle. Nothing conclusive, and for me all it proved is that we may talk a good game, but none of us really really know what we are talking about.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: The problem with this is that the act of pouring off 1/2 of a bottle still exposes the remainder to a fair amount of air, then pouring it into another bottle does the same, so it’s really hard to say that that portion is equivalent to a pop and pour after 3 hours. It’s very hard to design a comparison group that is truly equivalent to pop and pour unless you are using a separate bottle, which has its own problems, of course.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked: I’ve mentioned a double blind tasting at Ridge several years ago, which was 3 flights of three wines. “Something different and something in common” with all the wines. The wines in the first two flights were distinct from each other. (Easy conclusion to me they were all Estate Cab, since I know what they make and they surely didn’t show like Monte Bello.) In the third flight the 1st and 3rd wine were confusingly similar, and the middle wine was so different than the rest, showing like PV, it threw my theme theory into question. With time, as the wines evolved in the glass, I began noticing that the 3rd wine kept showing how the 1st wine had shown a few minutes earlier. Hmm. Christopher had gone around the table pouring one wine, then the next, then the next, so it was a couple minutes between each. Turned out to be from three 750mls of the same vintage. So, the two glasses following the same exact trajectory made sense. (The anomaly is another issue.)

You have a strong point in there. People are claiming you have confirmation bias, but so many people you pour for have the opposite expectations of you. Their experiences belie that claim.

While the shark analogy was poor, there certainly was a time there was no explanation for that phenomenon. No sharks observed in the area. Blood poured in water. More and more sharks appear.

It certainly is not scientific to dismiss something out of hand, simply because there’s no explanation. Having certainty in an alternative explanation, without basis, isn’t scientific. It’s an alternative assumption. A preponderance of anecdotal evidence is a starting point.

He often cites concerns. Sometimes he deems them unfounded, sometimes the wine confirms his fears (or everyone else’s scores do)

So, it seems to me there’s a greater chance there’s something to your method than not, even if we don’t know why.

Wes, you’re analysis appears to fail to consider that those to whom Francois is pouring the wines are provided the opportunity to try wines before and after the slow ox. If a 1927 is opened and they are told that it smelled terrible but now, 4 hours later, smells much better, you have a corrupted experiment. The confirmation bias returns, as they are presented an expectation with consumption: the wine has improved with air. The issue turns only if you have some means of a true double blind.

Ideally you use a magnum, use inert gas to push wine through a coravin-like device that protects the wine from oxygenation and into another sealed container loaded with inert gas to preclude any oxygenation in either bottle. Then you’d Audoze the first bottle, and pop and pour the second and allow participants to taste the two wines double blind. To make the tasting even better, do not clue them in on the test. Just a blind tasting of some old wines for preference.

Monitor preferences and request notes on evolution of the wines during the next two hours.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: I just believe François and do as he portrays the objective. So far, so good.

posts.name: Stan Stevenson
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:

I did the experiment with two wine groups. To eliminate bottle variation, I opened 6 magnums of different Bordeaux from the nineties, all left Bank and served them double blind. The top half was poured into a decanter and allowed to breathe for three hours. The bottom half was poured into fifth to the top excluding as much oxygen as possible and recorked.

Results were pretty mixed. At first, nobody figured out what was going on, when I told them, only one person was able to match all the wines. The less experienced group seemed to prefer the decanted wines, while the experienced one preferred the ones straight from bottle. Nothing conclusive, and for me all it proved is that we may talk a good game, but none of us really really know what we are talking about.

I tend to agree with this with the conclusion that the notion that wines need to breathe for long periods of time is wildly overrated.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
This is a tough crowd! Yes we could design (hypothetically) perfect experiments, except that we’re talking about very old bottles. These will show enough variation due to fills, storage, closure, etc. etc. to make any perfectly designed test still prone to failure, or meaninglessness. And ‘very old’ is crucial here - as these methods and results are probably irrelevant to the mass of what we drink (ie. bottles < 35 years old).

To me, this is a classic ‘perfect is the enemy of the good’ situation. When the perfect is unavailable, good is often very good. And, to me, Francois’ experiments are quite good. Thousands of bottles worked with, combined with meticulous note taking, certainly transcends the anecdotal. He may not be wearing a white coat in a lab, but I see this research as valid science. And François as a respectable scientist. His tools are his mind, nose, palate, and a few bottle opening devices. It may be that he has a unique/unusual sense of smell/taste, or other sensory capabilities that are not typical.

I know many here disagree, and that’s fine. We can choose to either learn from these experiences or not. To invalidate them wholesale seems extreme.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Where I struggle is hearing that anecdotal evidence is king-enough, and then sitting listening to Harry Karis tell me that his bottles of CdP are sound, that one is not corked, and that brett is neither pervasive in old bottles nor a flaw. He may know more about CdP and may have opened two thousand more CdPs than me, but I’d wager my mortgage on a lab test that his ass was wrong as to the wines we had together.

I do not mean to denigrate Francois and love his write-ups, but one saying “I believe this and I’ve observed this and it confirms my belief” is little different than a flat-earther saying "I observe no curvature and have observed no curvature during millions of waking seconds, and so my sensory observations support my personal hypothesis.

Our brains are routinely able to trick our senses and our senses are routinely unable to observe things accurately and our memories are inherently fallible and can be easily affected by internal and external influences.

That said, I recognize that before Isaac Newton figured out the formula for gravitational pull everyone knew that if you jumped, you’d come down. Billions of pieces of anecdotal evidence confirmed a hypothesis, and we later learned the science that proved up the physics which confirmed the hypothesis.

So I’m not saying that Francois is wrong, only that to expect agreement based solely on one’s anecdotal experiences, which confirm personal expectations, begs intellectually perilous behavior.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:

Wes, you’re analysis appears to fail to consider that those to whom Francois is pouring the wines are provided the opportunity to try wines before and after the slow ox.

No, they aren’t.

If a 1927 is opened and they are told that it smelled terrible but now, 4 hours later, smells much better, you have a corrupted experiment. The confirmation bias returns, as they are presented an expectation with consumption: the wine has improved with air. The issue turns only if you have some means of a true double blind.

You’re off the rails assuming they’re told anything like that. Regardless, intentionally inserting bias can only go so far. It won’t make a dead wine appear amazing. We aren’t talking about someone designing a trick to fool people. These are mostly people with a realistic view of the batting average of ancient wines, and how the good bottles show. If your own experience tells you about 15% of a given set of wines should be drinkable, and what you then experience is 80% meeting or exceeding that expectation, how is that confirmation bias?

Ideally you use a magnum, use inert gas to push wine through a coravin-like device that protects the wine from oxygenation and into another sealed container loaded with inert gas to preclude any oxygenation in either bottle. Then you’d Audoze the first bottle, and pop and pour the second and allow participants to taste the two wines double blind. To make the tasting even better, do not clue them in on the test. Just a blind tasting of some old wines for preference.

Is there an echo in here?

So I’m not saying that Francois is wrong, only that to expect agreement based solely on one’s anecdotal experiences, which confirm personal expectations, begs intellectually perilous behavior.

Who’s saying that? Certainly not me. I’m just saying there’s enough there there to give this serious consideration. There’s a strong preponderance of anecdotal evidence that makes it worth looking into. That people dismissing this out-of-hand, solely based on reading, shows a foolish level of personal bias. That is like someone who’s never strayed more than 30 miles from his birthplace dismissing those loons who claim the earth is spherical. “Don’t those idiots know that if the Earth was spinning, like they claim, they’d be thrown off? It makes no damn sense!”

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

Who’s saying that? Francois. In multiple posts he’s basically said, “I have all the experience, and I can’t believe you aren’t taking my word for it.” Certainly not me. I’m just saying there’s enough there there to give this serious consideration. I agree it is worthy of consideration, have not written off Mr. Audoze’s theory, and believe the next step is a test of the sort you and I agree on. There’s a strong preponderance of anecdotal evidence that makes it worth looking into. This may be mincing words, but I think there is a large volume of anecdotal evidence from a limited source, certainly not a preponderance of evidence in support of Mr. Audoze’s theory. That people dismissing this out-of-hand, solely based on reading, shows a foolish level of personal bias. I think categorizing people’s skepticism as “solely based on reading” is unfair. I think quite a bit of the skepticism comes from an understanding of the effects of oxygen on wine, personal experiences with oxidation, personal experiences of oxidation and old wines, analysis of blind tests addressing oxygenation in wines, etc. That is like someone who’s never strayed more than 30 miles from his birthplace dismissing those loons who claim the earth is spherical. "Don’t those idiots know that if the Earth was spinning, like they claim, they’d be thrown off? It makes no damn sense!"See this is where I think you’re exactly wrong. Mr. Audoze’s theory is anecdotal evidence that provides a theory adverse to what chemists would expect based on their readings and anecdotal evidence. Mr. Audoze’s theory is anecdotal evidence adverse to anecdotal evidence of oxidation-caused spoliation of tens of thousands of bottles of wine over time, and countless studies about them. It’s adverse to 10 year closure studies and the effects of oxidation on wine performed by the Australian Wine Research Institute. It’s a hypothesis antithetical to expectations, based solely on personal anecdotal evidence. And in somewhat younger wines, the opposite of other blind test results. So heavy skepticism of Mr. Audoze’s results should be the expectation, as Mr. Audoze’s hypothesis is antithetical to substantially every other hypothesis. It’s no different than being skeptical of folks (winemaker included) who say “you know what helps make terribly premoxed wines drinkable? More oxygen.” Wait, so apply even more of a tremendously corrosive substance to a corroded substance and it gets better? It’s an illogical proposition and illogical propositions warrant skepticism

In my opinion, of course.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I think categorizing people’s skepticism as “solely based on reading” is unfair. I think quite a bit of the skepticism comes from an understanding of the effects of oxygen on wine, personal experiences with oxidation, personal experiences of oxidation and old wines, analysis of blind tests addressing oxygenation in wines, etc.

The point of his method is it protects a wine from oxidation (as Alan Rath repeatedly proves) while something else is allegedly happening (which Alan and others doubt). Most of the skeptics are arguing that his method is no different than pop-n-pour.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

I think categorizing people’s skepticism as “solely based on reading” is unfair. I think quite a bit of the skepticism comes from an understanding of the effects of oxygen on wine, personal experiences with oxidation, personal experiences of oxidation and old wines, analysis of blind tests addressing oxygenation in wines, etc.

The point of his method is it protects a wine from oxidation (as Alan Rath repeatedly proves) while something else is allegedly happening (which Alan and others doubt). Most of the skeptics are arguing that his method is no different than pop-n-pour.

This is a recharacterization of Francois’ own position, which is that his method revives and improves old wines, even those one would suspect suffer the adverse effects of oxidation due to low fill levels. Moreover, if one wished to protect the wine from any further oxidation, keep the cork in. Presumably the Audoze method does expose some of the wine to a small amount of increased oxidation, though I acknowledge Alan’s point that it is probably a limited volume of wine, and the simple act of pouring the wine into a glass more significantly affects oxidation. To that end, pop and pour should have the same “blow off” effect of the Audoze method because of the increased exposure to air during pouring. So I think quite a few of the skeptics would argue that there are logical impediments to just going along for the ride. Again, Mr. Audoze has infinitely more experience than me with 100 year old bottles, or even bottles older than me, so I would not simply say he’s wrong. I’m rather fascinated by this discussion and his experiences. But I do think there is a logical hurdle to simply saying “well, he’s experienced it so let’s all acknowledge it’s a scientifically accurate hypothesis.”

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Part of the problem is that François really has no opinion on the value of wine breathing, except when it comes to his method, which, as Alan pointed out, is barely letting it breathe at all. Indeed, the question of letting wines breathe and the question of the Audouze method, which might best be described as waterboarding it instead of keeping it suffocated, are really two separate questions getting mixed up in this thread.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:

This is a recharacterization of Francois’ own position, which is that his method revives and improves old wines, even those one would suspect suffer the adverse effects of oxidation due to low fill levels.

You don’t reverse oxidation with further oxidation.

He claims his method brings a wine back to life, where it remains stable in the glass for some time. The typical performance of such ancient wines under pop-n-pour would be to either show up dead and remain dead, or to have a little life and then fall apart within minutes. In other words, the wine is being majorly transformed over the course of many hours, while being protected from the direct effect of oxygen.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
After 2 years and almost 600 posts, I’m starting to feel fatigue setting in. My take on this issue is the same as it was before the post was initiated.

Some wines do not get any better with extended air (generally younger wines). I think this is the “myth” that folks are focusing on. Some wines get worse with air (many very young wines tend to close up with air. Also wines with various flaws-oxidation and TCA taint). And many wines benefit tremendously and blossom with “air” (gen older wines–most notably for me red burg, aged Barolo/Barbaresco, northern rhone, older Rioja, and many whites that haven’t gotten oxidized). this is actually not the kind of slow-ox that Francois is talking about (with is to me a subset of getting “air”).

Have I proven this last category with blinded testing? No. I haven’t felt the need, since the phenomenon is so obvious. Those who say they haven’t experienced this leave me with question marks about what wines they are drinking, or their palates.

But I understand the push for science, and the importance of trying to eliminate bias. So this weekend, which is my birthday, I’ll likely open a >10 year old Dauvissat, and a 15-20 year old red burg. To try to replicate some sort of blinded experiment, I’ll coravain off a good sized glass of each wine, and try to keep at cellar temp. 2-3 hours later I’ll have a child or spouse open the bottles and pour a second glass, then taste side to side, blind. I realize this is not a perfect experimental set up, but prob best I can do.

Oh–and then I’ll give a report.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
François Audouze:

If I may say, to pour a glass is quick oxygenation.

If you have a wine of 100 years which stinks, to imagine that “P&P and 5 minutes in the glass” will make a great wine belongs to dreams.

Francois, I do not doubt your wonderful experiences with older wines (or my own), nor the value of clearing the gas in the headspace/very upper liquid-gas surface and cleaning the bottle neck where the cork was placed. But maybe it’s time to drop the “slow ox” terminology or comparing it to “quick ox.” Alan is right you aren’t adding any significant amount of oxygen through the neck with 4-5 hours open. I’m a physicist and this is actually basic physics. The act of pouring introduces significant oxygen, not your opening process.

What I would be interested to have you comment on, and would be more interesting to me at this point, is your experience in tracking the evolution of the older bottles you open after first pour as the wine is exposed to a significant amount of oxygen. Although my group of old wine drinkers typically double decants old reds off the sediment, many (anecdotally most, but I have never tracked it) of older wines we open benefit from time in the decanter. I am talking about wine from the 20s to 80s, within the 35+ year camp.

To talk about whites and anecdotes, a friend brought an amazingly young bottle of 1964 Laville Haut Brion to a dinner last year. It was clearly a great bottle but even after three hours and about 1/3 of the wine left in the bottle, in my opinion it was just starting to open but was still in a reductive state. By your method it would have been the same. So I do not hear you saying that an “older” bottle (particularly whites where sediment is not an issue) like the 64 LHB that is actually still quite young given its potential for continued evolution benefits more greatly from your method than a decant. We wished we’d decanted that bottle.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
i’ve been doing this for 40+ years, so I don’t think time invested has much to do with how you fall on this issue.

I’m a physician and a quite familiar with “science”, and almost science, and pseudo-science, and even carefully done “science” that gets published in top journals that subsequently turns out to be 100% wrong. It’s very, very difficult to do subjective wine science experiments properly and reproducibly. And unless I missed it in the 600 posts, I haven’t seen anyone report on doing the blinded trials on the kinds of wines I’m talking about and mostly drinking (actually most of the reports don’t even mention the specific wines involved, which makes a huge difference in whether the wines will benefit from air or not, and renders the reports worthless).

One aspect of this discussion that I don’t think has received enough attention is that there is dramatic difference among wine drinkers in how wines are enjoyed and appreciated. Those ITB are far more likely to be pop and pourers, enjoy their wines quickly with 5-10 minutes in the glass, and move on to the next wine. The ones I know, out of habit, have their wine senses focused on first impressions, the first few minutes of a wine opening up. they are far less likely to linger over a bottle for hours, not terribly interested in decanting and airing a wine for a few hours. This is not because they know more than we do, it’s just that they spend their days tasting 50plus wines per day and have had their wine enjoyment become focused on a different part of the wine airing evolution curve. I find this profile amongst wine merchants, and some, but not all, winemakers.

I’m part of a very experienced wine tasting group that has been together for many years. Each member of the group is a breather, except for one, who would pop and pour most anything, at most decanting only for sediment, and at most giving something perhaps 1/2 hr of air to wake up. Of course he’s ITB as a well know wine merchant. If he misses that early phase after a wine is opened, he’s disappointed, because that’s the phase he has come to appreciate based upon his daily practice.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Jayson,
Thank you for your message, very gentle, and I would like to say that I do not pretend to know the truth and I can accept that different methods can bring great results.

But I am so enthusiast in the way I live my passion that it could be understood that I neglect other opinions. I am sorry if I can give this impression.

I have attended many tastings organized by Bipin Desai who is a friend, and Bipin makes so : the wines are opened only less than one hour before the tasting and are immediately decanted, for two reasons : separate the sediments, and offer an homogeneous wine to everyone.

And I am always sad that wines arrive on the table absolutely not enlarged and expanded, very tight. And they expand in the glass but it is too late because we are already drinking another flight.

I am not sure that I have well understood your examples and your questions but for me decanting at the last moment will never enlarge the wines as my method does, and decanting 5 hours before will not help the wines to structure themselves gently.
I have drunk many old Laville (62 in 32 different vintages) and I have drunk 2 times the 64 and all of them were ameliorated by the slow-ox method.

In a way I am not so motivated to continue this discussion because people object : “it is impossible” and never mention : “I have audouzed the wine and it deteriorated it”. Please give me examples which show that this method does not work.

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
My experience with young and mature Bordeaux is that many go thru a series of phases as they “open up” after decanting.
I call these the dumb phase, fruit phase, acidic phase, tannic phase and finally the balanced phase where each aspect takes its proper place.
Each takes roughly half an hour, although it may be shorter or longer.
Even a wine with strong fruit aroma on opening will follow the same progression.
If I am taking a bottle of Bordeaux to an offline I will often decant for 2 hours in the morning, then return it to the bottle and back into the wine fridge for the day. At dinner the wine is generally ready to open up in the glass on first pour.

Some of the time, we even failed the basic AAB test of being able to reliably identify which pour was which. When I got that correct – some of the time, I had no preference, and some of the time I preferred the pop and pour, and some of the time I preferred the decanted wine. But there were some patterns in terms of the types of wines that went into each category, and I’ve used that knowledge going forward. But those experiments all focused on young wines with 2 bottles of each, as I had no way to control for handling/cork/etc. issues in older wines. So there I’m flying just as blind as everyone else.

Seriously, many of us have done numerous, numerous experiments drinking wines that didn’t get a chance to breathe and didn’t open up until the last drop if at all, and conversely learning to open a wine and assess whether it needs additional air, swirling the initial glass like Stan suggests and finding after 5 minutes, 1/2 hour, more, that nothing is happening. then decanting, double decanting, or just leaving the bottle to “air” (my least favorite option), re-tasting the wine 2 hours later with no toothpaste or further wine to accommodate the palate to find a wonderful nose and palate that were not in evidence before.

If I pop and poured a 20 year old Barolo at a tasting, frankly, I think I would be shot.

We are all clearly deluding ourselves, but I humbly submit that I’m going to continue deluding myself for the rest of my life.

Lastly, for Stan and Mel, see if you can delude yourselves as well. Take a 20 year old Barolo, or red Burg (don’t do Bordeaux as I’m not convinced they behave in the same way), pour a small glass to savor for your 5 min, then double decant. Retry the wine in 2-4 hours without any intervening wine and record your perceptions, again swirling for 5 min. Clearly this will be a faulty impression, but see how it goes.

In this tasting, the esteemed judges were asked to assess the quality of several red wines from France’s classic Bordeaux region (including 1961 Mouton-Rothschild, a 1982 Clerc-Milon, a 1980 d’Armailhac, and a 1990 Mouton-Cadet) that were

Uncorked a few minutes ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked a few hours ahead of time, and then poured and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few minutes ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked and poured into a decanter a few hours ahead of time, before poured into glasses and tasted
Uncorked, and then immediately poured into glasses and tasted (that is, no “breathing” at all).

For which wines, across the board, did these wine judges ultimately show the most preference? You guessed it:

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
Alan,
For the dinner for which I made a report I have tried to observe more than usually what happens.

First of all, there is a dramatic evolution during the first minutes. If the wine needs to enlarge itself, the smell 30 seconds after the first smell has changed, one minute later changed again and so on.
So in the first four minutes there are changes every second.

I did not observe after.

Then when the wine is served, every glass has a wine which has expanded, which is enlarged, serene, accomplished. And there is no part of the bottle which escapes that and what is very significative is that if the wine is consumed during 20 minutes, it is brilliant from the first sip to the last sip, as if the wine were reinforced and solid.

So, in my opinion, we should abandon the model of oxygen wanting to dive in the bottle and having difficulties to swim fast, but look for a phenomenon which creates dramatic changes of smells during the first four minutes and which makes that the whole bottle is concerned by the evolution.

For me the signs evoke bad air which escapes from the bottle, but you may propose another explanation.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:
K John Joseph:

I think this is right on topic…it is about your expectations affecting your perception of reality:

Anil Seth: Your brain hallucinates your conscious reality | TED Talk

This kind of metaphysics is very different from confirmation bias. It could be true even if the hallucinations our consciousnesses provided were exact analogues to the reality in which we operate.

Confirmation bias, by the way, cuts both ways. If we take it seriously, not merely for those who disagree with us, but for ourselves, we would put no trust in anything we observed. Since no one on this thread really distrusts him or herself, perhaps we should all stop merely writing off testimony that disagrees with us.

I believe that you are twice incorrect. First, the hearing test with the synth noise and then the words with it is, to me, an association of expectations and sensory perceptions that is precisely like the slow-ox makes wine better situation we’ve been discussing. That is the confirmation bias I have been arguing exists and is precisely why anecdotal evidence alone is, in my opinion, inherently precarious. You draw a hypothesis, expect a result, claim to experience the result. When scientific evidence suggests the perception is myth, or at least caused by some other action, there is instant skepticism. Why? Because one has a greater desire to believe one’s senses, despite the fact that our sensory perceptions are grossly affected by personal biases and expectations, than to acknowledge that one’s senses ARE inherently faulty.

Second, a solution in which we say “we should all stop merely writing off testimony that disagrees with us” is like saying “we should all start believing fake news because it’s all around us.” Maybe fake news in both directions balances out. But why would we rely on others’ sensory perceptions if we know that all of our sensory perceptions are inherently flawed? I mean, there is a reason that Wine Spectator, Decanter, and other wine critic mags taste wines blind. The goal is to eliminate biases that may affect our sensory perceptions. In other words, the goal is to eliminate as many sources of influence as possible in order to distill sensory perceptions down to something more “true.”

So instead of simply not rejecting everyone else’s anecdotal experience, we say, “we should all question our sensory perceptions because they are inherently flawed, and seek to obtain a better understanding of the scientific “realities” associated with this perception or that.” Let’s have blind taste tests since we know we can all be biased by labels, vintages, expectations. Let’s run experiments to see if, in a blind setting, we actually get better results from A technique than B technique. Let’s run experiments using the scientific method and solid controls in an effort to find out if the cause of our perception is A or B.

What I’ve never been able to understand is resistance to this line of thinking. It is okay to be wrong. If there is minimal potential harm to wines from Slow-Ox and you perceive an improved quality in the wine from it, keep doing it. Your affected perception is either (i) right for another reason, leading to greater enjoyment of the wine; (ii) entirely wrong but allows you greater enjoyment of the wine because you believe and expect beneficial results; or (iii) net nothing and you have no adverse effects on your perception of quality. So no harm no foul and, for you and Francois, perhaps it is a great benefit to your tasting experience.

But that alone is absolutely not a reason to state, as if it is a global factual reality based in real science, that slow-ox actually does what you think it does. And hey, perhaps it does allow “the bad molecules” to blow off. Who knows.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Once you read a few articles about the psychology of tasting, you’ll never be too dogmatic in this area.

Or they psychology of advertising…or political polling…or food consumption…or taboos

What stuns me on a near-daily basis is the near global belief that one is immune to biases. That one can taste non-blind without some influence, no matter how minute, affecting tasting or perception of quality. There are countless studies that show people one wine strongly over another (even when they are identical wines from the same bottle) when told one is $100 and the other $10. Labels and packaging affect perceptions of quality. Brand recognition affects perceptions of quality. Billboards, commercials, and radio ads influence consumer purchasing trends. Taste makers drive perceptions of quality and purchasing. Attacks on individuals’ fears drive purchasing trends, even where the fear is irrational. “Experts” drive decision making even where resoundingly refuted by larger numbers of more qualified experts (vaccines, non-gmo foods).

We are all inherently susceptible to biases, and are especially susceptible to believing information as true where information plays into our biases or confirms our biases. It is why, to me, skepticism is critically important. Even in wine tasting.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
This revised position is standard skeptic, objectivism, with which I entirely agree. I wasn’t saying we should take everything at face value but that we should subject all testimony including our own to the same skeptical criteria of demanding what kinds of evidence we can get to control for the various forms of bias, of which confirmation bias is only one3.

The synth test is like the old Whorf “proofs” of untranslateability. It shows that we can make sensory mistakes and are indeed, because of our training, given to them. It doesn’t show we can’t correct them. The Ted talk describes consciousness as an hallucination, which would entail that it could not be corrected with evidence. But that is another kind of argument. If you truly believe that confirmation bias exists and that it can be corrected by testing than Tchelitscheff’s attitude is precisely the wrong one to have about one’s own biases.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

This revised position is standard skeptic, objectivism, with which I entirely agree. The synth test is like the old Whorf “proofs” of untranslateability. It shows that we can make sensory mistakes and are indeed, because of our training, given to them. It doesn’t show we can’t correct them. The Ted talk describes consciousness as an hallucination, which would entail that it could not be corrected with evidence. But that is another kind of argument. If you truly believe that confirmation bias exists and that it can be corrected by testing than Tchelitscheff’s attitude is precisely the wrong one to have about one’s own biases.

I agree with your last sentence as would most in the scientific community (of which I am not a part). It is why even well established principals of physics are called theories. Even when repeatedly tested and “proven” accurate, they are not concrete facts because we cannot know all and cannot test a theory against all possibilities. Even theories we believe to be concrete are often proven slightly off in certain circumstances. One should never be 100% certain about anything as 100% certainty is inherently based on some ignorance.

As for the Ted talk, I think there is a compelling argument that consciousness is a hallucination. The case has certainly been made among philosophers for hundreds and hundreds of years. Does the chair exist? Is “evidence” that you would use to correct your flawed sensory perception real, or is it yet another falsehood based on some other flawed perception? Are we in the matrix? It’s good for a laugh, but the point is that we cannot know with 100% certainty that we are not. I believe that is a futile mental exercise and it is why I promptly walked away from a minor, let alone a major, in philosophy in college. In any event, that’s for another thread.

K John Joseph:

As for the Ted talk, I think there is a compelling argument that consciousness is a hallucination. The case has certainly been made among philosophers for hundreds and hundreds of years. Does the chair exist? Is “evidence” that you would use to correct your flawed sensory perception real, or is it yet another falsehood based on some other flawed perception? Are we in the matrix? It’s good for a laugh, but the point is that we cannot know with 100% certainty that we are not. How can you think there is a compelling case for a position and think that the philosophy from which it derives is a futile exercise. One cannot disprove complete subjectivism with certitude, but very few philosophers really hold the position because it turns out to be completely without interest or consequence. You wind up having to act as if it is not true.

Once one takes confirmation bias as a problem that a)befalls us all, and b) can, at least in principle, be dealt with, the way to deal with it is not, obviously, merely discounting the testimony of those who disagree with us but trying to calculate how best to deal with it.

John,

I open a wine. I let the sommelier smell it. He smells and as he is curious he smells it 30 seconds later. Then he tells me : incredible, the smell has changed so rapidly.

Lol, I can tell you that popping the cork sniffing, waiting 30 seconds, and sniffing again should result in a different smell given the alteration of the gas within the head space of a bottle. Just like I can tell you that “bottle shock,” the change in taste of a bottle during the initial months after bottling, is real based on legitimate, scientifically measured and measurable chemical changes caused by interactions between oxygen and SO2.

So I really don’t think it’s ridiculous at all. I think it might be ridiculous to wait 1 hour, then smell the unpoured wine still on bottle twice within 30 seconds and to expect a different result because there is no reason for the wine to have a different smell at that point, unless you’re funneling evaporating alcohol and phenolic compounds into the neck of the wine. If that is the case, you’re also losing them before the wine is in the glass, which I would think is not a good thing.

But that is not the point. The point is that you appear to be entirely skeptical (hey, that’s good!) that expectations can concretely alter real cognitive and sensory perceptions. They can. Read the linked study here:
Why expensive wine appears to taste better: It's the price tag | ScienceDaily. In summary, same wine poured multiple times. One time wine is shown to have much higher price tag. Same exact wine. Brain activity studies show that the drinkers actually had a different physical reaction to the wine based solely on the expectation that it would be better because it was more expensive. Let that sink in. Their physical reaction was different. The wine was identical. The result was a legitimately different response, a false reality, a “hallucination” about a difference in quality that was actually felt and perceived by the drinkers simply because they expected it even though it was not so. Their “reality” was still their “reality” in that their real, physical perception changed. It was just not “reality” in fact.

What I think is humorous is that you believe my position to be about negativism when it is about skepticism. Those are two profoundly different things. I question the reasoning behind your methodology because I am personally looking for the optimal way to experience wine. I would hope that, given our respective love of wine, you would invite that skepticism. Perhaps we may both learn from the results and both enjoy a more optimal way of drinking wine, if a more optimal way exists. If it does not, then we both still win as we have then confirmed that your methodology is optimal.

Given the study I linked, of which there are many with substantially identical results, perhaps you’d like to simply turn away from this discussion, as your methodology, regardless of whether it works or not, is contributing to your (and others’) enjoyment of wine. So why change anything?

link_counts.title: Why expensive wine appears to taste better: It's the price tag: When a bottle costs more, the reward center in the brain plays a trick on us -- ScienceDaily

At some point were that my position then there could be no such thing as a truly good bottle of wine. And yet if we look at the thread on our “10 most influential wines,” there are many wines that overlap, and many wines that were our respective “first wine” that opened our eyes to what fine wine could really be. In most cases, those experiences occurred even before we had real expectations about wine, or in fact had low expectations because we had been drinking poor quality wine. I am very ready to acknowledge that there are quasi-objective criteria for wine criticism, which is why Decanter Magazine exists and why Wine Spectator tastes wines blind.

I would expect that if we tasted together, our respective expectations for the wines would be different, and that those expectations might color how we each approached and actually tasted the wines. I would hope that we would both find great joy in the experience or, at the very least, find it immensely educational. I’d guess we might even agree on some of the wines!
