posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
The last time I had dinner with Andre (ok it was lunch) was at King Estate when I drove Andre Porcheret from Domaine Leroy there. Maybe we can call this the Lunch of Two Andres. Nobody used a decanter. Of course, Andre T also stored wine under his bed.

The point of the original comment is that most of our ideas about wine --even our mass mutual hallucinations–are subject to analysis and correction. Something I see at wineries sometimes is a kind of kennel vision, when nobody tastes wine blind or makes comparison tastings, etc. Most good winemakers try to do a few experiments every year.

Now when I read the Bespaloff story about breathing wine my first thought was to disprove it and get some publicity for the store. So I used wine that I thought would show well with breathing, notably Barolo and young Cabernet. I cannot remember the exact results but the recently opened Barolo showed best, even though accepted wisdom was that this was a wine that should be opened a day in advance.

My conclusions were
1/we sure did not show the genius of letting wine breathe
2/why not just open wine and decide where to go from there
3/sometimes wine benefits from breathing but not a majority of the time

In a blind tasting nobody has yet to show that wine-breathing is a great idea more than 50% of the time. Now I have not retested this theory lately and probably won’t, because
a/I don’t think it helps tannic wines
b/There are fewer and fewer funky wines nowadays, wines that need some air to recover from what ails them.
c/I rarely drink wine older than 1970 and if I do,it’s somebody else’s bottle. They can use the Audouze system…it’s their bottle!
d/I can always let the wine breathe some more

Here is an example of my favorite form of confirmation bias: I staged a blind tasting for some wine writers after we were debating how chardonnay should be made. Naturally when they voted blind, they agreed with me–don’t act surprised-- but as soon as they saw the labels…OH…it’s opening up now…it just needed more time to breathe!

Johnson is said to have refuted Berkeley by kicking a stone…or was it the idea of a stone?? In any event, we don’t have to go into epistemology here. Blind tasting is enough.
Just make sure your blindfold fits.

Here is an example of my favorite form of confirmation bias: I staged a blind tasting for some wine writers after we were debating how chardonnay should be made. Naturally when they voted blind, they agreed with me–don’t act surprised-- but as soon as they saw the labels…OH…it’s opening up now…it just needed more time to breathe!

Applies to more than just your Chardonnay Mel. Maybe Burgundy as a rule . . .

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
Johnson did assert that he refuted Berkeley by kicking a very large stone, one he virtually bounced off of. Despite the spectacle of the demonstration, most philosophers have not agreed with him.

If you assent to the fact that breathing works only 50% of the time or less, since no one, I think, thinks it is the best thing to do every time, you are just quibbling over numbers. It’s like the quotation frequently attributed to GBS, who, sitting next to a well-to-do lady at a dinner party, asked her if she would have sex with him for a million pounds. When she answered yes, he asked if she’d have sex with him for a shilling. When, outraged, she said, “what do you take me for, a common prostitute?” he answered, “we’ve already established that. Now we’re haggling over price.”

The chardonnay tasting was about picking times. They all preferred the later picked wines but after they had voted and the labels came off, they started to find virtues in the wines they had not liked while tasting blind.

Jonathan, good story. The point I was making is that if we get into discussions of metaphysics and epistemology,or Ted talks about the mass hallucinations of everyday life, then who cares about breathing bottles of wine?? It’s just more thread drift, certainly on a deeper plane than most.

No study done blind here has demonstrated that breathing wine on a routine basis makes much sense. What was it that Harry Waugh said?? Seeing the label is worth fifty years in the wine trade.

I agree with this. It’s foolish to blindly go through some one-size-fits-all decanting ritual. You can miss out on a wine’s zenith. You can miss a wine entirely. You can miss the magic of it evolving in the glass. Or miss part of that evolution. (And just because a wine is changing in the glass doesn’t necessarily mean it’s getting better or worse.) Why? At the same time, many wines do improve with air. Some wines need extended air time to show well. So, be flexible and strategize. Don’t be joyless and rigid.

I don’t think (breathing) helps tannic wines

It is factual that breathing doesn’t change the tannins. If it changes something else that effects our perception of tannin seems to happen sometimes. That makes sense because of the relativity and plasticity of our perceptions.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
So here’s the question. Suppose that:
A. Controlled double blind studies prove that for certain wines breathing makes no difference
B. Controlled non-blind studies, or just stats gathered from actual dinners etc, show that most tasters think breathing (of the wine) does make an improvement.

Then, is it wrong to have the wines breathe?

If in a social setting (where I do probably 90% of my tasting) do I actually care about the scientific study, versus my utterly subjective impression?

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
RichardFlack:

So here’s the question. Suppose that:
A. Controlled double blind studies prove that for certain wines breathing makes no difference
B. Controlled non-blind studies, or just stats gathered from actual dinners etc, show that most tasters think breathing (of the wine) does make an improvement.

Then, is it wrong to have the wines breathe?

If in a social setting (where I do probably 90% of my tasting) do I actually care about the scientific study, versus my utterly subjective impression?

I actually think this nails the “real” issue to a very large degree. I have obviously taken a relatively controversial position in this thread related, specifically, to slow-ox and the perception that slow-ox results in positive effects. But as I’ve also noted, if it causes us to have heightened expectations and to taste the wine differently on a very real measurable physical, brain activity basis, and thereby allows us to experience greater enjoyment (ala a placebo), why should one stop doing it? I hope Francois continues to slow-ox his wines. I will continue to not decant my (much younger) wines.

Richard’s question is an interesting one and shows the real difference between blind tasting and non-blind tasting. If you are tasting things blind, you have an odd balance of objectivity and subjectivity, but do not necessarily maximize the pleasure you might otherwise obtain from tasting non-blind. For instance, if we extrapolate the study I linked earlier in this thread, you might really enjoy a blind bottle of 1961 Latour, but you might have an altogether different physical reaction that results in significantly greater enjoyment of the 1961 Latour non-blind…especially if the wine lives up to your expectations. We know external factors influence our pleasure. Labels, knowledge of scores, knowledge of costs, environment, company, etc. can all affect our perception of a wine. A dinner with best friends and X wine my leave you enjoying the wine more than a passing glass while at the in-laws house, who you absolutely hate and who resent you.

So to some degree the question on must ask is, should I pursue my most objective analysis of a wine for the sake of “truth” or should I pursue the most pleasure from a given wine, knowing that various biases and preconceived notions will not just affect your mental assessment of a wine but also your physical reaction to tasting the wine.

To Richard’s point, I suppose it depends on the context. Am I endeavoring to figure out what I should buy based on my truest assessment of wines in a group, or am I just trying to have a nice little hedonistic evening? Most of the time, it’s the latter, so do whatever the heck you want.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:
Mel Knox:

So much goes on during a wine tasting it’s always hard to say what is what.
Let’s remember that the tasters are changing along with the tasted.

And this would be true for tastings of wines that were not left to breathe. Indeed all skepticism about tasting, perception and bias confirmation, obviously works both ways.

This ignores the OP test and other similar experiments, though. The sole purpose of running a blind setup like the OP one is to create a test and a control. The tasters’ changes are, in essence, the control. They’re going to change as they taste through all of the various pours. The decanting methodologies and times are the variables. The whole point of the blind test is to eliminate skepticism, perception, and confirmation bias. This allows the tasters to monitor each pour’s performance relative to its peers as the wine evolves in glass, as the taster’s tastes change during the tasting, etc.

The results of the OP study and others I’ve seen suggest that when we control against bias, perception, skepticism, etc. and require tasters to simply weigh materials presented, that the “benefits” of decanting go largely unseen.

In non-blind studies, everything you’ve said is absolutely true. It works both ways. I will readily admit that non-blind I would have biases and preconceived notions etc. that would lead me to pre-favor pop-and-pour over something like a 3 hour decant. You’re absolutely right about that.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: The accounts of blind tests here, and none of them are that careful as they are described, is that while some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting, the fact that preferences occur show that differences occur from decanting. This would show that some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting–hardly an astounding conclusion–but pointedly not that decanting makes no difference.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
It’s difficult to get away from cat-chasing-own-tail hardships here. (for me) Virtually everything about this pursuit is so tied to subjectivity and our own sense of what we want to enjoy in our wine experience. Richard, I almost think my counter-question would be “how can you ever have a “controlled” study done with the subjectivity that’s involved?”

At present, I only have what I’m working with for myself. I have “convinced” myself, I guess, that opening a white burg the day before, slow-oxing it and often decanting the day of for a couple hours gives me the best showing of what I myself am looking for from that wine. I also make sure to taste some as soon as I pop the cork so that I give myself a reference point. I’d like to (perhaps foolishly) think that I’m open-minded enough to discern if a white burg ends up being less good with this treatment, but so far, it hasn’t happened.

Opened a 2011 Pontet-Canet. On opening, moody and not very giving. Chose to decant for 6 hours overnight, double-decanted back into bottle then served the next evening. It definitely showed “more” to me—but it was also paired with burgers (a possibly decent and/or amplifying food match), it still wasn’t anything truly spectacular and I may well have missed its best “showing” by not taking a taste at 4 am the previous morning.

Bottom-line? Also akin to Richard’s last comment. It’s my wine and, while I might listen to others’ suggestions, I’ll do with it as I bloody well wish

posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

The accounts of blind tests here, and none of them are that careful as they are described, is that while some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting, the fact that preferences occur show that differences occur from decanting. This would show that some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting–hardly an astounding conclusion–but pointedly not that decanting makes no difference.

Obviously. Is anyone arguing that decanting makes no difference? The issue is not whether decanting makes a difference. Blind and anecdotal tests all indicate that it makes some difference. The issue is:

  1. The blind tests suggest that decanting has an adverse effect on taste and so
  2. Decanting should be avoided for most wines but
  3. Non-blind anecdotal testimony claims decanting is more often beneficial which
  4. Leads to directly contradictory positions about whether the “differences” resulting from decanting are positive.

So which do we trust, does this confirm our anecdotal evidence is more flawed than we’re willing to admit, and should we change our habits based on this evidence?

I believe Stan is arguing precisely that and Mel is coming pretty close, as is Alan. Since they are the major anti-breathing proponents, I would say most of the people taking this position. Since, except for François, neither is anyone arguing that either decanting or any other form of extended oxygenation is a panacea for all wines, if I am wrong about Stan, Mel and Alan, we can extinguish this whole thread as being mere verbiage over the relative numbers of wine which decanting and/or extended oxygenation either help or hurt. posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

I believe Stan is arguing precisely that and Mel is coming pretty close, as is Alan. Since they are the major anti-breathing proponents, I would say most of the people taking this position. Since, except for François, neither is anyone arguing that either decanting or any other form of extended oxygenation is a panacea for all wines, if I am wrong about Stan, Mel and Alan, we can extinguish this whole thread as being mere verbiage over the relative numbers of wine which decanting and/or extended oxygenation either help or hurt. Especially helpful would be the extinguishing of all arguments about the pernicious effects of confirmation bias on all people who disagree with one’s conclusions.

To be clear, I’m not making an argument one way or the other about the more general issue of decanting and “breathing”. I happen to believe that (younger) wines do change after opening, but admit it’s difficult to assess whether the wine is changing or my palate is changing. I’ve had the experience enough times of drinking a wine on night 1, and having pretty close to the same experience on nights 2 (and sometimes 3) to believe for myself that the changes aren’t huge, and that most wines are quite durable over a few days. This is for “youngish” wines, not 50 year old bottles that will clearly change over the course of hours, sometimes minutes, in the glass. Believing that aeration changes wines (within the limits of what I just wrote), and not believing that just opening a bottle and slow-ox’ing for a few hours does anything, are two different things.

Here is my policy: I open a bottle of wine and unless it is really something that I think needs breathing, I pour it. While everyone else is waiting for it to to open up, I drink it. This works all the time.

This topic is a bit weird, because everyone thinks wine gets better with breathing. I was talking to a winemaker buddy the other day about one of his wines. He said it was fine the first day, great the second and out of the world the third.

So we who think breathing is a bit of a myth have to disprove something that everyone believes. Should n t people who believe wine breathing is great have to prove this to us??

Nobody says, Oh Fanta is great the first day but fabulous the second…or, I like to let my orange juice sit in a decanter over night.

OK, so…

Unless you think it needs breathing, you pour it. How do you determine which wine will or won’t change?

“So we who think breathing is a bit of a myth have to disprove something that everyone believes. Shouldn’t people who believe wine breathing is great have to prove this to us??” No. Nobody needs to prove anything to anybody. This is turning into a Jesus/Santa/atheist thing. There are no proofs that are sufficient to dissuade someone about his mythology. This hobby is for fun, I don’t give a shit whether you believe my tasting impressions or not, and likely vice versa!

I can tell you about Fanta: It changes, like wine. Different on day two, three, and beyond. Give it a go! It might be eye opening! Hell, try it with juice if you think breathing is a myth. Have some two day old juice that’s been sitting in a decanter. I din’t even want this morning’s juice tonight! You think it would be the same? Better yet, since you are making such trenchant analogies - try your experiment with milk and tell me that milk sitting changing after a day or three is a myth.

Side point: wine changes as it breaths, whether or not it “improves” is up to the palate of the be-taster.

I will end this argument with an irrefutable statement: Just as there are those who cannot perceive TCA and could, therefore, argue to the end of the week that TCA taint must be a myth, so are there those whose palates seem unable to perceive that a wine changes as it ‘breaths.’

Easy solution, we just divide into people who can taste the effect of breathing on a wine and those who can’t.

Adjourned.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
A brief summary for those who are interested, but not interested enough to read 14 pages (plus that other thread):

“Decanting or breathing wines has been shown to not improve them!”

“What?”

“Yeah, that’s right. All the science says it’s baloney!”

“What science?”

“Well, there’s this article, plus I tried it at the pony club and most of the ponies preferred P&P”
“Hear hear! I’ve always preferred P&P myself”

“That’s not science, and many of THE most experienced drinkers hold that it’s not baloney!”

“But some other experienced drinkers say it IS baloney. Plus you have confirmation bias!”

“I what?!?”

“You have this expectation that decanting / breathing will improve the wine, hence you fool yourself into perceiving it to work!” (you gullible twit you, enjoying the superior smugness of the true Dunning Kruger victim)

“But what about old Barolo? What about Audouzing?”

“FOOLING yourself, sorry. Show me the science!”

“But but but… what do you mean, show me the science: show me YOUR science!”

“As I said, once upon a time at the pony club…”

“That’s not science!”

“You only think it’s not science because: confirmation bias! Ha!”

“Aaargh! But I know I prefer some wines with a jolly good decant! How about YOU have reverse-confirmation-bias! Ha!”

“No you don’t, you only think you do. And no I don’t because I prefer P&P. So that’s not a bias.”

“But there is no science!”

“Don’t go all Matrix on me, but ok, if you want to PROVE your side of the story then go and do the science!”

“But I don’t feel the need to prove it… it just works for me… I don’t need to do the science”

“Yes you do, or else you’re just fooling yourself”

“Hey… how about… like you know how some people like purple-drank-SQN-blueberry-milkshakes while others hate it, and we go that’s ok, different preferences, no right and wrong [except we don’t, of course, secretly we just KNOW they’re wrong]… how about we also agree to disagree on this one: breathing / decanting works for some people but not others! It’s just personal taste and preference.”

“Ha! Ok. But I know I’m right on P&P for me, except when the wine needs to breathe of course. Anyways, live and let live [except, of course, secretly we just KNOW they’re wrong]”

Of course all these gentle fights are just for fun, and I am sad because my poor English does not allow me to catch all the finesses.

Of course I know that this discussion must find an end. But please allow me to be sad to see that 45 years of opening thousands and thousands old wines, with recoveries and miracles which appear extremely often, is considered as having no value because some posters have never seen this.

For a young wine (after 1985) a miracle will never happen because the wine is rarely wounded. But for old wines, it occurs extremely regularly.

I have proposed Alan to come and see how it works, I have proposed to make a film with a journalist specialised in wine and a talented sommelier, to show how it works.

I agree on the fact that we are at the end of the discussion. I just want to say that I am ready to receive anyone in which some of you would trust to make the demonstration that it works.

I can convince by opening bottles and showing how it works. I cannot justify by science.
I would have been happy that the ones wo deny the method would give examples of old wines on which it did not work.

posts.name: Mike Grammer

Your post at bottom of page 14 was terrific, Rauno—put a smile on my face.

Continuing my personal empirical study of white burg using Methode Grammeroise. 14 Jadot Duc de Magenta Chassagne Morgeots Clos de la Chapelle—decanted for 6 hours the day before, popped cork back in, put in fridge, served next day. On opening, tightly-wound heavily lemonseed-sided presentation. On serving, a rounded, completely harmonious, multifaceted and beautiful example of Chassagne. If my bias comes in, then so be it. But my impressions were confirmed by a) everyone at my dinner table, b) my friend Heather the next day (I saved some off into a minimal-air mini-bottle for her), who has one of the more discerning palates among my peers. I’m trying to make sure I certainly taste these wines for myself at the P&P moment, but each one of the last 8 or so has, for me, benefitted tremendously from this type of treatment—to the point that if I can’t control the process for doing this at a restaurant, I probably will refrain from bringing any youngish white burg there.

Mike

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
We’re in danger of falling into an infinite loop here. The issue was blind tastings, even among people who typically decant their wines, showed that they actually preferred pop and pour over various lengths of decanting. Thus, we attribute benefits to things in a non-blind setting that we do not experience in a blind setting. Then, we got into discussions about the scientific merits of various positions, and whether science supports hypotheses.

We know from science that tasting notes are not entirely bologna, as phenolic compounds in wine are substantially identical to some of the compounds found in the things we “smell and taste” in a wine. For instance when someone says “I smell strawberry” they are very likely actually smelling in the wine the same phenolic compound that gives strawberries their smell. So there is much to learn from explanations of tasting experiences. It is not always a pure subjective mirage.

But our biases, preconceived notions, and expectations do affect how we taste wine. And so the more of those one is exposed to during the tasting process, the less reliable the tasting note. That is precisely why many critical wine publications, like Decanter and Wine Spectator for what those are worth, taste wines blind. Their effort is to create the most “objective” analysis so that they are more reliable. The whole goal is to mitigate the very things, Francois, that would diminish the value of the report on the wine.

If we had separate glasses of the same wine exposed to air for various periods in a blind tasting, and agreed that the wine with more time exposed to air was superior to the wine with less time in glass, that would be a report with some value.

If I stand in front of a group of tasters and say, based on my experience, this wine will improve with 30 minutes of swirling. It is too tight right now, and has a foul aroma. But vigorous aeration will make this wine aromatically explosive and purely divine! I have had this wine 100 times, and in all instances, that is how you make this wine sing!

And then you poll all of the tasters after they re-taste the wine 30 minutes later, I’d say the report on the wine has not the slightest value as the experiment is utterly corrupted.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
I imagine very well the situation :

  • I open an old wine (and I insist on ‘old’) and the smell is awful

  • four hours later I serve the wine and the smell is wonderful and the wine has a perfect balance.

  • I say to myself : no, no, no, what you smell is what you wanted to smell and it is not the real smell of the wine. In fact the smell of the wine is still ugly

  • my guests say that the wine is great and I think : no, no, no, what they think has been influenced by what I wanted them to taste and not by what they really taste

  • then I think : if my guests are right it means that what I have smelt by opening is false. The smell by opening was perfect and if I found it ugly, it is because I wanted it to be ugly, in order to show that my method works.

And, not knowing if what I have smelt or tasted is true at the opening or when we drank it, I conclude that I am unable to smell or taste, I sell all the wines of my cellar and I decide to drink only water. And I do not smell it !

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Stan and possibly Neal mistook the meaning of my reply to Neal. Neal’s point, as I read it, is that even if his perpceptions were deluded, the result with regard to his enjoyment of the wine would be the same. I was not saying that his perceptions were deluded (I don’t even know where he stands in the debate) but that delusions matter when we mistakenly communicate them to others as accurate accounts of perception. The solution to that would be to be circumspect about one’s own perceptions, not to go around accusing others of delusion or perceptual bias when they disagree with you. I have said this before, but I will say it in plainer language: I consider accusing other people’s reports of their experience as being based on perceptual bias to be the worst form of bad faith argument. I certainly did not intend to accuse Neal of that, therefore.

  • I say to myself : no, no, no, what you smell is what you wanted to smell and it is not the real smell of the wine. In fact the smell of the wine is still ugly

  • my guests say that the wine is great and I think : no, no, no, what they think has been influenced by what I wanted them to taste and not by what they really taste

  • then I think : if my guests are right it means that what I have smelt by opening is false. The smell by opening was perfect and if I found it ugly, it is because I wanted it to be ugly, in order to show that my method works.

And, not knowing if what I have smelt or tasted is true at the opening or when we drank it, I conclude that I am unable to smell or taste, I sell all the wines of my cellar and I decide to drink only water. And I do not smell it !

One of the most popular parlor games on this board (and any other space available for wine criticism) seems to be to discount or reject the experiences and perceptions of others as mistaken or even fraudulent when they are at odds with our own. It is an unfortunate aspect of wine appreciation.

I am in complete agreement with you, Francois, on the merits. I have been drinking wine for more than 40 years. I am convinced that wines change, and in most cases (at least with the wines I drink), they improve when they are given an opportunity to breathe. This, to me, seems to be so self-evident that it makes the contrary argument almost incomprehensible. I know I am right because my experiences – thousands of them over decades – tell me so.

But the incontrovertible nature of this truism is incontrovertible only for those who use my mouth, my tongue, and my brain to taste the wine. I have no basis to deny that others, with their own tasting apparatus, have had other experiences. It would be chutzpah in the extreme for me to tell person X, who claims to have had a different experience, that he is “wrong.”

You and I might well agree that a fire engine is painted red, but we do not know, and I suspect we will never know, if the color that registers in my mind when I think of “red” is the same color you conjure with the same stimulus.

To those who say that the “deluded” should keep quite and not pollute the board with their experiences because some unspecified damage might be done, I would only ask for a list of the deluded and some verifiable, scientific proof that the list is accurate.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
One of the double blind “pre” wines at a formal tasting a couple nights ago reminded me of a Kistler Chard at a very informal Squires Board OL a decade ago. While tasting the whites, one end of the table had about 5 super-alpha personalities booming praise over all these prestige label CA Chards, with others nodding in agreement. To me, one of the 4 Kistlers (young, btw) had some weird piercing quality that was physically painful. (Have never had that on a mature one.) There was the appearance of unanimity among the others on the wines, with all 8 prestige Chards getting praise and nothing said about the rest. I broke the ice and it turned out I wasn’t alone. People either loved or couldn’t take that Kistler. When we voted on favorite white, it got 7 votes, with the other 5 going to Russell’s Sauvignon Blanc.

I’ve seen plenty of alpha types in wine over the years. Many of them seem to be so sure of themselves, almost bullying, where they don’t listen and delude themselves that everyone agrees with them. They spew out falsehoods with their retarded wine knowledge - like any off wine must be the one flaw they know, which must be the only flaw that exists - despite their decades of “experience”. Of course, there are plenty of alphas who actively learn and observe, too.

The tasting the other night was a vertical of cool climate Syrah from a well regarded producer. Of course, those of us who went had high expectations. To me, they had a lot of good site characteristics, but were utterly lacking in vibrancy from acid and aromatics. A couple had heat. They evolved over a couple hours, with most improving and a couple getting worse. With 13 people tasting 9 wines, seven got first place votes, six got lasts. I found 5 enjoyable enough. 3 were muddled, unfocused. I was ambivalent on what I ultimately voted last. It had so much going on. Interesting, weird, a couple off-putting things trumping some highs. It got 6 lasts and a first. Around the time of the the reveal, the bad stuff had mostly faded, for me. No one else agreed. A while later it had resolved to the point I’d have ranked it third. That wine’s lone evangelist had proclaimed (with humor): “You guys are crazy! This is what I want in a Syrah! This could be a Hermitage.” Don’t quite agree, but know what he means. The showing is probably why the winery choose to pick late. I hold some hope better choices have been made in recent vintages under the new winemaker. I’m sure I’ll get to try at some point.

So, there’s a bunch of rambling, touching on a few issues in this thread. Keep your eyes open, wine is a magical thing. There always seem to be exceptions to any supposed rule. Wonderful surprises abound.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:
Jonathan Loesberg:

The accounts of blind tests here, and none of them are that careful as they are described, is that while some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting, the fact that preferences occur show that differences occur from decanting. This would show that some people or most people prefer some wines or most wines without decanting–hardly an astounding conclusion–but pointedly not that decanting makes no difference.

Obviously. Is anyone arguing that decanting makes no difference? The issue is not whether decanting makes a difference. Blind and anecdotal tests all indicate that it makes some difference. The issue is:

  1. The blind tests suggest that decanting has an adverse effect on taste and so
  2. Decanting should be avoided for most wines but
  3. Non-blind anecdotal testimony claims decanting is more often beneficial which
  4. Leads to directly contradictory positions about whether the “differences” resulting from decanting are positive.

So which do we trust, does this confirm our anecdotal evidence is more flawed than we’re willing to admit, and should we change our habits based on this evidence?

There’s a simple answer. If it’s a blind tasting event, don’t decant.
If it’s a social dinner type non-blind situation, decant.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Just musing about possibility of controlled tests, and things to control for. In no particular order

Red / white
Age… very young, very old, intermediate. (Wine, that is)
Style of wine, acidic, tannic, Oaked, malo…
Intrinsic Taster preference for young / fresh tastes versus older tertiary flavours (some people just don’t like old wine)
Situation… analytical vs social… sample size, 1 oz or 4 oz pour, food, ambient temperature of room, indoor / outdoor et etc)
Stemware… ISO / proper glasses
Type of decanter
Single / double decant
Time breathing

Long as it is, I suspect that’s an incomplete list. I think that the blind tests that have been done relate to particular circumstances and may not really have universal application. It’s all situational.

It is certainly true the breathing of the wine will not improve all wines for all tasters.
And vice versa.
So the “myth” is both true and false. Bit like a quantum effect!
That’s about as far as I can get.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: If the “myth” of letting wine breathe is that airing the wine won’t change the taste, even the experiments cited, limited as they are, prove the opposite. If the “myth” is that the change will make them taste better, that really is a matter of taster’s preference and no experiment will prove the point. The adherents to the position that letting wine breathe is a myth seem divided into these two positions.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

If the “myth” of letting wine breathe is that airing the wine won’t change the taste, even the experiments cited, limited as they are, prove the opposite. If the “myth” is that the change will make them taste better, that really is a matter of taster’s preference and no experiment will prove the point. The adherents to the position that letting wine breathe is a myth seem divided into these two positions.

There can be no myth that letting wines breathe changes the taste. We can scientifically prove that oxidation and evaporation affect the composition of what is imbibed, conclusively establishing some change of taste.

The myth, especially that tied with the OP, is that letting wines breathe, you know, by decanting or whatever, improves the wines. This is a subjective issue, at the very root of this thread, and pits various blind experiments against millions of bottles of anecdotal experience. It’s the whole reason this thread is as long as it is. Are we duping ourselves? How heavily should we weight anecdotal experience if even experts like Broadbent or whomever find in a blind tasting a result contradictory to their hypothesis and vast anecdotal experience? If this isn’t what we’ve been discussing, I’m going to go see if I can tell what the bottom of a pool smells like.

K John Joseph:

The myth, especially that tied with the OP, is that letting wines breathe, you know, by decanting or whatever, improves the wines. This is a subjective issue, at the very root of this thread, and pits various blind experiments against millions of bottles of anecdotal experience. It’s the whole reason this thread is as long as it is. Are we duping ourselves? How heavily should we weight anecdotal experience if even experts like Broadbent or whomever find in a blind tasting a result contradictory to their hypothesis and vast anecdotal experience? If this isn’t what we’ve been discussing, I’m going to go see if I can tell what the bottom of a pool smells like.

The blind experiments cited have very low sample sizes and show a general preference, not an absolute one.

From there, people with different palates are relating their different experiences with different wines of differing maturity.

It’s pretty silly to claim no wines need air to open up. One of my experiments took 10 days (in a recorked bottle sampled every few days) to open up. Super stable with minimal oxidation at 6 months open, though some aromatics were gone after a couple months. A friend’s just bottled '17 PN from his mountain vineyard took 6 days to open up (in recorked bottle, sampling daily). I’ve had plenty of beastly wines that took hours in a decanter to open up. None of that is going to get me to decant out of habit.

Exactly. The above statement from John is a vast overstatement of the various data.

I’ve certainly done the home-scaled experiment, with better controls than any of the stuff I’ve seen linked to here, I expect – with the possible exception of Tom Hill’s experiment. But then, all that Tom says in his summary is that for most of the wines, there was insufficient evidence to conclude that the decanted wine was better. He doesn’t say how many wines had folks preferring the decanted wine, but not enough to make the 99% confidence interval, and how many wines were split 50/50, etc.

But nobody’s home-scaled experiments count as anything other than anything other than anecdotes, in the big picture. Just wayyyy too many uncontrolled variables, as Richard points out in his post above.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
A bit of a thread drift, but suppose I flip a coin 8 times. What are the odds that at least 6 of the cases came up either heads or tails? It turns out to be just shy of 29%, and that’s for and experiment where the outcomes are essentially random, with no real underlying cause, and with no systematic bias inherent in the “experiment”. It’s not just important to design a careful experiment, but also to quantify the uncertainties in the experiment. The statistical uncertainties are the easy ones.

-Al

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Lee,

Let’s look at it this way. What is the best way to serve wine?? Do we know?? Have we tested our hypotheses?? Can we learn more?? I would add that all these experiments, totally unapproved by you, Dr Science, indicate that breathing (for wine, not humans) is contraindicated most of the time. Why don’t you do an experiment?? I did one that took two days.

I’ve done that experiment, and the results are very far from “breathing is contraindicated most of the time.” Well, I guess that depends a great deal on what bottles you open “most of the time.” And your palate. And on and on. But that’s why I’m quite skeptical of the OP and the article it links to.

But then, the sample sizes in my experiments in (both x, y, and z) are very small. Far, far below the sizes needed for a 99% confidence interval. In fact, the sample size of tasters was exactly two: me and my partner.

Let’s start at the beginning. My motivation to even try this was because I noticed that some wines improved with air, others degraded, and others seemed to stay the same. My goal was to train myself to identify which wines fit into which of these three categories, and to identify the duration of the ideal decant. Seemed like the best way to maximized my enjoyment out of a given bottle. I’m not sure I really succeeded, but I’m pretty sure I have a better grasp than I did before.

So I ran several experiments as follows. Open a few wines from the same region (usually 4 or 5, usually on a Saturday afternoon), and put them all under Vintage Keeper (questionable on the name – the nitro system with little tanks). Pour out 1/3 of each bottle. Wait 3-4 hours. Pour out another 1/3 of the bottle. Wait an hour. So now you’ve got 3 samples each (of 4-5 wines), one is fresh, one is an hour old, one is 4-5 hours old. Pour small samples (AABC, ABBC, vary as needed) of each of the 3 wines into 4 glasses. Twice (that is, into 2 sets of 4 pours). Blind, of course (requires willing partner). Now taste through your flights, and make sure you can pick out the wine that was poured twice in the flight. In both flights of 4, and match between flights. (If you can’t pass this test, just eliminate the wine from your data – if you can’t reliably identify the pours, you sure as hell don’t have a valid opinion on which is better. That probably eliminated more than half the flights). Now rank the three pours.

I ran this experiment probably a total of 12-14 times, usually with wines from a single region. Sometimes the same vintage, sometimes not. Sometimes varied regions instead of a single region. Regions I know for sure that I tried: Chablis, Muscadet, Barbera d’Alba, Chinon, Vouvray, Cote de Beaune, Oregon Pinot Noir. I did Chablis 3 times, I’m pretty sure, with the wines from 95, 96, and 97. Other than the minimal sample size, probably the biggest faults in this experiment lie in the biased choice of wines to taste, and the choice of only young wines.

Having done this, I’m pretty sure that, for my tastes and those of my partner, breathing improves a substantial number of the young wines I like to drink. In fact, the early results led me to expand the range of the maximum decant from 4-5 hours to overnight.

I didn’t think to mention this earlier because the ridiculously small sample sizes indicate that the utility of the experiment shouldn’t be generalized beyond its original purpose. It’s not valid as a general commentary on how many folks like decanted wine and how many don’t. It’s not even enough to establish 99% statistical confidence on what we like. But it is better than nothing.

EDIT: For those keeping score at home, you’ll see that each iteration of this experiment involved 4-5 bottles, each of which was divided into like 15 pours. Once I came to the conclusion that I really need to be opening magnums to run this properly for 2 tasters, I threw in the towel. My strongest conclusion was that vintage was king. One of my iterations was with 4 consecutive vintages of Pepiere Clos des Briords. IIRC, 2 needed lots of air to show best, 1 never seemed to change, and 1 just degraded over time.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Wes Barton:

It’s pretty silly to claim no wines need air to open up. One of my experiments took 10 days (in a recorked bottle sampled every few days) to open up. Super stable with minimal oxidation at 6 months open, though some aromatics were gone after a couple months. A friend’s just bottled '17 PN from his mountain vineyard took 6 days to open up (in recorked bottle, sampling daily). I’ve had plenty of beastly wines that took hours in a decanter to open up. None of that is going to get me to decant out of habit.

Wes, if you believe a wine improved after 10 days, or had minimal degradation over 6 months, I don’t think we’ll ever see eye to eye on this topic. I’ve tasted thousands of wines in the past 13 years, worked wine bars with both argon systems and bottles re-corked on the counter, and have had my own bottles at home both recorked on the counter and recorked in the fridge. I have never, ever, not even once, had a bottle improve on day 3. Mind you, I’m throwing out here that I have my own biases and preconceived notions, and that undoubtedly affected my perception of the wines. But six months? Good lord man the wine is half way to vinegar then unless it was so profoundly ODed with SO2 that the winemaker shit the bed during the wine making process.

I’ve never said oxygen can’t help a glass of wine, or that everyone’s perceptions about decanting are entirely inaccurate. Shit I swirl the hell out of my wine and I’ve decanted young bordeaux and rhones. Even a barolo after a warning about drinking them young. So I’m not just pissing away everyone’s experiences. Just skeptical of them. But not more skeptical of any experience mentioned than the ones you’ve highlighted above.

Good God, man. 6 months? A bottle of port doesn’t even do well open 6 months.

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
I have recently drunk 1996 Vega Sicilia Unico. This is the triumph of the youth, so natural, juicy, a pure delight.

Stored at 15° C, on day 3 (day 1 being the day of opening and drinking) the wine had lost its energy. Not bad, very possible, but the brilliant charm had gone.
Like some others, I find that red wines often become undrinkable on day 2, and even when they do not decline to that degree, as Francois puts it, the charm is almost always gone.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Good point, Jayson. A lot of serious Mosel rieslings benefit from being opened well ahead. And they often last for a very long time in the fridge.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Young and middle-aged Sauternes will often improve over 3-5 days in the fridge as well.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:

Wes, if you believe a wine improved after 10 days, or had minimal degradation over 6 months, I don’t think we’ll ever see eye to eye on this topic. I’ve tasted thousands of wines in the past 13 years, worked wine bars with both argon systems and bottles re-corked on the counter, and have had my own bottles at home both recorked on the counter and recorked in the fridge. I have never, ever, not even once, had a bottle improve on day 3. Mind you, I’m throwing out here that I have my own biases and preconceived notions, and that undoubtedly affected my perception of the wines. But six months? Good lord man the wine is half way to vinegar then unless it was so profoundly ODed with SO2 that the winemaker shit the bed during the wine making process.

I’ve never said oxygen can’t help a glass of wine, or that everyone’s perceptions about decanting are entirely inaccurate. Shit I swirl the hell out of my wine and I’ve decanted young bordeaux and rhones. Even a barolo after a warning about drinking them young. So I’m not just pissing away everyone’s experiences. Just skeptical of them. But not more skeptical of any experience mentioned than the ones you’ve highlighted above.

Good God, man. 6 months? A bottle of port doesn’t even do well open 6 months.

Vintage Port most likely won’t, but a good Tawny might. I’ve also had some spectacular Madeiras that have been performing well over several years after having been opened.

My friend visited Markus Molitor in the recent past. They served some sweeter Rieslings there from bottles that had been opened before. All the wines performed well, some even spectacularly. The person pouring the wines asked my friend how long he thought the wines had been open. He guessed that probably within the week, one perhaps two weeks because it had started to show subtle signs of oxidation, yet still noting close to being tired.

Bar a few wines that had been opened just for the tasting, no wine was opened less than two weeks ago. Some of the wines had been open more than a month and the one that had showed the first signs of oxidation had been open for more than 4 months.

It’s hard to say whether the wines were any better than immediately after opening, but at least they sounded perfectly sound and delicious to me despite their extensive air contact.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: My experience is that decanting is not always beneficial. Sometimes the wine tastes better with air and sometimes its getting worse. Even with 35 years of drinking experience I have´t found a proper formula when decanting is a good idea. That is true for red and white and for young and old.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

My experience is that decanting is not always beneficial. Sometimes the wine tastes better with air and sometimes its getting worse. Even with 35 years of drinking experience I have´t found a proper formula when decanting is a good idea. That is true for red and white and for young and old.

This has been my experience over 35 years as well: it’s difficult to predict whether decanting will have a positive or negative effect. And it seems to be confirmed by the blind experiments here where most but not all bottles were preferred without extended air time. There is no one size fits all answer.

However, if I’ve had a wine within a year or so I can usually expect another bottle of the same wine/same vintage to behave the same as the prior bottle. This is why notes here or on CT that mention whether the wine was decanted and how it performed over time are so helpful. Even that is not perfect, especially with older bottles where cork variability and storage conditions come into play.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jayson Cohen:

I can tell you never drank 1998 J-J Prum Wehlener Sonnenuhr Spatlese and Auslese on release. They started to open after a couple of weeks. Prime young drinking by my recollection was during the third week.

Wow, you got me with one specific wine and your experience with it. Even had I opened one on release, there is no way I’d be drinking it during a third week. Even had I, I would remain concerned about oxidation, as even wines with high acidity and sugar (like y’quem) can deteriorate with extended oxidation. John Morris and I have had a PM discussion about some of this stuff and the science behind varying evaporation rates and rates of deterioration. I readily acknowledge that not all wines handle oxidation in the same way.

But, regardless of how you’d like to couch some extraordinarily specific wine, oxygen is a corrosive substance and it is deteriorating your wine. Putting something in the fridge, especially in a fridge with a temp near freezing, dramatically slows down the rate of molecular interaction and thus decay. Hence why some folks believe whites can last a week in the fridge (which I dispute anyway, as I see considerable degradation in quality by day 3 even in refrigerated whites, and even in whites with residual sugar and high acid like champagne…and not just from CO2 depletion) or, in your tremendously specific example, for three weeks.

Sugar is a very effective anti-oxidant. Certainly sweet wines will keep for very long periods in a refrigerator. The sweeter they are, the longer they’ll last. Indeed, I found a few ounces left in a bottle of 2001 Strub Spatlese in my fridge last night that had been open for weeks and it was really wonderful – better than when it was first opened.

I rarely drink port, but I’d guess it would last a long time if refrigerated. At room temperature, you lose a lot of aromas pretty quickly. That’s separate from any oxidation.

(I first learned about this property of sugar when I was working in a camera store. A knowledgeable colleague said you could add sugar to your paper developer so you could leave it out in a tray overnight. It prevents the developer from going dark brown or black. Of course, you need to rinse your prints well so they aren’t sticky. )

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:
Jayson Cohen:

I can tell you never drank 1998 J-J Prum Wehlener Sonnenuhr Spatlese and Auslese on release. They started to open after a couple of weeks. Prime young drinking by my recollection was during the third week.

Wow, you got me with one specific wine and your experience with it. Even had I opened one on release, there is no way I’d be drinking it during a third week. Even had I, I would remain concerned about oxidation, as even wines with high acidity and sugar (like y’quem) can deteriorate with extended oxidation. John Morris and I have had a PM discussion about some of this stuff and the science behind varying evaporation rates and rates of deterioration. I readily acknowledge that not all wines handle oxidation in the same way.

But, regardless of how you’d like to couch some extraordinarily specific wine, oxygen is a corrosive substance and it is deteriorating your wine. Putting something in the fridge, especially in a fridge with a temp near freezing, dramatically slows down the rate of molecular interaction and thus decay. Hence why some folks believe whites can last a week in the fridge (which I dispute anyway, as I see considerable degradation in quality by day 3 even in refrigerated whites, and even in whites with residual sugar and high acid like champagne…and not just from CO2 depletion) or, in your tremendously specific example, for three weeks.

It’s not unique to that wine. Very sweet wines with good acid really don’t oxidize in the fridge. Not even after weeks. Certainly not if they’re Spatlese level or better, and certainly not Sauternes, where you have higher alcohol as well. In theory, they might lose some aromas, but in practice I just don’t find that’s the case, even after weeks. More often than not, I find find they show more complexity.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Jayson Cohen:

Sulphur. Sugar. Acid.

Dang, that sounds bad for the dry red and white wines that I drink every week that rarely come from cold, Alpine regions and are never fortified. No wonder none of those survive 3 weeks, let alone three days.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

It’s not unique to that wine. Very sweet wines with good acid really don’t oxidize in the fridge. Not even after weeks. Certainly not if they’re Spatlese level or better, and certainly not Sauternes, where you have higher alcohol as well. In theory, they might lose some aromas, but in practice I just don’t find that’s the case, even after weeks. More often than not, I find find they show more complexity.

John I do not share your experience with Sauternes improving after weeks in the fridge. I’ll admit that I do not drink Sauternes with consistency and only tend to spring for some if I’m hosting a tasting and use that as a wrap up. I do not recall ever thinking the last 6th in a bottle being better after a week, let alone a few weeks or a month. It is clear to me, though, that Sauternes can oxidize, turn brown, and lie dead in a bottle from extended exposure to air. Certainly the difference between weeks and years is a huge one and as we’ve discussed the fridge slows pace of play and the composition of wines affects rates of oxidation, evaporation, etc.

While you say “it’s not unique to that wine” what you and others have done is identify a tiny little piece of winedom in support of this position that wines can improve with weeks (which I still contest … the improvement). Rieslings with high to very high residual sugar and acid; Sauternes; Tawny Port; Madeira; Sherry.

So, huge sugar contents and acid; huge sugar content and fortification; huge sugar content, fortification, and a wine style made in an ultra oxidative manner to preclude spoilage on maritime voyages.

If anything, this is proving my point that normal dry wines cannot take the oxidation some of you guys suggest is “beneficial”. Even you have said that a DAY out on the counter, unrefrigerated, is typically enough to send a wine to hell.

What you guys are doing now is finding minute exceptions to the rule that make up, even on this board, some infinitesimally small percentage of bottles consumed.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Recent red wines off the top of my head that were more integrated and just as good or better on days 3 and/or 4 - but no claim this is a universal trait, just that it depends - and also most bottles don’t last long enough to find out what’s going on with 2+ days open:

2014 Levet Côte Rotie Chavroche (lactic, funky, and not very pleasant on Day 1; much better by Day 3)

2016 Durand St. Joseph Coteaux (seemingly thin and muddled on Day 1; tightens up and improves on Day 3 with cleaner fruit and better delineation - I missed Day 2; still can’t hold a candle to the Gilles I drank right after the Durand after nearly two weeks open)

2015 Gilles Cornas (excellent on Day 1; just as good Days 3 and 4 but more integrated; still excellent on a plateau after nearly 2 weeks open for last glass)

2013 Juge Cornas (fantastic on Day 2; though fantastic on Day 1 too; it didn’t last to Day 3 so I guess I’m cheating with this one)

2013 Cogno Barolo Cascina Nuova (strange awkwardness on Day 1 turns into classic by Day 3 and last glass at 2 weeks out very fine still; best Days 3 and 4 IMO)

Now some whites:

2014 Francois Cotat Culs de Beaujeu (needed a couple days to sort itself; drank well on a plateau for about a week afterward, then slow decline)

2014 Rollin Pernand Cloux (somewhat softish / lactic on opening; better / firms up with a couple hours of air; much better days 2 and 3)

2014 Roally Vire-Clesse (fantastic day one; still fantastic the first few days; then amazing when drank last glass after 8 days open)

The point isn’t to pick a fight. I have my anecdotes going back twenty years. Other people have theirs. I think I’m right that many young wines in particular (dry and with RS) improve with significant time in the open bottle, but I’m not claiming most or all - enough to encourage me to test that possibility on a regular basis. Others may disagree based on their own experience. That divide seems to about par-for-the-course for this interminable thread.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I don’t push my experiments or drinking experiences as far as John and Jayson - speaking about days open on a bottle - but generally side with both of them on their views that many wines, in particular young (quality) wines, do indeed taste much better and more open on days 2 and 3, rather than going the opposite direction. Jayson cites a Levet as just one recent example, and I would highlight that one too, which almost always seem to be better on day two! Now mind you, I always put any remaining open bottle into the regular fridge with a cork shoved far back down, to re-open on the next day.

While I have limited experience in this respect, as this is not the type of wine that I drink, but I do not think low acid wines would fare well here.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
K John Joseph:
John Morris:

It’s not unique to that wine. Very sweet wines with good acid really don’t oxidize in the fridge. Not even after weeks. Certainly not if they’re Spatlese level or better, and certainly not Sauternes, where you have higher alcohol as well. In theory, they might lose some aromas, but in practice I just don’t find that’s the case, even after weeks. More often than not, I find find they show more complexity.

John I do not share your experience with Sauternes improving after weeks in the fridge. I’ll admit that I do not drink Sauternes with consistency and only tend to spring for some if I’m hosting a tasting and use that as a wrap up. I do not recall ever thinking the last 6th in a bottle being better after a week, let alone a few weeks or a month. It is clear to me, though, that Sauternes can oxidize, turn brown, and lie dead in a bottle from extended exposure to air. Certainly the difference between weeks and years is a huge one and as we’ve discussed the fridge slows pace of play and the composition of wines affects rates of oxidation, evaporation, etc.

  1. I have quite a bit of experience with leftover German rieslings, plus some Sauternes and similar wines like Coteaux du Layon. Very often in my experience, they improve with a day or two, and sometimes more, as with my 01 Strub Spatlese if refrigerated. The very sweet ones (e.g., Auslese, BA, TBA) can old up, though not necessarily improve, with much longer periods in the refrigerator.

  2. I’d be shocked if a Sauternes turned dark in the refrigerator, even after a couple of months. I just don’t think you’ll see that happen.

  3. Refrigeration is essential. That not only slows down oxidation; it also retards the evaporation of volatile aroma compounds. Discussions here tend to assume that oxidation is the only thing at work once an wine is open. But a big part of the deterioration in wines left open is the loss of aromas.

K John Joseph:

While you say “it’s not unique to that wine” what you and others have done is identify a tiny little piece of winedom in support of this position that wines can improve with weeks (which I still contest … the improvement). Rieslings with high to very high residual sugar and acid; Sauternes; Tawny Port; Madeira; Sherry.

So, huge sugar contents and acid; huge sugar content and fortification; huge sugar content, fortification, and a wine style made in an ultra oxidative manner to preclude spoilage on maritime voyages.

The discussion here was never limited to sweet whites. It just went off in that direction because of posts by David G, Jayson, Jay and Mel about German and other sweet wines, and my observation about the antioxidant effect of sugar. But, in any event, I would not consider the universe of sweet rieslings, Sauternes, sweet Loires, passitos, etc., to be “a tiny little piece of winedom.” (I’ll leave out wines like port. madeira and sherry that are intentionally oxidized.)

FYI, I think that most of the improvement with the sweet wines occurs in the first couple of days, but they remain much more stable after that than dry wines, which do degrade, even when refrigerated, after a couple of days.

K John Joseph:

If anything, this is proving my point that normal dry wines cannot take the oxidation some of you guys suggest is “beneficial”. Even you have said that a DAY out on the counter, unrefrigerated, is typically enough to send a wine to hell.

What you guys are doing now is finding minute exceptions to the rule that make up, even on this board, some infinitesimally small percentage of bottles consumed.

  1. I have been clear in all my posts that I’m talking about refrigerated leftovers, and the improvement, when it does occur, rarely goes beyond the second day. And, remember, this all began with Stan contending that all improvement stops after five minutes in the glass.

  2. You are now ignoring all the discussion of reds. I don’t consider young Bordeaux, Burgundy, Northern Rhone, Barolo and California cabs to be “minute exceptions.” But in many cases, I find that they open up with a day if refrigerated.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Jayson
Were these wines poured blind for you?

I’m not Jayson (though I know Jayson).

I don’t retaste blind, but I regularly retaste leftovers from tastings I host on day 2 and 3. Sometimes I’m curious about highly touted wines that didn’t show well on day 1, tasting blind. Sometimes I’m curious to see how my favorites held up.

Sometimes my hunch that a wine was just closed on day 1 is borne out and it has blossomed; sometimes not. Sometimes my day 1 favorite has cracked up by day 2 or 3.

My notes on a tasting of 2014 Calif cabs in April are a great example of this. Some improved substantially over three nights; some didn’t.

The fact that my day 2 and day 3 experiences do not correlate well with my hopes/expectations for particular wines strongly counters the notion that this is all confirmation bias.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I guess there are different aspects of this discussion. The first would be the question of what happens to wine after a bottle is opened. That’s a question for chemists.The second would be, What happens to us after the bottle is opened? That’s a question for doctors, physiological psychologists, etc.

My question is, What do we really like?? This is perhaps a question for social psychologists. Probably somebody at Proctor and Gamble could work on this.

Everyone who has ever presented the same wine treated in two or three different ways vis a vis aeration in a blind tasting format has discovered that for the most part people prefer the most recently opened bottle.

Now, those of you who leave bottles open in various parts of your house have different results.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg

posts.name: John Morris

posts.cooked: Older wines can be fragile. Years ago right after I started working in wine there was an extensive vertical tasting of Robert Mondavi wines being held at Stars in San Francisco. They were magnums and three liters that had been opened and poured out. The thing that made those of us who were invited lose interest in this great lineup was Iraq had started firing Scuds into Israel and CNN was on in the back room. We were all drinking stronger stuff thinking the world would end any moment. By the time we got to the Mondavi wines they had all been sitting out for too long. Younger wines I have kept open for a week or more with no ill effects.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
“Everyone who has ever presented the same wine treated in two or three different ways vis a vis aeration in a blind tasting format has discovered that for the most part people prefer the most recently opened bottle.”

That’s hardly a fair summary. You’re basing that on a handful of reported “experiments”. I’m certain there are countless examples that have not been reported. I would argue also that these “experiments” are completely irrelevant for me, not knowing: the setting (e.g. was wine served with food, at what temperatures), the size of pours (e.g. 60 ml thimbles a la wine “competitions”), how long tasters spent on each wine, who the tasters were and what their preferences are, what the specific wines were.

I’m not sure anyone on this thread has argued you’re “wrong” to enjoy P&P, or that others who do so are. Nor has anyone argued that decanting / aeration are ALWAYS the best for ALL wines. However, there’s a general tone of “everyone else is wrong” from the P&P camp that grates for some people. Do you like blue cheese by the way?

posts.cooked:

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
Perhaps one of the scientists on this site could design an experiment.

A wise man who lived a long time ago wrote "It is the mark of an educated mind to rest satisfied with the degree of precision which the nature of the subject admits and not to seek exactness where only an approximation is possible.”

Well, he wrote it in ancient Greek, so that’s not an exact quote. But, basically, there are questions where it’s important to apply scientific rigor because they deal with fundamental aspects of nature. Whether 60% of participants in a particular study that involved particular wines preferred newly opened bottles over decanted bottles would not be such a question, nor would it say anything about what you or I would prefer as a general rule, and I suspect there is no general rule to be discovered even for my own individual palate that would be true for all wines.

So, I don’t think this is an area where rigorous experiments will really be much help. I know that I’m not seeking guidance about what to do with young or old bottles, suspect most others are the same.

-Al

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Imagine for a moment, Mel, an experiment with a bunch of people to try some cheeses blind. Lets pick 8-year olds as appropriate blind tasters with unpolluted palates. There’s also a scientist, who can clearly establish the similarity between the blue cheese flavour and olfactory stimulating compounds, and rotting meat, urine, rotten vegetables and rotten milk. The blind tasters have a go, and SIX out of eight hate blue cheese. They really like the cheddar. “This reminds me of the time I ate my own poo as a baby!” they wail. A few of them are physically sick. I’m pretty confident I can conduct the above experiment to those expected results, and I’m also pretty confident you would not accept that as “scientific evidence” that you’re just deluding yourself into liking blue cheese.
The point isn’t “where are the other experiments”, but rather that what you and others have submitted as “scientific” is no such thing. It holds no more water than the massive experience you dismiss as tainted and anecdotal, from those people who prefer to aerate some of their wines, and report great results doing so.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Rauno E (NZ):

Imagine for a moment, Mel, an experiment with a bunch of people to try some cheeses blind. Lets pick 8-year olds as appropriate blind tasters with unpolluted palates. There’s also a scientist, who can clearly establish the similarity between the blue cheese flavour and olfactory stimulating compounds, and rotting meat, urine, rotten vegetables and rotten milk. The blind tasters have a go, and SIX out of eight hate blue cheese. They really like the cheddar. “This reminds me of the time I ate my own poo as a baby!” they wail. A few of them are physically sick. I’m pretty confident I can conduct the above experiment to those expected results, and I’m also pretty confident you would not accept that as “scientific evidence” that you’re just deluding yourself into liking blue cheese.
The point isn’t “where are the other experiments”, but rather that what you and others have submitted as “scientific” is no such thing. It holds no more water than the massive experience you dismiss as tainted and anecdotal, from those people who prefer to aerate some of their wines, and report great results doing so.

This is me as a young adult. And it’s an apt analogy. When I was 22, I travelled extensively in France and a few months later tried to go to Neal’s Yard in London. I could not even go into a cheese shop. The smell immediately made me nauseous. As I grew older, I gradually developed a taste for cheese of any sort. I love going into cheese shops. Likewise, people on all wine forums for years have talked about palate development and changes in preferences that evolve over time and with experience. It is to deny your humanity to claim otherwise.

You can’t do clinical-type science without a control. You can’t do meaningful statistics here without a stationary or at least well-defined and definable ensemble of test subjects and measures. And to be honest who on this board cares about how average person X who is not interested in wine reacts to Bottle Y fresh or with air? We all develop and care about our own preferences and experiences, and those experiences are one wine at a time, each wine and even bottle different than another. The only way to know if you will prefer a wine with air or not is to try it. I put my proverbial money where my mouth is, and if possible, see if I like a wine better with 1, 2, 3 days and sometimes week(s) of air. It’s fun. I’m curious. And I’m not interested in predictability, nor do I believe I get any particular additional enjoyment or am biased toward wanting wine to improve with air. There is certainly curiousity and enjoyment out of evolution with air. Seeing how the wine changes. But broadly speaking you have 4 qualitative outcomes as a function of time - improvement and change, change where the wine is enjoyable but no sense of qualitative improvement, shut down, degradation. All of those can and do happen in various combinations.

What is the point of pseudo-science and flawed statistical and polling analysis to understand it further?

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Rauno,
Let’s suppose you conduct a similar experiment with people who eat blue cheese on a regular basis.
That is more akin to what I did. They may not be dairy science majors or people with genius palates, but they were people who consumed blue cheese on a regular basis.

Let’s say that accepted wisdom for blue cheese was that it best served on a certain kind of cracker.
But the experiment showed that people liked it on another cracker. I take it you would not try that kind of cracker because unreported experiments showed our experiment was wrong. Indeed you and your friends always have used that kind of cracker and know that kind of cracker is best for blue cheese.

Of course, many of these unreported experiments may have been done with eight year olds with no appreciation of blue cheese. I don’t know for sure, since these experiments were never reported.

Now, if the point of letting wine breathe is to make it taste better, then shouldn’t people like wine that has been opened longer??

Allan Shoup, who ran Ste Michelle for many years, originally did consumer tests for a cosmetics company. They would tell women that they were experimenting with cold cream. Which sample did women like best?? Really they were experimenting with packaging. The product was always the same. Now, how scientific was this?? There is no ‘best’ packaging, but they just wanted to know which packaging regular consumers of blue cheese liked best. That’s all.

Jayson, what kind of control are you looking for??

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
The analogy with blue cheese would be to serve the same cheese refrigerated and at room temperature (which is how one is “supposed” to serve cheese). I fully expect that if you tested widely enough, you would get some variety of preferences (as you would, probably, if you served the same white wine chilled and at room temperature). I doubt anyone would think you had proved anything in either case except that preferences differ.

Marketers aren’t trying to prove things but to sell things. If one form of packaging sells 10/% more product, that is a very significant variable. For the claim that wine should not be decanted, it isn’t.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Jonathan,

If you did your experiments it would be easier to tell which was chilled and what was room temperature. When you do the one we did, and you do it blind, you don’t know what is what until the brown paper bag comes off the bottle.


My point about cheese and crackers is that it seems that even after an experiment showed preference for a new kind of cracker, all the supporters of the old cracker would shout ‘this is nonsense…i’ ve been using crackers for 30 years and I know crackers and I know cheese
and don’t tell me this new cracker is any good…’

Somehow this reminds me of a contest the old Pacific Wine Company. It was a fill in the blank contest. I know wine because_______________.

The winners were

I know wine because I’m French and
I know wine because I’m a lawyer.
I know wine because I’m Elvis.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Al Osterheld:

A bit of a thread drift, but suppose I flip a coin 8 times. What are the odds that at least 6 of the cases came up either heads or tails? It turns out to be just shy of 29%, and that’s for and experiment where the outcomes are essentially random, with no real underlying cause, and with no systematic bias inherent in the “experiment”. It’s not just important to design a careful experiment, but also to quantify the uncertainties in the experiment. The statistical uncertainties are the easy ones.

-Al

Actually not thread drift at all.

One other thing … I’m not sure most of the blind tests control for the tasters’ palates. Do they like mature wine? I have a suspicion that in general not all wine lovers do. There is a very large number of variables to control for, which in turn means that a large number of separate experiments is required.

[edited for typo]

That’d be well over a hundred variables. To show what? That it’s a futile exercise that can only show what we already know? The OP already showed that not all preferences line up on how much air is optimal for a given wine, and that it can be different for different wines. We know there’s no one-size-fits-all approach for opening wine. We should even know two bottles of the same wine, young or old, together in the same case since bottling can show quite different to the same person, poured side-by-side. One may need air, while the other doesn’t. You can go in armed with all the information about a given wine, and still may have to play it by ear if thrown a curve.

posts.name: Dusty Gillson
posts.cooked:
If I can chime in really really late on this discussion: when it comes to ‘is it us or the wine?’

I only know that my day and I have a hell of a lot more variables in them than my wine’s day does. I don’t think nearly enough people blame themselves when a wine tastes off. What did I just eat? Is it February and the RH inside has been 15% for a month, drying out my whole system? Is this the 1st wine I’ve tried today (shock to the system) or the 10th (palate is dulled by now)? Maybe I’m in a bad mood because I had an argument at work earlier? Maybe I’m craving a crisp Chablis but alas we are tasting aged Rioja?

Compared to the wine. . . (55-62 degrees F for the last 10 years)

My mentor in the wine world never decanted, regardless of the wine, because he insisted on watching the wine change over time. He was also happy to sip on a 2 oz pour over 4 hours if it deserved that kind of treatment.
